While I could bet for protection or thin value, checking behind allows me to control the pot size and reach showdown with my modest pair. My hand has decent showdown value, but it is unlikely to get called by many worse hands if I bet, and I'd prefer not to be check-raised off my equity.

River: Kc 7h 8d Th As. I hold Qh3s — nothing but Q-high. Both players checked flop and turn, which usually signals a wide, fairly weak checking range from the preflop raiser (missed overcards, small pairs, some Kx/Tx/air). Checking here gives up the pot to any real pair; a small bet is a low-risk bluff that can pick off those missed hands.

Pot = 650, the minimum bet is 100. The break-even fold frequency for a 100 bet is ~13.3% (100 / (650+100)). Given Flash’s passive line (checked flop & turn after opening wide), I expect him to fold at least that often to a small river probe. If called I only lose 100; if he folds I scoop 650. I have no showdown value, so I should attempt a cheap steal rather than check and concede.

I will make a small bluff probe — bet the minimum to maximize fold equity while keeping my risk low. If he calls I concede the pot; if he folds I pick up a decent pot with minimal investment.

On the river, the board is Kc-7h-8d-Th-As. I have a pair of eights. Minni has checked twice and is now leading with a very small bet of 100 into a pot of 750. This small bet size (roughly 13% of the pot) offers me excellent pot odds of approximately 8.5 to 1. I only need to be right about 11% of the time to make this call profitable. While the Ace is a scare card, Minni's line of checking the flop and turn suggests she likely doesn't have a King. She could have a Ten, a Seven, or a small Ace, but she could also be betting a weaker hand or a missed draw given the tiny sizing. My middle pair is strong enough to call such a small bet in this spot.

Holding A7o on the button in 3-handed play is a solid hand. With Minni in the small blind (24,550 chips) and Flash in the big blind (8,400 chips), I should play aggressively from my position. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate - it puts pressure on both blinds, takes the initiative, and is in line with sound button strategy in shorthanded poker. My 6,900 chip stack is healthy enough (69 big blinds) to make this play without significant risk. This hand has decent playability post-flop with ace-high and straight potential, making it a good spot to raise and win the pot immediately or play with an advantage if called.

Holding 3♥2♠ in the small blind against a button open to 300 is a clear fold. This hand is one of the weakest in the deck: poor high-card value, not suited, and has very limited postflop playability. To call 250 into a 450 pot I’d need roughly 35–36% equity just to break even; 3‑2o has far less versus a button’s opening range and is even worse if the big blind defends. A min 3‑bet to 500 would be a light isolation play, but with no blockers, out of position and a competent big blind behind who can reraise or call, that line is a recipe for difficult spots. I’ll conserve my large stack and wait for a better spot — fold.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Five of clubs. Claude raised to 300 from the Button and Minni folded. While the hand is suited and I am getting reasonable odds to call, Ten-Five suited is near the bottom of a defending range against a 3x open from the button. Given that I will be out of position for the rest of the hand and Claude has shown aggressive tendencies in previous hands, I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

On the button with 5♦7♠ I have a marginal hand but maximum leverage: huge effective stack (24,550) and position vs the small and big blinds. This is a classic steal spot — raising puts pressure on the blinds, isolates the pot, and uses my positional advantage postflop. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — big enough to discourage routine defenses but standard at this table. Plan: fold to a substantial 3-bet (I don’t want to play a bloated pot out of position with 57o); if called I’ll proceed cautiously postflop, c-betting favorable textures, checking dry boards and folding to heavy resistance.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit (AKo), which is a premium hand, particularly in a three-handed game. The player on the button (Minni) has opened with a standard raise. Being in the Small Blind, I am out of position for the rest of the hand. To extract value, gain the initiative, and potentially fold out the Big Blind or thin the field, I will 3-bet (re-raise). A sizing of 1,100 (approximately 3.5x the initial raise) is standard to discourage the button from calling too wide while in position.

K6 offsuit is below-average strength and doesn't warrant engaging in a 3-bet pot from out of position. Facing Flash's 3-bet raise, I'm likely trailing premium holdings. Calling 1000 more (14% of stack) is a poor risk/reward given the hand's limited potential and my position disadvantage. Folding preserves chips for stronger opportunities.

Flash (SB) 3-bet to 1,100 after my button open to 300. Pot is ~1,500; calling is 800 more (total committed would be 1,100) which requires ~34.8% equity (800 / (1,500+800)). I have 5♦7♠ — a weak, easily-dominated hand with poor blockers and limited playability vs a 3-bet range. A call puts me in marginal spots postflop despite position; a 4-bet bluff is thin here (no useful blockers and I can face a shove or a call). My stated plan was to fold to a substantial 3-bet, and this sizing is enough to discard 5-7o. I’ll fold and preserve my deep stack for better spots.

I am on the button with 64 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. While the button is the best position to steal from in 3-handed play, 64o is significantly below the threshold of an opening range even against wide blinds. Minni has a massive stack and has shown willingness to call or 3-bet, and Claude has also been active. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my position.

2-8 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker. Despite getting 5:1 pot odds, this hand has minimal equity against any reasonable big blind range. Out of position on later streets, the negative EV is clear. With a short stack of 6,900 chips, I should preserve resources for premium situations. Folding the small blind is the mathematically correct and disciplined decision.

Folding J7o from the button. While position is valuable, J7 offsuit is too weak to justify playing against aggressive opponents with deeper stacks. I need to preserve my 6900 chip stack for premium hands and better opportunities.

6♥8♥ in the small blind is a playable, suited connector for an open in this spot. The button folded so it’s heads-up vs Flash (BB 8,700). I want initiative and fold equity rather than seeing a cheap flop out of position, so I’ll open-raise to the min (200 total). That size keeps a wide range in, extracts value from worse hands, and is small relative to both stacks (I’m 24,250), while allowing me to fold to a large 3-bet or continue postflop if called. Amount 200 is the standard 2x BB open and balances equity realization with pressure.

I hold Ace-Ten of spades in the Big Blind, which is a very strong premium hand in a 3-handed game. Minni, who has a large stack and has been active, min-raised from the Small Blind. I will 3-bet to 600 for value and to take the initiative in the hand, as my hand is well ahead of a typical Small Blind opening range.

I opened from the SB to 200 with 6♥8♥ and Flash (BB) 3-bet to 600. With stacks deep (effective ~8.2k) and the 3‑bet size small, this is almost certainly a wide-ish 3‑bet and I get about 3:1 pot odds to call (400 more). 6♥8♥ has good playability and implied odds versus a wide range; 4‑betting would be too polar with this hand and folding surrenders a lot of equity. I’ll call the extra 400 to see a flop and play cautiously OOP—continue with strong draws or well‑connected flops, fold to heavy action on dry boards.

I missed this flop completely with 6♥8♥ on Q♦ A♣ 4♠: no pair, no backdoor heart on the board, and I’m out of position vs Flash who 3‑bet preflop. His 3‑bet range is weighted toward A‑x and high broadways/pairs, so many of his hands have at least an Ace or good showdown value here. Pot is 1,200 and effective stacks are ~8,200 — too deep to gamble OOP with no equity. I’ll check and keep the pot controlled. Plan: if Flash checks back I see a free turn and can barrel/lead if I pick up equity (a heart or a straight/paired turn) or apply pressure selectively; if he bets, I’ll fold to a standard c‑bet size and only consider a call as a float if the bet is very small (enough to justify calling to see a turn).

I have top pair with an Ace and a Ten kicker. The board is relatively dry (Q-A-4 rainbow). Since I was the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have the range advantage here. Minni checked to me, which is expected. I will place a continuation bet of 600 (half the pot) to get value from weaker Aces (like A9, A8), pocket pairs, or draws like KJ, while also protecting my hand and maintaining the initiative.

I opened preflop with 6♥8♥ and called Flash’s small 3‑bet to see a flop. On Q♦ A♣ 4♠ I have no pair and no backdoor heart on the board — essentially zero raw equity and very poor playability out of position. Flash is the 3‑bettor, so his flop c‑bet is heavily weighted toward A‑x, Qx and pocket pairs; his 600 into the ~1,200 pot (making it ~1,800) is a standard-sized c‑bet. Calling 600 to win ~1,800 only justifies ~25% equity, which 6♥8♥ does not have versus this range, and floating OOP against a 3‑bettor will put me in awkward spots on turns/rivers. Raising would be over‑polarized and reckless with this hand. I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better, more playable situations.

I'm on the button in a 3-handed pot with 5♠3♦ — a weak hand but position and short-handed dynamics favor stealing. Calling 100 is too passive (multiway pot, poor equity) and folding gives up profitable pressure. I'll open to a standard 3x BB (300) to maximize fold equity versus the blinds while keeping the size reasonable. If I face a large 3-bet I will fold; if I'm called I'll use position to c-bet favorable flops and fold to significant resistance on dry boards.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Four offsuit. The button (Minni) has raised to 300. Queen-Four offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position, especially against an active player like Minni who currently holds a large stack advantage. Folding is the most prudent and standard play in this situation.

With 8-10 offsuit from the big blind against a button raiser, I'm facing a difficult spot. While I'm getting decent pot odds (2.25:1 on my 200 chip call), several factors make folding optimal: (1) Hand quality - 8-10o is marginal at best; (2) Position - out of position means acting first on every street and surrendering information; (3) Opponent tendencies - Minni has shown an aggressive button-raising pattern, but that doesn't mean I should chase marginal hands out of position; (4) Stack preservation - I have 6,800 chips and should preserve them for better situations where my hand strength or position is superior. Early in the tournament (hand 68/150), it's better to be selective and wait for premium spots rather than commit chips with weak hands from poor positions.

I am on the Button with 6-7 offsuit. While this is a 3-handed game where opening ranges are wider, 6-7 offsuit is near the bottom of the range and doesn't play well post-flop, especially against Minni, who has a very large stack and tends to call frequently. Claude is the short stack and might look for opportunities to jam. Folding here is a disciplined play to wait for better spots.

Folding 5-2o from the small blind. Despite 3:1 pot odds, this is the worst starting hand in poker with no straighting, no flushing, and weak kicker issues. Post-flop play would be extremely difficult with minimal equity against any range. Preserving chips for stronger opportunities is the profitable long-term strategy.

J9o on the button with favorable position. I have decent pot odds (1.5:1) and will be last to act post-flop. This hand plays well in position even if it's marginal in early position. Both opponents are aggressive, so keeping the pot controlled while maintaining positional advantage is ideal. I'll call and evaluate the flop with my position edge.

I have 5h6h in the small blind — a suited connector with decent flop equity and good implied odds, but I'm out of position vs the button and big blind. The pot is 250 and it only costs me 50 more to see a flop (total commit 100), so a call is a cheap way to realize equity. I don’t want to raise with this hand out of position — raising invites harder decisions and possible 3-bets from the big blind or button. Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit a heart, pair, or strong draw I’ll continue, otherwise fold to serious aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. Both the Button (Claude) and the Small Blind (Minni) have limped into the pot. There is no reason to raise and bloat the pot with a mediocre hand out of position. Checking allows me to see a flop for free.

Flop: A♣ K♥ T♦; my hand 5♥6♥. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart draw (need both turn and river hearts). I have ~6 outs to pair (≈24% to pair by river) plus ≈3.3% for the runner-runner heart flush — total improvement equity roughly 25–28% vs a random hand. Out of position against two opponents on a high-card board that heavily intersects normal calling/raising ranges (lots of A/K/T, broadways, pairs). Multiway bluffing here is low EV and I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot and realize equity. If faced with a small c-bet (~100 into 300) I’ll generally call (break-even ≈20% and my raw chance to improve is higher), but I’ll fold to larger-sized aggression or a raise unless I pick up a pair or a stronger draw on the turn.

I am in the Big Blind in a limped pot with Queen-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack). The board (Ace-King-Ten) is extremely heavy and very likely to have connected with either of my opponents' ranges. Since Minni checked, I will check as well to see a turn card for free and hope to improve my hand.

With J9o on a flop of Ac Kh Td, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs with any Q or 8). Both opponents checked, indicating marginal holdings or a desire to check down. Checking behind is the correct play here because: 1) I'm not in a strong position with just a draw, 2) both players showed weakness, 3) I maintain my positional advantage for future streets, 4) I get a free card to improve my hand, and 5) I control the pot size while holding a medium-strength draw. With ~32% equity in the hand, I should see another card cheaply and reevaluate on the turn based on whether I hit my straight or need to make a decision.

Board is Ac Kh Td 3d, I hold 5h6h — no pair and no real draw. With only the river to come I have 6 outs (three 5s + three 6s) ≈ 13% to improve. Pot is 300 and it costs me nothing to check. Betting OOP into two players who both checked the flop is a thin bluff here: my fold equity is limited and my raw equity is too low to justify calling a standard turn bet. For reference, calling a 100 bet into 300 requires ~20% equity to break even, which I don’t have; only an extremely tiny turn bet (≈50) would be justifiable, but the table minimum/raise is 100. Plan: check to control the pot and take a free river; fold to any meaningful bet on the turn (call only if an impossible-to-find micro-bet ≤50 is offered). If checked to the river I’ll re-evaluate with the extra card.

In a limped pot with a board of Ace-King-Ten, most players will check when they have nothing or are trapping with a big hand. I have Queen-high and a gutshot straight draw. Since Minni checked the turn, and I have no real showdown value, checking allows me to potentially see the river for free. If Claude checks behind, I get a free card to hit my straight. If he bets, I can evaluate based on the sizing.

On the turn with J9 on a board of Ac Kh Td 3d, I'm holding an open-ended straight draw (any Q or 8 completes a straight) with approximately 32% equity. Both opponents checked the flop and again on the turn, indicating marginal holdings or weakness. With just king-high and a drawing hand, checking is optimal here because: (1) I'm not strong enough to bet for value, (2) both players demonstrated weakness, (3) I get a free card to the river where hitting my straight would make a strong hand, (4) I maintain positional advantage if the river brings action, and (5) keeping the pot small (~300 chips) aligns with my hand strength. I'll see the river cheaply and reassess based on whether I hit the straight or need to evaluate river action.

Final board Ac Kh Td 3d 4d, my hand 5h6h: no pair, no flush, no straight — essentially zero showdown value. Everyone checked through, which shows weakness but also makes a multiway bluff very thin: with two opponents my fold equity is low and the minimum bet (100) is unlikely to fold all the hands that beat me (pairs, any Ace/King/ten). Pot is only 300 versus deep stacks, so risking chips here with air has negative EV. I’ll check back and take the free showdown; if anyone now leads out I will fold to value-sized bets since I can’t beat them.

The board is Ac-Kh-Td-3d-4d and I have Qc-7d. I have zero showdown value with Queen-high. The hand was limped pre-flop and has been checked all the way through the river. Minni has checked again. There is very little point in betting as a bluff into two opponents in a small pot where anyone could have a weak pair or even an Ace/King they were playing passively. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown or just fold if Claude bets significantly.

River decision with J9 on Ac Kh Td 3d 4d. I have jack-high, which is air. The entire hand was checked down by all three players (flop, turn, and now river), indicating widespread weakness in the hand. Betting here with no hand strength serves no purpose—I cannot win against reasonable holdings that check-call, and I would only win against hands that fold (which are likely already folding at this point). My best play is to check and take the 300-chip pot down at showdown if everyone has marginal holdings, which the action throughout the hand suggests. Checking maximizes my expected value in this weak situation.

I’m on the button with pocket 5s (5h5d). Pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call — in 3-handed position this is a prime spot to open for value and initiative. A standard 3x open to 300 isolates the blinds, gives me position post-flop, and builds the pot for set-mining with deep stacks. If I face a small 3-bet I’ll consider calling to pursue a set; against a large 3-bet I’ll fold to avoid an overpriced coinflip. Plan: raise to 300 and play postflop aggressively on favorable boards, control the pot on wet textures.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 9c. Minni raised from the button to 300. 8-9 offsuit is a weak hand to play out of position, even against a wide button opening range. Given Minni's aggression and the disadvantage of being first to act post-flop, folding is the most disciplined and standard play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Analyzing this situation:

**Hand strength**: 8c Tc is a decent suited connector with straight potential and broadway components. It's not a premium hand, but it's certainly playable.

**Situation factors**:
- I'm in the BB facing a raise from Minni (button), who has shown consistent aggression throughout the session
- Flash (SB) has folded, making this heads-up
- Pot odds: Need to call 200 into a pot of 450 = 2.25:1, requiring about 30% equity
- My stack: 6550 (approximately 65 big blinds), relatively short
- Minni's stack: 23500 (deep stack)

**Decision rationale**:
With 8cTc, I have a hand that plays well postflop with two overcards, a gutshot straight draw possibility, and suited connector implied odds. Against Minni's wide range of raising hands from the button, this hand has decent equity. The pot odds justify a call, and going heads-up is more favorable than multiway. My stack size (65bb) suggests I should avoid creating huge pots, but calling here keeps options open and allows me to evaluate the flop before committing more chips.

I won't raise here as 8cTc doesn't warrant significant preflop commitment - better to see a flop cheaply and make decisions based on the texture and action.

Checking on Kd 4h 4c with 8cTc. My hand has weak showdown value against Minni's likely range - no pair yet, just broadway cards with potential for a pair on the turn (K gives two pair, T gives top pair). With a short stack (63bb), I should avoid committing more chips with marginal holdings without better information. Checking lets me see the turn while keeping the pot small and gathering information about Minni's hand through her betting action.

I raised preflop and Claude just flat-called from the BB. Flop K♦ 4♥ 4♣ with my 5♥5♦ is an underpair to a king but I'm the preflop aggressor and this board is relatively dry. A half‑pot c-bet (~325 into 650) leverages my initiative: it gets value from worse pairs/underpairs, folds many unpaired overcards, and denies free cards that could improve their range. If Claude check‑raises big I’ll interpret that as trips or a very strong K and fold — preserving a healthy stack vs a likely dominating holding. If called I’ll proceed cautiously on turn, pot‑control or target thin value depending on the card. Betting 325 maximizes fold equity and keeps control of the hand.

Folding 8cTc on Kd 4h 4c. The paired flop drastically reduces my equity, and I have no meaningful draws. Against Minni's raise, I'm likely dominated or drawing thin (~30-35% equity). While 4:1 pot odds technically justify a call mathematically, the implied odds deteriorate significantly if I miss on the turn and face continued aggression from her deep stack. Preserving chips for premium spots is the higher EV play here.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with King-Seven offsuit. This is a standard opening hand from the button to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A standard 3x raise is appropriate here.

I'm in the small blind with 7d-Qh, a weak hand. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and I need to call 250 more. Queen-seven offsuit lacks the equity and playability needed to defend from the small blind against a button raise. Even assuming a wide stealing range from Flash, Q7o doesn't have sufficient showdown value or post-flop potential to justify entering the pot. With a deeper stack, I could consider more aggressive plays, but folding is the mathematically correct choice here. I'll preserve my stack for stronger hands in better positions.

Button (Flash) opened to 300; I’m in the BB with Q♦7♥. Heads‑up versus a 3bb open I should defend relatively wide — this hand has reasonable showdown equity vs a wide opening range and the pot odds are acceptable (I must call 200 into a 450 pot; break‑even ≈31%). A 3‑bet bluff here is suboptimal: the min‑3bet to 500 is too small and easy to exploit, and a larger 3‑bet would commit too much with a mediocre hand. Folding is too tight. Plan: call to see a flop, keep the pot controlled out of position, fold to heavy continuation‑bet pressure when I miss, and play aggressively only if I pair my queen or pick up strong draws.

Flop: 3♣ 9♥ 2♣. I hold Q♦7♥ — missed the flop, only a single backdoor heart and one overcard (Q). Button opened to ~3bb preflop so his range is wide and he’ll c‑bet frequently, but out of position with no pair I don’t want to bloat the pot or bluff‑raise without strong blockers or equity. Best line is pot control and to realize equity: check and see what he does. Plan: call small c‑bets to try to hit my Q (or river a cheap showdown) but fold to large, polarized barrels. If he checks back I can take a free turn card and sometimes barreled as a bluff when the texture favors it. I’ll check now.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board is relatively low (9-high). Since Minni checked to me, a standard continuation bet is appropriate to take down the pot or define the strength of her hand. My King-high has some value, but I'd rather win the pot now than risk a turn card that improves her range or makes it harder to bluff.

Call. Flop: 3♣ 9♥ 2♣ and I have Q♦7♥ — I missed and only have one overcard (Q); there are no meaningful straight or flush draws for me. Flash’s bet of 300 into a 650 pot (pot now 950) gives me ~24% break‑even equity to call (300/(950+300)). I only have ~12–13% to pair Q or 7 by the river, so this is borderline purely by raw outs, but the button opened wide pre and will c‑bet a lot of air here. The bet size is moderate and calling keeps his bluffs in while controlling the pot out of position. Plan: call to see a turn and fold to heavy turn aggression unless I improve (pair the Q/7 or pick up a clear fold equity change). If I hit a pair on the turn I will extract value; if villain checks the turn I’ll often take a free card or bet for value/protection.

Check. I missed — Q7o has no made pair and effectively no realistic draw (only one heart; would need runner‑runner). The K♥ on the turn is a high, scare card that improves a large portion of the button’s opening/c‑betting range (Kx, Ax, K9, KQ, even some two‑pair combos). I’m out of position and have little fold equity, so leading as a bluff is low EV and I shouldn’t bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Plan: keep the pot small, check and fold to any sizable river aggression unless I pair up; if he checks back I get a free river to try to improve or realize showdown value.

I have turned top pair with King-Seven. Minni called my flop continuation bet and has now checked to me on the turn. The King is an excellent card for my range, and while my kicker is weak, top pair is a very strong hand in a heads-up pot. I will bet for value and to protect against potential club flush draws or straight draws (like 45 or A4) that Minni might be holding. A bet of roughly 60% of the pot seems appropriate to build the pot while still being a price she might call with a Nine or a draw.

The turn bet is too strong for my hand. Pot is 1,250 after the flop call and Flash bets 700 on the turn (pot 1,950, I must call 700) — I need ~700/1950 ≈ 36% equity to make a call break‑even. I hold Q♦7♥: no pair, no meaningful draw (only a single heart backdoor), and only six river cards to pair (three Qs + three 7s) ≈ 12.8% to improve. The K♥ on the turn also helps a large portion of the button’s continuing range (Kx, AK, some two‑pairs/sets), and his line (open → c‑bet → larger turn barrel) is heavily weighted toward value. While some bluffs with club combos or air are possible, they are unlikely to be frequent enough to make a +EV call here. Raising as a bluff is unrealistic with such weak blockers. Folding preserves my deep stack and avoids making a long‑term losing call with almost no outs. Action: fold.

3-9 offsuit is a weak hand without good equity or playability. While the button is a favorable position, post-flop I'll be out of position against two active opponents. The pot odds of 2.5:1 don't justify playing a marginal hand with limited equity. Folding weak hands in non-essential positions is correct strategy for stack preservation and waiting for better opportunities.

Heads-up postions: I’m in the small blind with Q♠4♠. Claude folded, so it’s just me vs Flash in the big blind. Pot is 150 and I need to put in 50 more (I’ve already posted 50). Calling 50 more (total committed = 100) gives me good pot odds (50 to win ~200 → ~25%). Q4s has reasonable equity vs a wide BB defending range and some postflop playability as a suited queen. Raising to 200 would be an OOP attempt to steal but Q4s is too marginal to isolate and could put me in difficult spots. I’ll defend the blind cheaply, see a flop, and proceed cautiously — barreling/raising only with real equity (top pair, strong draws) and folding to heavy action when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Five unsuited. The Button (Claude) has folded and the Small Blind (Minni) has completed the bet. In this blind-versus-blind situation, I have the option to check and see the flop or raise to take initiative. Since I will have position throughout the rest of the hand and A5o is a decent but not premium hand, checking is a sound strategy to keep the pot small and see if I can improve on the flop without over-committing.

I defended the blind preflop with Q♠4♠ and completely missed this flop (2♥ T♣ 3♣). I’m out of position and hold only Q‑high with no club and only a very remote backdoor spade possibility, so my equity is minimal. A c‑bet here could fold out some hands but will also get called by tens, small pairs and club draws — hands that dominate or have real equity — putting me in tough spots OOP. I’ll check and see a free turn; plan is to fold to meaningful aggression unless I improve to a real hand (pair or strong draw).

I have Ace high with a backdoor straight draw in a limped pot. Since Minni checked, I will check back to see a free turn card. Ace-high often has showdown value in this spot, and there is no need to inflate the pot with a marginal hand.

I just paired the Q on the turn (Q♥) with Q♠4♠ — top pair but a very weak kicker and I’m out of position. The board has two clubs (T♣, 3♣) so there are still plenty of drawing hands that can beat me if given a free card. Flash has been passive so far (checked the flop), so his range includes many worse pairs, tens, and club draws that I can charge. Betting takes the initiative, protects my hand, and extracts value from worse hands while folding out some pure equity hands. I’ll size it to about half the pot to get value without overcommitting with a mediocre kicker. If he raises I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and reads, but for now bet 100 into 200.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 4). Minni's lead on the turn is a small half-pot bet. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the fact that Ace-high may still be the best hand or has outs to improve (Aces or a 4), calling is the most appropriate action while in position to see a river card.

I turned top pair (Q4s) with a very weak kicker and bet the turn to protect/extract. The river 2♣ pairs the board and also leaves three clubs out there (T♣, 3♣, 2♣), meaning a lot of hands now beat me: any deuce (trips/quads/full house), any Q with a better kicker, and two‑club combos that make a flush. Flash has been passive (checked flop, called my small turn bet), so his calling range contains many of those hands as well as some worse pairs and missed draws. Betting now would get value mostly from hands I already beat rarely, while getting called by a significant portion of hands that beat me — poor risk/reward. I’ll check back and see showdown; if he fires a small bet I can call, if he raises big I can fold. Checking preserves my stack versus the many river hands that dominate my weak top pair.

On the river, the board has paired with a second deuce and completed a club flush. I am holding Ace-high. Minni bet the turn and has now checked the river. In a limped pot, Ace-high has significant showdown value, and betting is unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. By checking behind, I can win the pot against missed draws or lower high cards without risking a check-raise.

I'm on the button with 3♥9♥ in a 3-handed pot. This is a marginal but playable suited hand with huge positional advantage — I want to take the initiative and either steal the blinds or play postflop in position. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is a clean size: it gives good fold equity against the blinds and keeps the pot manageable if I'm called. If I'm 3‑bet I will fold to avoid playing a weak holding out of position; if called I'll use position to c-bet on dry textures and pursue my flush/straight potential. Limping is too passive and invites squeezes, so raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with Q7o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While Q7o has some marginal equity, Minni has been quite active and a 3x raise often puts me in a tough position post-flop out of position, especially with a hand that doesn't play well in multi-way or raised pots. Claude is also still to act in the Big Blind and has a relatively short stack compared to the others, making him potentially more prone to jamming. Given the high rake/variance and the weakness of my hand out of position against a big stack, folding is the most prudent play here to preserve my stack.

Calling 200 more chips with J7s from the big blind against Minni's button raise. This is a standard play because: (1) J7 suited has playability with 5 high cards and straight potential, (2) pot odds of 2.25:1 are favorable, (3) I have positional advantage postflop being in BB heads-up, (4) my stack of 6,200 allows me to see flops and play poker, and (5) Minni's button range is wide enough that J7s likely has reasonable equity. This is not a marginal fold/call situation - J7s plays reasonably well postflop.

Checking J7c on a 3-2-8 flop from the BB against Minni's button raise. I'm out of position with a marginal holding that has overcards and backdoor draws but no made hand. Checking allows me to: (1) control pot size while out of position, (2) see Minni's continuation bet frequency, (3) realize my modest equity cheaply, and (4) make turn decisions with more information. With a hand this weak, betting is risky and doesn't achieve much other than building a pot I'll likely lose. Checking and playing turns is optimal.

I have 3♥9♥ on a 3♣2♠8♠ flop — that’s top pair (treys) with a decent 9 kicker in position against one opponent who just called from the big blind. Claude’s calling range is wide (overcards, small pairs, suited connectors), so as the preflop raiser I should c-bet to extract value from worse pairs/overcards and deny equity to any spade/backdoor draws. The board is relatively dry, so a modest-but-meaningful bet is appropriate: big enough to charge draws and fold out pure air, but not so large I get committed vs a strong hand. I’ll bet 300 into the 650 pot (~45% pot). If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll proceed cautiously (likely folding to severe aggression).

Calling 300 with J7c on a 3-2-8s flop. J7 suited has strong equity as a flush draw (9 outs) plus two overcards. Pot odds of 3.17:1 are favorable—I need only 24% equity to break even and have well above that. Minni's wide button range means I have reasonable equity against her range. Stack size supports seeing the turn. This is a clear value call with both immediate and future equity.

Betting 600 on the turn with Jc7c (4-flush) on a 3c2s8sTc board. This is a strong semi-bluff with 9 outs to the flush plus overcards. Minni checked, indicating weakness or a draw, making this an ideal spot to apply aggression. The bet builds value with my premium draw while generating fold equity. If called, I retain substantial equity on the river. If Minni folds, I win the pot. This balances aggression with equity, consistent with my positional play throughout the hand.

I have top pair (3♣ board, I hold 3♥9♥) but the 10♣ on the turn is an overcard that improves many hands in Claude’s range. Claude called preflop and my flop c‑bet, so his range is wide: small/medium pairs (22–99), some 8x, occasional T‑x, plus spade draws or floats. He now leads for 600 into a 1,850 pot; calling 600 gives me pot odds of about 24.5% (600 / (1850+600)), which is reasonable for my hand vs a mixed value/bluff range. Raising here with a weak kicker would bloat the pot and could get me committed against tens/eights or induce a shove; Claude also tends to call down, so I’d rather control the pot and keep positional advantage. I’ll call the 600, see the river, and then decide — if he fires big on a scary river (T/8/2 or a spade) I’ll fold unless I improve; if he checks I can try a small value bet.

Checking on the river with J7c on a 3c2s8sTc3d board. My semi-bluff from the turn didn't connect—the river 3d is a complete blank that doesn't improve my Jack-high and fills the board. Minni called my aggressive turn raise, indicating she likely has a real hand (pair, two-pair, connected holding, or strong cards). With marginal Jack-high equity and limited fold equity against a player who showed strength postflop, betting would be burning chips. Checking allows for a check-down to showdown or reveals if Minni has a value hand. Given my stack size (5,100) and the pot (2,450), preserving chips with a marginal hand is optimal.

I have trip 3s (3♥9♥ with 3♣3♦ on board) — a very strong showdown hand but vulnerable specifically to full houses (pocket TT, pocket 22, or combos that include a 3 plus a board-rank like T3/32/38). Claude’s line (call preflop, call my flop c‑bet, bet the turn) is consistent with a range that contains overpairs/strong pairs, sets, and some turn‑improvers, and his river check is ambiguous (could be a trap or just a medium/weak hand taking showdown). I want to extract value from the many one‑pair and two‑pair hands (T‑x, 8‑x, lower pairs) that will call a reasonable river bet, while avoiding bloating the pot and getting exploited by a large check‑raise from an unlikely full house. A modest value bet (~1/3 of the pot) accomplishes that: it gets calls from worse hands but keeps the pot controllable if Claude shows aggression. I will bet 1000 chips. If he raises big (turns into a shove or large raise) I will re‑evaluate and lean to folding since that line strongly indicates a full house.

Folding Jc7c on the river against Minni's 1000-chip raise. My hand is Jack-high, which loses to the vast majority of Minni's range given her aggressive line throughout this hand: she check-raised the flop, called my turn semi-bluff raise, and now value-bet the river. The pot odds of 4.45:1 require approximately 18.3% equity to break even, but I'm beaten by any reasonable hand Minni holds—pairs, two-pair, or any draw that improved to a better hand. My best case is against complete bluffs, which are unlikely given her consistent aggression. Preserving my 5,100 stack by folding marginal hands on the river when beaten is critical for long-term profitability in cash games. This is a disciplined fold of a very weak hand.

I am on the button with K8o. This is a standard opening hand in a three-handed game. Claude (SB) and Minni (BB) are both involved. With a stack of 9900, I have enough chips to play aggressively. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or gain control of the betting lead.

5-Jack offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't merit defending from the small blind. I'm facing a button raise from Flash and would be out of position postflop. While Flash shows aggression from the button, I lack the hand strength to defend or fight for the pot. My stack is also relatively short at 5050, so I shouldn't engage in marginal situations. Folding preserves my chips for better opportunities where I have superior hand strength or position. This is a standard, disciplined fold.

Heads-up vs Flash who opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with Ah5s; I’ve already posted 100 so calling requires 200 more (300 total commitment). The pot is 450, so the call costs 200 to win a 650 pot (~30.8% immediate pot odds). Against a button open the range is wide and my ace has decent showdown value and reasonable playability, so defending is standard. I don’t want to 3-bet with a marginal offsuit ace out of position and bloat the pot — I’d prefer to flat and play the flop (look to continue on A-high flops, fold or pot-control OOP on bad runouts). Call to 300 total.

Flop: 3♣ 9♦ 2♥; I hold A♥5♠ — only Ace-high with a lone heart (no pair, just marginal showdown value). I'm out of position vs a wide button open; this is a very dry board where the opener will c-bet a high frequency. Leading out OOP with a marginal offsuit ace is suboptimal — it bloats the pot and gets called by better hands. I’ll check to control pot size and gather info: call small-to-medium continuation bets (good showdown value vs a wide c-bet range) and fold to very large/ polarizing overbets unless the turn brings direct improvement. If Flash checks back I can take a free card or seize initiative on safe turn cards.

The flop is quite dry (3c-9d-2h). Minni checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. I will fire a continuation bet of 300 to take down the pot now, as this board likely missed most of her calling range, and I still have two overcards if I am called.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash). I’m OOP in the BB with A♥5♠ on a very dry 3♣-9♦-2♥ flop. Flash’s 300 into a 950 pot is a standard small/medium c-bet; my Ace‑high has decent showdown value and also blocks many Ax combos. Calling 300 requires ~24% equity to be correct — A5 has roughly that vs a wide c-bet range plus 4 outs to the wheel (any 4) and clear improvement if an A or a 5 hits. Raising OOP here with a marginal offsuit Ace is thin (bloats the pot, opens me to 3-bets, and is easily called by better hands), so I prefer pot control and to see a turn. Plan: call the 300, re-evaluate on the turn — call small/medium bets, fold to large polarizing overbets unless I’ve improved, and play aggressively if I hit an Ace, the wheel (4), or get a favorable checked board.

Turn: 3♣ 9♦ 2♥ 2♦. I hold A♥5♠ — still only Ace-high and I’m out of position. Flash (button) c-bet the flop and now checked the paired turn, which makes both a wide checking range and slowplays (including trips) possible. Betting OOP with a marginal offsuit Ace here is thin: it bloats the pot and gets value-called or check‑raised by hands that currently beat me (9x, 3x, pocket pairs, or a 2). I prefer pot control and information—check to keep the pot manageable, induce bluffs, and retain the option to call small/medium river bets with my blocker to Ax combos. If Flash bets the river I’ll evaluate sizing (call small-to-medium for showdown, fold to large/polarizing raises); if he checks back I take showdown with Ace-high.

On the turn, the deuce pairs the board (3c-9d-2h-2d). Minni has checked to me after calling my flop continuation bet. While I have King-high, which has some showdown value against her complete air, I am likely behind a 9 or a small pocket pair. Since the board is very dry and she called the flop, she is less likely to fold a marginal hand to a second barrel. By checking behind, I take a free card to try and hit a King or simply reach showdown cheaply if she is also holding a weak hand or a missed draw.

I have A♥5♠ on 3♣-9♦-2♥-2♦-J♠. Flash c-bet the flop then checked the turn, showing weakness or pot-control; my Ace-high blocks many Ax combos and has decent showdown value versus his wide opening/c-bet range. A river bet would be a thin bluff (won’t fold Jx and will get called by many worse hands) and only risks bloating the pot against a deeper stack. I check to take a low-variance showdown — if Flash bets a small size I’ll call for showdown; I’ll fold to a large polarizing shove.

The river is the Jack of Spades, making the final board 3c-9d-2h-2d-Js. Minni has checked for the third time this hand. My King-high has some showdown value in a three-handed game, particularly after the turn went check-check. While I could potentially bluff here, Minni's flop call suggests she has a piece of the board (like a 9 or a small pocket pair) that she is unlikely to fold to a single river bet after I showed weakness on the turn. By checking back, I minimize my losses if I am behind and occasionally win the pot against missed floats or weaker high cards.

Calling with A6o from the button. Position is excellent and pot odds are reasonable (3:2). While A6o is not premium, I can play it profitably from the button post-flop when out of position opponents act first. My stack size (50.5 BB) allows for this call without overcommitting. Will evaluate the flop carefully based on what hits.

I’m in the small blind with T♥5♦ facing a limp from the button and the big blind still to act. The price to see a flop is cheap (50 more into a 250 pot) and T5o is a marginal hand that plays poorly if I inflate the pot preflop by raising — I don’t want to get isolated heads-up or multi-way bloated with a weak holding. I’ll complete the small blind (call) to realize equity and see a flop; plan is to proceed only if I pair T or 5, pick up a strong draw, or get favorable action; otherwise be prepared to fold to significant aggression OOP.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Six of clubs. Claude limped on the button and Minni completed from the Small Blind. With a suited hand like this in a limped pot, there is no need to bloat the pot out of position. I will check and take a free flop to see if I can hit a flush draw, straight draw, or a pair.

Flop: 8c 2c Qc. I hold Th5d — no pair, no club, no meaningful equity. This is a limp multiway pot (3 players) and I’m out of position to two opponents who likely have wide ranges and many club combos. With no backdoor flush/straight and the board hugely favoring clubs, I should avoid bloating the pot with a pure bluff. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack; fold to any significant bet and re-evaluate if I see a free card or clear weakness on later streets.

I have flopped a flush with the Ten and Six of clubs on an 8c-2c-Qc board. This is a very strong hand in a limped pot. Minni has checked to me. While my flush is not the nuts, it is significantly ahead of most of my opponents' ranges. I want to build the pot and get value from hands like a single high club (the Ace, King, or Jack of clubs) or a pair of Queens. Betting here also protects my hand against a fourth club appearing on the turn, which might kill the action or put me in a difficult spot if a higher club is out there. I will bet 200 into the 300 pot.

Calling with a nut flush draw on a three-club flop. With 9 clean outs to the nuts plus backup equity, I have ~35% equity against a typical BB raising range. Pot odds of 2.5:1 are highly favorable. Ample stack size and button position allow me to play for another card. This is a mathematically sound call that realizes equity from my strong draw while maintaining flexibility for future streets.

Fold. I have T♥5♦ on 8♣ 2♣ Q♣ — no pair, no club, no meaningful backdoors and I’m out of position to two players. Flash bets 200 and Claude calls in a limp multiway pot, which strongly suggests clubs or at least a Q in the calling range. The pot is 700 and the call is 200, so I need ~22% equity; my hand’s equity here is far below that and it plays very poorly multiway. Raising would be reckless and calling to see a turn is throwing chips away with almost no fold or improvement potential. Preserve the stack and fold.

The board has paired on the turn with the Queen of Spades, making the board 8c-2c-Qc-Qs. I currently hold a flush (Ten-high). While the paired board introduces the possibility of a full house (88, 22, or Qx), it is still a very strong hand in a limped pot. Claude called my flop bet, suggesting he has either a piece of the board, a high club draw, or a flush. If he has a Queen, he now has trips and is likely to call another bet. If he has a high club draw (like the Ac or Kc), he may still call. I will bet 400 into the 700 pot to continue extracting value from these hands while maintaining control of the pot size in case he has me beaten.

Calling with the nut flush draw. With 9 clean outs (any club completes the nuts), I have approximately 19-20% direct equity plus additional equity from A-high. Pot odds of 3.75:1 are favorable, requiring only ~21% equity to break even. The position advantage and strong implied odds when I make my hand make this a mathematically sound call. Stack size (4750) is sufficient to take this line. Standard premium draw play on the turn.

I have a Ten-high flush on a board that has paired (Queens). While a flush is usually very strong, the board pairing on the turn (Qs) and the river being a Ten (Ts) creates some danger. My best 5-card hand is the flush (Qc-Tc-8c-6c-2c). However, in a limped pot, Claude could be calling with any two clubs (including higher ones like the Ac, Kc, or Jc) or a Queen that has now made trips (or potentially a full house if he has Q8, Q2, or QT). Given that I have led the betting on the flop and turn, checking the river allows me to control the pot size and potentially see a showdown cheaply. If Claude bets, I will have to evaluate based on the size, but checking is the safest way to realize my equity without over-committing against a better hand that has been calling me down.

River check-back decision. Flash checked on the river after aggressive lines on flop and turn. My hand is A-high with a missed flush draw (best 5 cards: A-Q-T-8-6). This is extremely weak. Betting would be speculatively weak and I'd get called and lose when Flash has any made hand. By checking, I preserve my stack and either win 1500 against complete air or take a small loss on a showdown. This is the disciplined play with a whiffed draw against an opponent who checked back.

I'm on the Button in a 3-handed pot with Js8c offsuit. Position is a big advantage so I should open-raise a wide range to steal the blinds and play postflop with initiative. The pot is 150 and calling 100 is passive — min-raising to 200 is too small and will invite too many defenders. A standard ~3xBB open to 300 gives solid fold equity vs the blinds (Flash in SB is loose/aggressive and will defend some, Claude in BB has a shorter ~42BB stack and may 3-bet or shove occasionally). I keep stack depth in mind — if I face a large 3-bet or shove I can fold Js8o, but if called I can play the flop in position and leverage my initiative. Raise to 300 (total) to maximize EV here.

I am in the Small Blind with K6o. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. While K6o can sometimes be a marginal defend in a 3-handed game, I am out of position against the chip leader (Minni) and the Big Blind (Claude) has a relatively short stack, which increases the risk of a squeeze or a difficult post-flop scenario. Given the hand's poor playability and my disadvantageous position, I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With 2-5 (deuce-five), one of the worst starting hands in poker, folding is the correct decision despite being in the big blind. While Minni appears to be opening frequently from the button with a wide range, I have virtually no equity pre-flop with this hand. I'm already depleting my stack (now 4250 chips), and playing out of position against a larger stack with a trash hand will lead to losses. I should preserve my chips for a legitimate hand where I can accumulate value. Given my stack size relative to the blinds, I need to be selective about which hands I defend with, and 2-5 doesn't qualify.

I am on the button with Q5o. This is a weak hand, but it is a potential candidate for a steal if the action were folded to me. However, in this 3-handed game, standard opening ranges are wider. With a stack of 100 BBs, I need to be careful not to bleed chips with marginal hands. Folding is the most conservative and safest play here to wait for better spots, especially since I'm facing two active opponents in the blinds.

Calling 50 more (total 100) with 8c7c to get 3:1 odds into the pot. The suited connector has reasonable equity against Minni's BB range and can flop draws/pairs. Given good pot odds and the speculative nature of the hand, this is a defensible call from SB.

I'm in the big blind with Q♦7♠. Flash folded and Claude (SB) just called my blind, leaving a 200-chip pot and no raise. Q7o is a marginal hand and not worth isolating preflop against a single opponent — better to see a flop for free and keep pot control. I'll check and evaluate on the flop: fold to strong aggression without improvement, call small continuation bets for pot odds, and only barrel/raise with clear equity or a strong read.

I flopped two pair (eights and sevens) on 7h-8s-6s with my 8c7c. This is a strong hand that's likely ahead of Minni's big blind range. Minni checked, so I'm first to act. I bet 125 chips as a value bet to build the pot and charge draws (particularly the flush draw with two spades on board). This sizing is roughly 60% of the current pot and appropriate for extracting value while keeping the action alive. My stack (4150) is healthy relative to the pot, so I can comfortably build and play for value.

I have Q♦7♠ on a 7♥8♠6♠ flop — a pair of sevens (medium strength) with a decent queen kicker. Claude (SB) called preflop and now makes a small flop bet (125 into 200, pot 325). The bet size gives me strong pot odds: calling 125 into a 325 pot (pot becomes 450) requires ~28% equity to break even. Claude’s flop c-bet range from the small blind is wide (overcards, weak pairs, some draws), so my pair is often good or at least has reasonable equity. Raising here would inflate the pot and can induce a jam from Claude (he’s a relatively short stack ~4k) or get called by better hands (8x, 66, pockets) where I’d be committed with a marginal holding. I’ll call to keep pot control, realize equity, and reevaluate on the turn — fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, or extract value if I hit trips or the board becomes favorable. Call 125.

Betting 300 chips on the turn with two pair (8s and 7s) on a 7h-8s-6s-Ks board. This is roughly 2/3 pot sizing of the 450 current pot. My two pair is ahead of most of Minni's likely holdings (draws, smaller pairs, weak holdings). The King doesn't scare me significantly - while it could have completed some draws, it's unlikely Minni had a made hand that improved dramatically. Betting maintains value extraction and pot control while building a healthy pot for the river. My stack (4025) is positioned well to play this hand through to showdown.

Turn analysis — board is 7♥ 8♠ 6♠ K♠, I hold Q♦7♠ (a single pair, one spade). Claude limped/called and c-bet the flop; his sizable turn bet (300 into the pot) after the K♠ significantly strengthens his range — top pair Kx, any two-spade combos that now make a flush, sets/two-pair, or a shove-capable value hand given his short stack. Calling 300 to win a 750 pot requires roughly 28–29% equity. My hand has very few clean outs (two sevens, three queens — and several of those still lose to Kx or a made flush), so my equity versus a Kx/flush-heavy betting range is well below the threshold. Raising is thin and likely to get me committed against better hands. I preserve my deep stack and avoid a marginal call here — fold.

Folding 9h5s on the button. While position is favorable, 9-5 offsuit is a weak, unconnected hand with poor potential. Given my depleted stack of 4475 chips and the aggressive tendencies of both blinds (particularly Minni with a 25k+ stack), the risk/reward doesn't justify entering the pot. My previous hands show I've been running a bit loose and losing value. This is a spot to tighten up and preserve chips for stronger situations where I have better equity.

Heads-up from the small blind with T♥7♦. With only the BB to act, this is a spot to open-raise rather than limp — I want initiative and fold equity. T7o is marginal but has decent heads-up playability (can make top pair on Ten boards and has some straight/backdoor potential). A standard 2bb open to 200 (200 total committed) is small, keeps the pot manageable, and applies pressure to the BB’s wide calling range. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold (hand is easily dominated); if called I’ll c-bet on dry flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated/ace-high boards. Raising also avoids being OOP with a passive line. Action: raise to 200 total.

I have Kd 3d in the Big Blind. Minni in the Small Blind has raised to 200, which is a min-raise. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call and I will have position for the rest of the hand. A suited King is a strong hand in a blind-versus-blind scenario, so I will call and see a flop.

I was the preflop aggressor heads-up and this flop (8c Qc 2h) is very dry. T♥7♦ has no pair and no realistic draw; my primary weapon is fold equity. Flash’s calling range from the BB will include many hands (Kx, Ax, clubs, small pairs) that will fold to a c-bet. I want to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable in case I face resistance, so I’ll make a standard ~50% pot continuation bet. If I face a big check-raise I’ll concede (hand has poor showdown value); I’ll call small or medium turns depending on action only if odds and reads justify it. Bet size: 200 into 400 (total committed 400).

I am in the Big Blind with King-high and no real draws on a Queen-high board. Minni, who raised pre-flop from the Small Blind, has followed up with a half-pot continuation bet. While I have position, my hand has very little equity against a standard c-betting range which includes all Queens, pairs, and even some draws that have me beaten or are flipping. Given the lack of a direct draw or a pair, and the fact that my backdoor diamond draw is non-existent on this club-heavy board, I will fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I’m on the button with J♠7♦ in a 3-handed pot — a marginal hand but in position it’s worth opening. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds (especially the deeper SB), so I’ll open-raise to a standard 3x the big blind. 3x (300) gives good fold equity, avoids giving the big blind cheap odds, and keeps the pot manageable against Claude’s ~43BB stack. If I get called I’ll play postflop aggressively with position; I’ll re-evaluate if I face a large 3-bet or shove (folding to a committed shove from BB in most cases with this holding).

I am in the Small Blind with 4h 3d, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 300 from the button. Given the strength of my hand and being out of position for the rest of the hand, I should fold to conserve my stack.

8-2 offsuit is the weakest hand in poker with negligible equity against any reasonable range. While Minni may be stealing from the button, I'm out of position post-flop and my equity (~15-20%) doesn't justify the call despite decent pot odds. With 70 hands remaining and a declining stack, I should preserve chips for higher-equity situations rather than gamble with one of the worst hands in poker.

Holding a pocket pair of sixes on the button in a three-handed game is a strong position. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds or play the hand in position.

Folding 6d8d from Small Blind. While 68s has some playability, I'm out of position, short-stacked at 4325 chips, and facing a Button raise with Minni's massive 25725 stack in the Big Blind. The risk of a 3-bet forcing me into a bad all-in spot with a marginal hand is too high. I should preserve chips for premium hands in better spots where I have fold equity and clearer equity versus my opponents' ranges.

Facing a button open to 300, I'm in the big blind with A♦3♦. This hand is well within a defend range heads-up: it has solid equity vs a BTN opening range (nut-ace potential and backdoor diamond/fold equity) and the pot odds are attractive — calling 200 into a 450 pot (300 total commitment) requires ~31% equity, which A3s exceeds. I'm out of position so I don't want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet; a small min-3-bet would be easy to play against and a larger 3-bet isn't necessary here. Plan: call and see the flop, play cautiously OOP when missing, and extract value/raise when I hit the ace or strong diamond/paired boards.

Check. I’m out of position with A♦3♦ on a very dry 2♣9♣4♥ flop — I missed the board (no pair, no club draw, only a backdoor diamond). Flash opened wide from the button and will c-bet often, but I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with marginal equity. Checking controls the pot, keeps his bluffs and weak c-bets in the range, and allows me to call reasonable-sized bets to realize my equity and see if I can hit an Ace or backdoor diamond. Plan vs likely actions: call small-to-medium C-bets (≈≤½ pot) and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to very large, polarizing overbets; if he checks back I can take a free turn and apply selective pressure on appropriate cards (diamond or A).

I have pocket sixes on a relatively low flop (9-4-2), putting me in a position where I likely have the best hand against a wide range of calling hands from the Big Blind. However, the board has two clubs and several overcards could come on the turn. I will place a continuation bet to take down the pot immediately or get value from drawing hands and smaller pairs (like 4x or 2x). A bet of 350 into a 650 pot is a standard sizing to achieve this.

Call 350. The flop is very dry (2c-9c-4h) and Flash’s c-bet is small (350 into a 1000 pot) so he’s likely c-betting a wide BTN range. Calling requires ~26% equity (350/(1000+350) ≈ 26%). A♦3♦ has reasonable showdown equity here — I have six primary outs to pair (three Aces + three Threes) and modest backdoor diamond/runner equity, and I block some of his Ax combos. I’m out of position so don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin check-raise/3-bet; calling keeps his bluffs in and lets me realize equity cheaply. Plan: call and re-evaluate on the turn — if I pair an Ace or pick up a diamond I can apply pressure or raise for value; if he barrels large/polarized I’ll likely fold. Given stack depths and the small bet size, a flat call is the lowest-variance, +EV line here.

I called the flop as planned to realize equity with A♦3♦ vs a wide BTN c-bet. The turn pares the board (2♥), leaving me with only 6 clean outs (3 Aces + 3 Threes) — roughly a 13% chance to improve on the river. Flash checked the turn, which looks like pot control or a capped/missed-broadway/draw-heavy range. Leading OOP here with just Ace-high and no flush backdoor is thin: my fold equity is limited, and if called I face tough river decisions and potential check-raises. With healthy stacks and the small current pot, the lowest-variance +EV line is to check and see the river for free — keep the pot under control, realize equity, and only convert to value when I actually pair (or face a small river bet I can call). Therefore I will check behind on the turn.

I have pocket sixes on a board of 2-9-4-2. While I likely have the best hand if Minni doesn't have a 9 or a 2, the second 2 on the turn doesn't significantly help my hand and potentially strengthens any deuce in the opponent's range. By checking back the turn, I am controlling the size of the pot with a marginal made hand, avoiding a check-raise from a 9 or a 2, and setting up a situation where I can comfortably call a reasonable river bet or take the hand to showdown for free if she checks again. This also prevents me from over-committing against a range that likely only calls a second barrel with better hands like a 9.

Check. The river (2c 9c 4h 2h 9h) leaves me with A♦3♦ — just Ace‑high. The board is double‑paired (2s and 9s) so many villain holdings (any 2, any 9, pocket 4s, etc.) are full houses and beat me. Flash’s line (button open, small flop c‑bet, check turn) can include a lot of air or missed draws, but the river heavily improves lots of value combos and makes a bluff here thin. I also block some Ax bluffs by holding the Ad, reducing my bluff equity. Betting would be a thin, low‑EV shove to fold out hands that will rarely fold (and will often get called by worse or crushed by full houses). Checking is the lowest‑variance, +EV line: realize showdown for free and force Flash to create a story. If he checks behind I get to show; if he leads, I’ll fold most reasonable bets (only calling a very tiny probe if I think he’s turning a missed draw into a tiny bluff).

The board is double-paired with Nines and Deuces (9-9-2-2-4). My pocket Sixes give me a two-pair, Nines and Sixes, with a Four kicker (9-9-6-6-4). This hand beats any non-pair hands like Ace-high or King-high, which only have the board's Nines and Deuces. It also beats pocket pairs like 33, 44, and 55. However, I lose to any Nine, any Deuce, or any pocket pair higher than Sixes (77, 88, TT, etc.). Minni has checked three streets. While she could be trapping with a Full House, it's more likely she has a missed draw (like clubs) or a weak piece of the board. Since a bet is unlikely to be called by many worse hands (maybe only 4x or 55) and I could be check-raised by a better hand, checking back is the best way to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown.

Calling with K8o on the button in a 3-handed game. This hand has reasonable playability in position, and the 100 chip call is a small investment. With my relatively short stack, I need to play more hands from late position to build chips. Position advantage and reasonable odds justify calling here rather than folding a potentially profitable spot or raising speculatively.

T5o is a weak, often dominated hand. With Claude having limped and Flash in the big blind, calling 50 more (100 total committed) is the cheapest +EV option — I'm risking 50 to win a 300 pot (~6:1), so I only need ~14% equity, and T5o has enough multiway equity to justify a call. Raising to 200 to isolate is marginal here: I don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a hand that plays poorly postflop. Plan: complete to see the flop and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression unless I pair the Ten (or make a strong draw), otherwise check/fold OOP to significant bets.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit. Both the Button (Claude) and the Small Blind (Minni) have limped in. While Ace-Seven is a decent hand in a three-handed game, raising out of position against two opponents who have already committed chips can unnecessarily bloat the pot with a hand that often plays better in a smaller, controlled environment. By checking, I see the flop for free and maintain the pot size, allowing me to evaluate the strength of my hand based on the board texture without over-committing early.

I completed preflop with T5o as a cheap speculative call. The flop (Ah 8h 4c) completely misses me: I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor heart possibility (needs runner-runner) plus 6 thin pair-outs (3 Tens, 3 Fives) that are frequently worthless vs any Ace. This is a three-way pot, so my bluff equity is poor and I don't want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and fold to meaningful aggression. I only continue if I actually pair or pick up a real draw on later streets.

I have top pair with an Ace on a board that contains a flush draw (two hearts). In a limped pot, my top pair is likely the best hand currently. I will bet 200 chips into the 300-chip pot to extract value from weaker hands like an 8, a 4, or various draws, while also protecting my hand against the heart draw.

Calling 200 to reach total of 300 chips in pot. I have a pair of 8s with King kicker on a 4c-Ah-8h board. Getting 2.5:1 pot odds with a hand that has showdown value and can improve to two pair or better. Minni's check suggests weakness. With my short stack in a 3-handed game, I should continue with reasonable holdings at favorable odds rather than over-fold. The turn card will provide more information for decision-making.

I’m in SB with T♥5♣ on A♥8♥4♣. I checked the flop to control the pot and Flash makes a small c‑bet to 200; Claude calls. This is a multiway pot and I have no pair, only a very weak backdoor heart (runner‑runner) and ~6 pair‑outs. However the bet is small relative to the pot — calling 200 into ~900–1100 gives ~5.5:1 pot odds, so I only need ~15% equity. My chance to improve to a pair by the river (~24%) makes a cheap call +EV here to see a turn. I’ll call to see the turn but will fold to significant turn aggression (a raise or larger bet) unless I actually pair or pick up a real draw. I won’t inflate the pot OOP with a weak hand or attempt to lead out as a bluff in this spot.

Check. On 4c Ah 8h 7c with T♥5♣ I have no pair and only a very weak runner‑runner heart possibility plus ~6 thin pair outs (3 Tens, 3 Fives). This is a multiway pot where both opponents showed interest on the flop, so they likely have an Ace or a decent pair/draw. There is no value to betting OOP — a bet will usually be called or raised by better hands and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position. With only one card to come (≈13% to hit a pair) I’ll take the free river and fold to significant aggression unless I actually improve or pick up a real draw.

I have improved to two pair (Aces and Sevens) on the turn. In a three-way pot where both opponents called my flop bet, I need to continue betting for value and protection. There are still heart draws and straight draws (like 56) possible, and I can get value from weaker Aces (A5, A6, A9, etc.) or an Eight. I will bet 600 into the 900 pot to extract value while maintaining a strong lead in the hand.

Calling 600 with 8d-Kh on a 4c-Ah-8h-7c board. I have a pair of 8s with decent kicker. Pot odds of 2.5:1 justify a call with approximately 30% equity against Flash's likely range. Minni's check indicates weakness. With a short stack in 3-handed play, I need to take marginally profitable spots to build chips. One river card can still help me improve to two pair or better.

Facing Flash’s turn raise to 600 into a 2100 pot with Claude already calling, I’m out of position with T♥5♣ and no pair. Naively I have ~10 outs (3 Tens + 3 Fives + 4 Sixes) ≈ 21.7% to improve on the river, but I need ~22.2% equity to break even on a call (600/(2100+600)). Many of those outs are not clean — Tens/Fives often still lose to an Ace or a bigger pair — so my realistic equity is below the required threshold. Flash has shown aggression both streets (value-heavy range like Ax, hearts, sets), and Claude’s call is consistent with a connected/made hand. I won’t bloat the pot OOP with a marginal, often second-best draw; preserve the deep stack and fold.

I have Two Pair (Aces and Sevens) on a board of 4c-Ah-8h-7c-5d. While the river 5d does complete some potential straights (6-5, 6-9), it is more likely that Claude is holding a single pair of Aces given his calls on the flop and turn. I will bet 1000 into the 2100 pot to extract value from a weaker Ace or a lower two pair. This amount is large enough to get paid but small enough that he might still feel comfortable calling with one pair.

Calling 1000 on the river with pair of 8s and King kicker. Getting 3.1:1 pot odds which requires only 24% equity to break even. Against Flash's likely continuing range (mix of strong Ax hands and possible bluffs), I estimate 30-35% equity. My pair has showdown value and I need to accumulate chips with my short stack. Mathematically sound marginal call with reasonable pot odds.

Button with A♠7♦ in a 3-handed pot: this is a standard open. With the blinds as the only opponents (SB Flash 12,650 — plays aggressively/defends; BB Claude 2,325 — short ~23bb), raising lets me (a) steal the blinds, (b) isolate the short stack, or (c) take the initiative and play postflop in position. I’ll raise to 300 — large enough to discourage free flops and to apply pressure on Claude’s short stack, but a normal table sizing here. If Claude jams his 23bb I will call (good equity vs a wide shove); if Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold unless the sizing is small. Goal: leverage position and table dynamics to pick up the pot or play a favorable heads-up pot.

I am in the Small Blind holding K4o. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While I have a King, my kicker is very weak and I will be out of position for the remainder of the hand if I call. Furthermore, Claude in the Big Blind is short-stacked with only 2,325 chips, making a 3-bet shove from him a significant possibility. K4o is not a strong enough hand to call a shove or to play profitably out of position against an active Button open. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Short-stacked (23 BB) with J7o in the BB vs. button raise. Minni's wide raising range combined with favorable pot odds (2.25:1) makes this a marginally profitable call. At critical stack depth, need to get chips in with reasonable equity rather than fold away blinds. J7 has ~35-40% equity against her button range.

Short-stacked at 21 BB with J7o on K-3-2 flop. J-high is very weak currently, though I have some straight draw possibilities. Checking allows me to keep pot control with a marginal hand and gather information about Minni's action before committing my remaining stack. With such limited chips, I need to be strategic about when to go all-in. If Minni bets (likely given her aggressive style), I can then reassess pot odds and equity before making a commitment decision.

I opened preflop and have position in a HU pot. Flop K♠3♣2♣ (K high with two clubs) misses my A♠7♦ but is fairly dry — good board to continuation-bet and take the pot away from unpaired defending hands. Claude is short (~21bb) and will defend wide but is also incentivized to shove only with real hands; I want to apply pressure without bloating the pot. A small c-bet (≈1/3 pot) maximizes fold equity, keeps the pot manageable if he wakes up, and preserves maneuverability for the turn. I will bet 225 to charge his draws and weaker holdings; if he jams I will fold (his shove is likely strong and calling ~19bb with just A7 is a marginal +EV decision), and if he calls I continue to use position to control the pot and re-evaluate on the turn.

Critical short-stack fold with J7o on K-3-2 flop vs aggressive opponent. While pot odds (4.9:1) are technically attractive, J7 has only ~25-35% equity against Minni's likely range. At 21 BB, I need to be more selective about committing my chips. Even a successful call leaves me at 19 BB in a weakened position. Fold to preserve stack for better spots.

I am on the button with Ten-Five offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand, even in a 3-handed game. While the button should typically open a wide range, Ten-Five is well below the threshold for a profitable opening raise. With Claude having a short stack and Minni being a very aggressive big stack in the blinds, there is no reason to get involved with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling 50 to complete the small blind with A9o against Minni in the big blind. Getting 3:1 pot odds (150 to call 50) with a reasonable hand that plays well heads-up. The cost is minimal relative to my stack and the odds are favorable.

I have T♠3♦ (T3o) in the big blind, pot is 200 and there’s no additional cost to see the flop. This is a weak, unsuited hand with little showdown value and poor playability out of position against the small blind. Claude is short-stacked (2025) and already just called the SB; there’s no need to iso-raise and bloat the pot with junk. I’ll check to see a cheap flop, preserve my deep stack, and proceed cautiously — folding to serious aggression unless I hit a meaningful improvement (pair/top pair/strong draw).

Checking with A9c on a KK9 flop in heads-up play. I have a pair of 9s with an ace kicker, which is decent but vulnerable to Minni having a King. Being out of position and having a relatively short stack (2025), checking allows me to gather information about her hand strength without committing additional chips. This is the most prudent line - if she checks back, I can proceed cautiously on the turn. If she bets, I'll need to evaluate based on the bet size and my hand strength.

Board: K♥ K♣ 9♦. I hold T♠3♦ — no pair, no meaningful draw (only 4 weak outs to a single pair). Claude limp-called preflop and checked the flop; he’s short-stacked (~20BB) so he can shove or call light, and he also can have Kx or a small/medium pocket pair. I’m in position but this is a very dry, high-board spot where my hand has almost no showdown value and very poor equity against reasonable calling ranges. There’s no need to bloat the pot or turn this into a shove contest with essentially air — a probe bet can be met by a shove or a call by many hands that beat me. I’ll check to exercise position, control the pot, and fold to meaningful aggression; reevaluate on the turn if a cheap card comes or if Claude shows weakness.

Checking on the turn with A9c (pair of 9s) on KhKc9d4c. Minni checked the flop showing weakness, suggesting she likely doesn't have a King. My pair of 9s is likely ahead, but the double Kings make this a dangerous board to inflate the pot when holding a vulnerable hand. With a limited stack of 2025, checking allows me to control pot size and reach the river economically. If she bets, I can fold without major damage. If she checks back, I can assess the river with more information. This is a prudent line given my stack depth and hand vulnerability against her possible King.

Check. The board (K♥ K♣ 9♦ 4♣) is very dry and my T♠3♦ has almost no showdown value or fold equity. Pot is only 200 and Claude is short (~20BB) after a limp/call — if I bet small he can easily shove and force me into a marginal, often losing all‑in decision. Checking controls the pot, lets me realize my tiny equity (few outs to pair on the river) for free, and keeps options open on the river. Plan: check to the river, fold to any sizable bet/shove, and only continue to small river bets or with an actual improvement.

Checking on the river with pair of Nines and Ace kicker. Minni has checked through flop, turn, and now the river, indicating weakness. With a short stack of 2025 and a likely-ahead hand, checking allows me to either win the pot if she checks back, or make an informed decision if she bets. This conservative line preserves chips while capitalizing on her apparent weakness.

River: I hold T♠3♦ and the Ten paired on the river (board K♥ K♣ 9♦ 4♣ T♣) — bottom pair. This is a marginal hand: it beats small pairs and many 9x but loses to any K (trips), to two‑pair combos (T9) and to Tens with a better kicker. Claude limp‑called preflop and has been passive (checked flop/turn/river) and is short (~20BB), so his range is wide but he can still shove with strong hands. The pot is tiny (200). Betting risks inducing a shove or a call from hands that currently beat me; checking realizes my showdown value for free and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal holding. I’ll check back and show down; if Claude had fired a small bet I’d consider a call for thin value but would fold to a large shove.

I'm holding 5♠4♦, one of the weakest possible hands in poker. Despite being in the Button (the best position), this hand plays poorly in all situations. While I'm only facing a call of 100 chips into a 150-chip pot, my stack is relatively short at 2,225 chips, and I need to be selective with my plays. Folding weak hands like this from marginal situations is crucial for stack preservation and allows me to wait for better spots to make my moves. The risk/reward simply doesn't favor playing this hand, especially when both opponents have significant stacks and can punish me post-flop if I miss (which is very likely). I'll fold and look for a better opportunity on the next hand.

Heads-up from the small blind (I'm effectively the button postflop) with 7♣6♦. This is a playable hand in position — cheap to see a flop and has good straight/connector potential. Pot is 150, I need to call 50 more (total 100) which is a small investment vs our 25k stack and gives ~4:1 immediate pot odds. Raising to 200 to try to steal is an option, but with this marginal offsuit holding I don't want to inflate the pot and risk a 3-bet out of position on later streets. I’ll complete to 100, see the flop, and use position to play aggressively when I hit (pairs, draws) and control the pot when I miss.
