I am in the Big Blind with Td 9h. Claude folded and Minni completed from the Small Blind. T9o is a solid hand to see a flop with in a limped pot. There is no need to raise and build a pot with a mediocre hand against the chip leader, so I will check and take the free flop.

I have 7c6d on 2s Jd 7h — middle pair with a weak kicker, in position heads-up. Flash checked preflop so his range is wide (many missed overcards, small pairs, draws and some Jx). I want to get thin value and deny free cards that can improve overcards/draws, while not bloating the pot with a marginal holding. A half-pot bet (~100 into the 200 pot) charges draws and gets called by worse hands. If he raises big I’ll fold and preserve my stack; if he calls I’ll use position to re-evaluate on the turn and extract or control the pot accordingly.

On a board of J-7-2 rainbow, my T9o has no pair and no direct draw. Minni, as the small blind completion, leads out for half the pot. Given her large stack and aggressive history, chasing with only two overcards (which may not even be good if they hit) is not a profitable strategy. Folding is the most disciplined move here to preserve chips for better spots.

I’m on the button in a 3‑handed pot with Jc3h — a weak offsuit holding but I have position and can open wide. I’ll open‑raise to 300 (standard ~3x) to apply pressure and try to steal the blinds. If Claude (the short 21BB) jams over my raise I will fold this marginal hand; if I get called I’ll play the flop in position, c‑bet favorable textures and control the pot size against resistance.

I am holding Jd 4h in the Small Blind. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. J4o is a very weak hand to defend with, especially out of position. Additionally, Claude in the Big Blind is short-stacked, increasing the likelihood of an all-in squeeze. Folding is the most profitable and safest long-term decision here.

Folding Ad4d from the big blind. This is a weak hand (ace with poor 4-kicker) and I'm out of position against an aggressive opponent with a larger stack. My short stack (2,125) means I should reserve chips for premium hands rather than marginal ones. While button raises are wide-ranged, I lack sufficient equity and positional advantage to justify calling 200 more. Better spot will come.

I am on the button with 5-3 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude in the small blind has a short stack of about 20 big blinds, making him likely to shove over a raise. Minni in the big blind is the chip leader and has been playing aggressively. Folding is the most prudent action with this hand in this position.

Folding Tc5h from the small blind. This is a weak hand with poor playability out of position. Despite the low additional cost to call, my short stack (2,075 chips with 63 hands remaining) demands strategic chip preservation. I should wait for premium hands or better spots to maximize my remaining equity rather than getting involved in marginal situations.

Calling 100 with pocket 3s on the button. Getting 1.5:1 pot odds and excellent position. Short stack requires preservation of chips while maintaining equity. Pocket pair is a complete hand with reasonable win probability in a 3-way pot. Position provides fold equity post-flop if the board doesn't help.

Hand is 5♣8♦ in the small blind facing a limp from Claude (button) and a big blind posted by Flash. It’s only 50 more to see a flop (pot 250 -> calling 50 gives ~6:1 immediate pot odds), so completing is the low-variance, correct play. This hand is weak and plays poorly out of position, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a raise and face wide defense from the BB or a short-stack shove from Claude. Plan: call to complete to 100, see the flop, and exercise pot control — check/fold to significant aggression unless I improve to a pair or a realistic draw.

In the Big Blind, Claude (the short stack) has limped from the button and Minni has completed from the Small Blind. I hold Qd9s. While not a premium hand, it is strong enough to check and see a flop for free in a three-way pot, especially since my stack is comfortable and Claude is very short. Raising here might force a shove from Claude that I don't necessarily want to call with this hand, or a call from Minni which keeps the pot bloated out of position. Checking is the most prudent play to maintain pot control and see how the board develops.

I missed the flop — no pair, no heart, only a gutshot to a 5-6-7-8-9 straight (a 7 gives me the straight). That’s ~4 outs (~8.5% to hit the turn, ~17% by the river), so my equity is low against likely Ax or other made hands. There are two opponents and an A on board, so betting would bloat the pot with weak equity and put me in tough spots out of position. I’ll pot-control and check to see a free turn; I’ll call a small bet to continue for cheap but fold to significant aggression unless I pair or improve to a clear draw/winner.

In this limped three-way pot, I have flopped middle pair (nines) with a Queen kicker on an Ace-high board with a flush draw. Minni (Small Blind) has checked to me. While my hand has some showdown value, it is not strong enough to value bet aggressively, especially with the short-stacked Claude yet to act. Checking behind allows me to keep the pot small, control risk against a potential check-raise from Minni, and avoid being forced into a difficult decision if Claude decides to shove his remaining chips. I can comfortably evaluate the turn for free.

Both opponents checked the flop to me in position. With pocket 3s, I have a made hand that wins against all air. The Ace-high board likely missed both players based on their check-to-position action. I'm betting 175 (58% of pot) as a value/thin-value bet to win the pot now or extract value if called. This sizing controls my short stack while representing strength and taking advantage of my positional advantage.

Board is 6♥ A♠ 9♥; I hold 5♣8♦. After I checked, Claude (button) bets 175 into a 475 pot. I only have a 4-card straight draw to the 7s (4 outs) plus pair-improvement outs (three 5s and three 8s) — 10 total outs to improve by the river (~38% raw). The bet requires ~27% equity to call (175/(475+175) ≈ 26.9%). Given the small bet size, my raw improvement odds, and Claude’s limp/c-bet tendencies, the low-variance play is to call and see the turn. I’m out of position and have no backdoor flush, so raising as a bluff is unattractive and folding concedes >25% equity. Plan: call to realize equity, fold to significant raise on the turn, and continue only if I pair or make the straight (or spot a favorable read).

I have middle pair (nines) with a Queen kicker on an Ace-high board. Claude, the button limper, has made a small bet of 175 into the 300 pot, and Minni has called. Given the pot odds (calling 175 to win a total pot of 650), I have a very easy call. My hand still has significant showdown value in a limped pot, although I must be cautious of the Ace and the heart flush draw. Raising would likely only fold out worse hands and isolate me against an Ace, so calling to see a turn is the most logical play.

I check. I hold 5♣8♦ on 6♥ A♠ 9♥ (turn A♥). I called the tiny flop bet to realize equity (on the flop I had four 7s for the gutshot plus three 5s and three 8s — ~10 outs to the river). The turn pairing the Ace and bringing a third heart dramatically improves opponent ranges: any Ax is now trips, a 9 or 6 can be two pair, and two hearts in hand become a made flush. I’m out of position in a three-way pot (pot 825) and both opponents showed willingness to call the flop — multiway bluffs are unlikely to succeed and betting only bloats the pot with very little equity. My chance to improve on the river is only ~21.7% and many of those outs are dirty vs. Ax/full-house possibilities. Pot control is the correct approach: take a free card to the river, fold to significant turn aggression, and only continue if I pair/make the straight or face a very small bet that the pot odds justify. Therefore I check to the river.

On this turn, the Ace of Hearts pairs the board and completes a potential heart flush. My hand, middle pair with a Queen kicker, is now effectively two pair (Aces and Nines). In a limped pot, this has some showdown value, but it is extremely vulnerable to any Ace or any heart. Minni has checked to me, and Claude is short-stacked behind me. Checking is the most sensible play to control the pot, avoid being forced into a decision by a Claude shove, and see if I can get to a cheap showdown.

On the turn with pocket 3s on a board of 6h As 9h Ah, I'm in a weak position. The second Ace that arrived is actually detrimental to my hand, as it strengthens potential holdings for my opponents. Both players checked the turn to me after I bet the flop, suggesting they're either on draws or missed hands entirely. However, betting again with such a weak hand (just a low pair) when my opponents show continued weakness is unlikely to win more chips - I'd likely face a check-raise or be called by a better hand. My chip stack (1800) requires careful preservation for better spots. Checking allows me to potentially win the pot at showdown if my pair of 3s holds, or see a river card for free. This maximizes my equity while minimizing risk with a hand that's vulnerable to many possible holdings.

Board is 6♥ A♠ 9♥ / turn A♥ / river 2♥ — four hearts on board and paired aces. I hold 5♣8♦: no hearts, no pair, no bluff-catcher. This is a three-way pot where both opponents called a small flop bet and then checked the turn; that range includes many heart combos, Ax, and medium pairs. With four hearts on board any single heart in an opponent’s hand is a made flush, and there are also combos that make full houses or quads (Ax+one of the board ranks, pocket pair matching a board card, or pocket Aces). Bluffing multiway here has very poor equity — the chance both opponents fold to a river stab is low, and getting called by a heart or better will cost more than the occasional scoop. I preserve stack and avoid creating marginal spots: check and fold to any bet. If everyone checks through I win the pot (no risk); if someone leads I’ll fold unless I see a very specific line that indicates missed draws only.

The river completes a four-flush board (6h, 9h, Ah, 2h). I hold Qd 9s, giving me two pair (Aces and Nines) but no heart. In a limped pot, it is highly probable that one of my opponents holds a heart, especially with the board showing four of them. Minni has checked, and Claude, who showed aggression on the flop but checked the turn, is still to act. Betting here is unlikely to get called by worse hands and will almost certainly be called or raised by any flush. Checking allows me to reach showdown as cheaply as possible and potentially win against hands like lower pairs or complete air that didn't connect with the hearts.

River action with pocket 3s on a double-paired board (6h As 9h Ah 2h). My hand is extremely weak - just a pair of 3s. Both opponents checked the turn and have now checked the river, indicating continued weakness or completed draws that missed. With a short stack of 1800 and pot at 825, betting my weak pair accomplishes nothing: I'm only called by better hands (any pocket pair above 3s, any ace with kicker), and if I bet, opponents fold their air anyway. Checking allows me to see showdown cheaply with equity against their checking patterns. The 2h river didn't improve my hand. Given my marginal holding and precarious chip position, preservation through showdown with a weak but potentially winning hand is superior to bluffing away chips or trying thin value on a board that's likely hit both opponents' ranges better than mine.

I’m on the button with J♠8♣ — a marginal but playable hand in position against only the blinds. Both opponents are live: Flash (SB) is capable of aggression but Claude (BB) is short (~25 BB) and often calls; that makes an open-raise attractive to seize initiative and either take the blinds or play post‑flop in position. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the big blind) — big enough to generate meaningful fold equity and isolate one opponent, but not oversized. If I face a large 3‑bet or a shove from Claude I’ll fold J8o (he’s likely ahead); if heads‑up or called I’ll c‑bet and play a controlled, positional post‑flop game.

I am in the Small Blind holding 82o, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has raised to 3BB (300) from the button. Calling with such a weak hand out of position is a significantly negative expected value play, especially with Claude having a short stack behind me who might shove. Folding is the only sensible move.

8-3 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds, the hand only has ~20-25% equity against Minni's button-raising range, which falls short of the ~30% needed to justify a call. With a modest stack of 2525 chips, I should preserve capital for premium holdings rather than commit chips to marginal spots. This is a clear fold.

I am holding King-Queen offsuit (Kc, Qs) on the button. This is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. With Claude being short-stacked in the small blind and Minni as the big stack in the big blind, I want to open for a standard raise to take control of the pot and potentially isolate or take the blinds down immediately. A 3x raise is standard and effective here.

I'm in the small blind facing a button raise from Flash with 2-3 offsuit, one of the weakest hands in poker. With only 2475 chips in my stack, I need to be selective with my investments. Several factors make folding the correct decision: (1) 2-3o has minimal equity against Flash's raising range; (2) I'm out of position post-flop; (3) Minni in the big blind still has action and could re-raise, putting me in an even worse spot; (4) My stack is relatively short, so I should preserve chips for better situations. This is a routine fold.

I’m in the big blind with 4c8d facing a button open to 300 (I have 100 already in). 4–8 offsuit is a very weak hand and I’ll be out of position post‑flop. Calling 200 into a 450 pot requires ~31% equity to break even; 4c8d won’t have that against a standard BTN opening range, and a light 3‑bet bluff OOP is unattractive with this holding. Preserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

I'm holding 5-10 offsuit on the button with a short stack of 2475. This is a weak, unplayable hand with poor showdown value against two opponents (Minni and Flash). The hand lacks both strength and playability - I cannot win without improvement, and even then, my reverse implied odds are poor given my stack size. With 5-10o, I should be folding the vast majority of the time, especially from the button in an open situation. Folding here preserves chips for better opportunities and avoids getting entangled in a marginal spot.

Heads-up from the small blind I should be opening a very wide range — I’ll have position postflop. 6♥7♦ is a playable, connected hand with decent equity and good postflop playability, so I want to take the initiative. I’ll raise to 200 (2x BB) to generate fold equity, keep the pot manageable against a deep, sticky BB, and play the hand in position. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold or re-evaluate; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to extract value or fold to strong resistance.

I am in the big blind with 6s3c. Minni, who has a very large stack and has been opening frequently, raised to 200 from the small blind. I only need to call 100 more to see a flop in a 300 chip pot (3:1 odds). However, 63o is one of the weakest hands in poker and plays poorly post-flop, especially against a deep-stacked opponent who has shown aggression in previous hands. Given the low quality of the hand, folding is the standard play to avoid difficult post-flop situations with a marginal holding.

Open-raise to take the blinds and play the rest of the hand in position. K♦7♠ is marginal, but with only the SB and a short-stacked BB to act I want to seize initiative from the button. A 3bb open to 300 is a standard size here — it gives fold equity against Flash (aggressive but OOP) and either isolates heads-up or forces the short-stacked Claude to commit a large portion of his stack if he shoves (which I can call/re-evaluate profitably given the small relative risk). If called I'll use position to c-bet dry flops and pot-control on coordinated boards; if I face a big 3-bet I'll fold and preserve my deep stack. Raising maximizes my long-term expected value vs limping or just calling.

I am holding Seven of Hearts and Deuce of Diamonds, which is the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Minni raised from the button, and I am in the small blind. There is no reason to play this hand, especially out of position. I will fold.

With 5d-Js in the big blind against Minni's button raise, this is a clear fold. The hand is too weak (weak kicker with a jack), and I have a relatively short stack (2375 chips). At this point in the session (hand 92 of 150), I need to preserve chips and wait for premium hands or better spots to make moves. Minni has been aggressive throughout the session, but that doesn't change the fact that 5-J offsuit is dominated by many of her raising hands. Folding allows me to stay patient and look for better opportunities when I'm in a better position or have stronger cards.

I am on the button with Ace-Three suited (As3s). In a 3-handed game, this is a very strong starting hand and a standard open-raising candidate. Claude in the small blind has a relatively short stack (around 23 big blinds), so I need to be prepared for a potential shove, but I should definitely open to apply pressure and build the pot. I will raise to 300.

7-2 offsuit is the worst hand in poker. In the small blind facing a button raise, this is a clear fold despite the reasonable pot odds. With 2325 chips (23BB stack), I'm not yet in desperate push-all-in territory where I'd need to gamble with trash hands. Flash's raise from the button suggests a reasonable hand range, and 7-2o has minimal equity against most holdings. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities ahead.

Facing a 3x open to 300 from the button with Q♠3♥ in the big blind. The pot is 450 and I must call 200 (to 300 total); that gives ~30.8% break‑even equity — and vs a wide button opening range this hand has enough equity to defend but is too weak to 3‑bet as a bluff or for value. With deep stacks I want to keep the pot manageable and see a flop: I’ll call and continue only with a clear piece (pair of queens or a well‑priced draw) or if I can exploit post‑flop weakness from Flash; otherwise I’ll check/fold to sustained aggression. A min‑raise to 500 is unnecessary and commits me with a marginal hand against an aggressive opener.

I hold Q♠3♥ on A♥–3♣–2♠: I flopped a weak pair (a trey) with a mediocre Q kicker. The Ace on board heavily favors the pre‑flop raiser’s range (lots of A‑x and broadways), and there are virtually no meaningful draws to protect against. My objective is pot control and to realize equity — this hand is good for showdown vs bluffs and worse pairs but too weak to build a big pot or to lead for thin value. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable and give Flash chances to bluff. If he checks back I get a free turn. If he cbets a small amount (≈¼–⅓ pot, ~150–250) I’ll call to see the turn because my pair has showdown value and he may be barreling light; if he barrels big or raises I will fold, since that line is usually Ax or a strong made hand and I don’t want to commit with a marginal pair. Betting myself here would create difficult decisions vs raises and overcommitment — checking is the lowest variance, highest expected-value line.

I have flopped two pair (Aces and Treys) on a very dry board. This is a very strong hand. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni has checked to me, I will place a value bet. A bet of approximately half the pot (350) is appropriate here to get value from weaker Aces or straight draws while keeping the pot manageable against any potential traps.

I hold Q♠3♥ on A♥–3♣–2♠: a medium pair (treys) with a decent kicker but the Ace on board heavily favors the preflop raiser’s range. Flash opened wide from the button and now bets 350 into the ~650 pot (~54% pot). The pot is 1000 and calling 350 requires ~26% equity to break even. Versus a wide BTN c‑betting range I still have meaningful showdown equity (I beat missed Kx/Qx and pure bluffs and tie/beat some small pairs), so the call is close to +EV. Raising is the wrong line — I have no strong redraws and would be risking too much with a marginal hand. Folding would be too nitty given the pot odds and villain’s wide opening range. I will call 350 to keep the pot controlled and see a turn; plan is to fold to sizable turn aggression (big second‑barrel or check‑raise) but to continue (check/call) against small turn bets or check back safe turns.

I have Q♠3♥ on A♥–3♣–2♠–K♥: a medium one‑pair hand (trip blocker only to pocket 3s) with decent showdown value but no backdoor draws. Flash (button) opened and barreled the flop; I called to keep the pot controlled. He now checks the King on the turn — a line that usually shows either pot control with A‑x/K‑x or a missed bluff attempt rather than strength that I can value‑extract against. Betting here would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots against Ax or Kx; a bet also has limited fold equity versus this opponent’s range. Raising is out of the question with such a marginal hand.

Given deep stacks and the board texture, the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line is to check and see a river. I’ll conserve chips, realize my showdown equity, and re‑evaluate on the river (fold to large aggression, call small bets or shove river if it’s a clear bluff). Therefore: check.

I have two pair (Aces and Treys) on a board of 2s-Ah-3c-Kh. My hand remains very strong as it is unlikely the King improved Minni's hand, as hands like AK or KK would typically 3-bet pre-flop from the big blind. Minni's most likely holdings are weaker Aces (like A4-AQ) or potentially a straight draw like 45. I will bet 700 to extract value from these hands and protect my equity before the river.

I hold Q♠3♥ on A♥–3♣–2♠–K♥: just one pair (treys) with a mediocre kicker. The pot is 2050 and Flash fires 700 on the turn; calling 700 requires ~700/(2050+700) ≈ 25.5% equity to break even. I have 5 clean outs to improve (3 queens + 2 threes) — roughly an 11% chance to hit on the river — and my current showdown equity versus a realistic button barreling range is well under the required 25%. Flash opened from the button, made a large flop c-bet and now a sizable turn barrel: that line is heavily weighted toward value (A‑x, K‑x that picks up the turn, sets, two‑pair) rather than pure bluffs. Raising is a poor option — I have no redraws or fold equity to make it profitable and I would only get action from better hands. Calling is marginal at best and will frequently lead to folding on a river shove or paying off more vs value hands. With a strong stack and many profitable spots ahead, the disciplined, lowest‑variance play is to preserve chips and fold to this sizable turn aggression.

Folding 6-7 offsuit on the button. While the pot odds are decent (2:1 to call 100 into 150), my hand is too weak to profitably play in this situation. I'm in a short-stacked position (2325 chips = ~23 BB), which means I need to be selective about which hands to invest chips in. 6-7o lacks the strength and playability for a profitable call here. Minni and Flash have both shown aggressive tendencies throughout the session, making it likely I'll face post-flop pressure with a marginal hand. Better to preserve my stack and wait for a stronger opportunity to accumulate chips.

3♦4♦ is a speculative suited connector with good implied odds but poor postflop position (I’ll be OOP vs the BB). Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 more to see a flop (calling to 100 total) — ~25% break-even — so a flat call is the lowest-variance + EV play. Raising to 200 would bloat the pot and put me OOP with a marginal hand that plays better seeing flops cheaply; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call and evaluate the flop — continue aggressively on strong diamond/connected flops (flush/straight/paired boards), fold or proceed cautiously vs large C-bets on dry boards.

I am in the Big Blind with 6h 4s. Claude has folded and Minni has completed the small blind. Since there is no raise, I have the option to check and see a free flop with my weak hand. There is no strategic benefit to raising with such a low-equity hand in this position.

Flop: Q♠ 4♣ A♦; my hand 4♦3♦ = middle pair (pair of 4s) plus a backdoor diamond draw. Opponent checked preflop so his range is wide and contains many A/Q and overcards that currently beat me. This is a vulnerable pair on a two-overcard flop — I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position or get check-raised and committed with just a small pair. I’ll check for pot control and to induce bluffs. Plan: call a small C-bet (thin value/bluff) and re-evaluate on the turn; fold to a large, polarized bet. If he checks back I’ll take a free card and either lead on a safe improving runout (diamond or a 3/4) or continue cautiously vs aggression.

I have bottom pair (fours) on an Ace-Queen-Four board. Minni checked to me in a small pot. There is no reason to bet here as I am unlikely to get better hands to fold or worse hands to call. Checking back allows me to see a turn for free and keep the pot small with a marginal hand.

Turn (Q♠ 4♣ A♦ J♣) — I only have a small pair (pair of 4s) with no realistic redraws (the backdoor diamond draw is dead after the J♣). Both checked the flop and I took the free card; the villain’s range is wide and contains lots of A/Q/overcard hands that already beat me. Out of position with a vulnerable pair, I don’t want to bloat the pot or get check‑raised off a thin value line. Checking for pot control is the lowest‑variance + EV line: it lets me induce bluffs, see how the BB reacts, and keep options open for the river. Plan: check the turn, call a small bet to realize equity but fold to a large, polarized bet; if checked through I’ll re-evaluate the river (only continue aggressively if I improve or pick up a clear bluffing opportunity).

I have bottom pair (fours) on a board of Ace-Queen-Jack-Four. Minni completed from the small blind and has checked the flop and now the turn. While I have improved to a pair, this board is very coordinated and hits many hands in a completion range (any Ten, King, or even a Jack). There is very little value in betting here, as most hands that call will beat me, and I have enough showdown value to check through and hope to see a cheap river or showdown. I will check back to keep the pot small.

Board: Q♠ 4♣ A♦ J♣ K♥. My hand 4♦3♦ = middle pair (pair of 4s) with no backdoor after the turn. Both streets checked through, so villain’s checked range is wide and contains many hands that beat me (A/Q/K/J and broadway combos), plus some air and tiny pairs. Pot is tiny (200) relative to stacks. Betting here is thin value at best (I only beat very small pairs/missed junk) and risks getting raised off a marginal holding; a small bet won’t extract reliably from better hands and a larger one is unnecessary risk. I’ll control the pot and take the showdown: check. If Flash now fires a river bet I’ll fold to any realistic value/raise and only consider calling a very small, obvous blocking bet — but prefer to preserve stack with this vulnerable second pair.

The board is currently Ace-King-Queen-Jack-Four. My best five-card hand is Ace-King-Queen-Jack-Six (using the six in my hand to replace the four on the board). Minni has checked every street. In a limped pot with a tiny 200-chip pot, there is no value in betting. Most hands that call me will have a Ten for a straight or at least a higher kicker than my six. Checking allows for a cheap showdown where we might occasionally split or I might win if she has a five or lower.

Button with 9♣8♥ offsuit in a 3-handed pot is a good open-raising hand: connected, lots of post‑flop playability and big positional advantage. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call, but opening here builds the pot and puts pressure on a short-stacked BB (Claude, ~22bb) and a deep SB (Flash). I’ll open to a standard 3x the big blind (300 total). This size balances fold equity and keeps the pot manageable if Claude shoves (likely a wide short-stack shove) — I’m prepared to call a shove given my stack and hand equity — and gives me good options postflop in position if called by Flash. Action: raise to 300.

I am in the Small Blind with 4s 3d, which is an extremely weak hand. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Claude is in the Big Blind with a very short stack and might shove or call. Given my poor hand strength and being out of position for the rest of the hand, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

Folding J5o out of position against an aggressive button raise. With a short stack relative to the game stakes, I should play tighter and wait for stronger holdings. J5o plays poorly out of position and is vulnerable to overcards even if I hit the jack. The extra 200 chips isn't worth committing to a marginal situation against a strong opponent with a larger stack.

I am on the button with King-Jack offsuit (KJo), which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. Claude is quite short-stacked (about 22 big blinds), while Minni is the chip leader. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure, take control of the hand, and potentially win the blinds or isolate if Claude decides to commit his short stack.

Folding A4o from small blind against a button raise. With 21.75 BBs, I'm in a critical zone but not yet forced to jam with marginal hands. A4 lacks the kicker strength to justify getting all-in vs. Flash's likely strong range, and Minni's big blind defense could trap me with a premium hand. The risk/reward doesn't favor this marginal hand. I should preserve my remaining chips for premium spots where I have better equity.

I'm in the big blind with Q♠6♠ facing a button raise to 300 (pot 450, I must call 200). Q6s is marginal but suited Q has decent equity vs a wide button-steal range and the stacks are deep enough for implied odds. I don't want to 3-bet light out of position with a weak kicker — that bloats the pot and hands like Q6s play much better seeing a flop. Plan: call to 300, evaluate the flop and continue only with top pair, strong draws, or a favorable board; otherwise fold to heavy aggression.

Flop: T♣ K♦ K♣. I have Q♠6♠ — no pair and only a very slim backdoor spade route that needs both turn+river spades. Button opened preflop so their range contains many Kx, Tx, pocket pairs and broadways that connect well with this board. I have almost no showdown equity and little fold equity, and I'm out of position. Don’t want to inflate the pot OOP with air. Plan: pot-control — check and fold to meaningful aggression. If Villain checks back I get a free turn to re-evaluate; only consider calling a very small probe bet as a speculative float if I have a strong read, otherwise fold.

I have flopped trip Kings with a Jack kicker on a board of Tc-Kd-Kc. This is a very strong hand. Since the board is paired, it is less likely my opponent has a King, but they could have a Ten, a pocket pair, or a straight draw like QJ. I will bet 300 into the 650 pot to get value from those hands while keeping the pot manageable and encouraging a call from second-best hands.

I defended preflop with Q♠6♠ planning to check/fold OOP. The flop T♣ K♦ K♣ leaves me with no pair and zero spades on board (so a spade flush is impossible). Flash's line — preflop raise then a 300 bet into a 950 pot — is exactly the sort of action that represents strong Kx, Tx or pocket pairs; calling 300 into the 1250 pot (post-bet) requires ~24% equity. Q6s has almost no showdown or improvement equity vs a value-heavy raising range and I have no fold equity OOP. Raising would be a pure bluff with poor chances of success. Fold and preserve stack for a better spot.

4-Jack offsuit is a very weak hand that doesn't warrant investing chips from the button, especially given my short stack of only 2,175 chips (about 21.75 BB). While position is valuable, I have significant chip disadvantage against both opponents (Minni: 24,600 and Flash: 13,075). Calling 100 into a 150 pot doesn't provide sufficient equity for a weak, unpaired, unconnected, offsuit hand. I need to preserve chips and wait for premium holdings given my short-stack situation. Folding is the disciplined play here.

9♣7♣ is a playable suited connector in the small blind heads-up to the big blind. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure rather than limp/call and let Flash see a cheap flop. A standard raise to 200 (2x BB) isolates the BB, creates fold equity, and keeps the pot a manageable size with deep stacks and good implied odds if called. Plan: c‑bet favorable/dry flops, play cautiously on highly coordinated boards, and fold to large 3‑bets (but consider calling small 3‑bets for pot odds/implied odds). Raising to 200 maximizes EV in this blind-vs-blind spot.

I am in the Big Blind with KQo. Minni, the small blind, has opened with a minimum raise to 200. KQo is a very strong hand in a blind-versus-blind battle. I will 3-bet (raise) to 600 to take control of the pot and extract value from Minni's wide opening range. This also puts pressure on the opponent and allows me to define the hand early.

Flash 3-bet to 600 after my 2x open. I hold 9♣7♣ — a suited connector with good multi‑street playability but I'm OOP. Pot is 800 and I have to call 400 more (600 total) which gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ≈25% equity). A 600 3‑bet from the BB here is relatively small and his range will include a fair number of bluffs/medium strength hands, so 9♣7♣ has ~25–30% equity and strong implied odds with the deep stacks. 4‑bet bluffing with 97s OOP is unattractive and folding is too tight given the odds. Plan: call to see a flop, c‑bet or float on favorable/dry textures, fold to heavy action on runouts that miss, and always respect a large 4‑bet/shove. I will call — commit 600 total.

Flop: 9♦ 2♥ 7♠ — I hold 9♣7♣, which is two pair (9s & 7s) on a very dry, rainbow board. This is a strong, value-heavy holding versus Flash’s 3‑betting range (his small preflop sizing includes a fair number of bluffs and single-pair/overcard holdings). I should build the pot and charge worse hands rather than check and allow him to take the initiative. There are very few draws to worry about, but I want a bet size that extracts value from top pair/overcards and denies cheap equity. Pot is 1,200 — betting ~2/3 pot (800) is a good balance: it gets called by one-pair/overcards and folds pure air. Plan: bet 800; if he calls I’ll continue to extract on safe turns; if he check‑raises big I’ll re-evaluate (many check‑raises are sets/trips, so proceed cautiously and fold to extreme action only if reads/odds demand it).

I 3-bet pre-flop with KQo and Minni called. The flop is 9d 2h 7s, which is relatively dry. Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 800 into a 1200 pot. This is often a sign of a marginal made hand (like a 7, or a pair of 9s) or a draw that wants to set the price. Given that I have two overcards and the pot odds are 2.5:1 (800 to call for a total pot of 2800), I have enough equity to call once to see a turn, especially since my K and Q might still be the best cards if they pair up, and she might be bluffing. Raising here against a lead usually just gets called by better and folds out worse.

Turn gives me a virtually-nut full house: with 9♣7♣ + board 9♦ 2♥ 7♠ 9♥ I have NINES full of SEVENS. There is no realistic way for an opponent to have a better full house or quads here (the card distribution makes quads impossible), so this is a huge-value hand. Flash called my sizable flop bet, so his range contains a lot of hands I now crush (single nines, sevens, two‑pairs, sets, overpairs, bluffs). I want to extract maximum value without folding out calling hands by shoving. A medium‑large value bet (~2/3 pot) will charge top pair/two pair/sets and keep worse hands willing to call; if he calls I can still get more on the river. If he raises very big I’ll re‑evaluate (but few hands beat me). Bet 2000 to build the pot and keep a lot of worse hands in the pot.

I currently hold King-high with no made hand and no draw. Minni led into me on the flop after I 3-bet pre-flop, and she has now fired a large second barrel (2000 into 2800) on the turn when the top card (9) paired. This 'donk-bet' leading line is typically very strong, often representing a 9, a 7, or an overpair. Even if she is bluffing with a straight draw like 86s, I only have King-high and would be forced to fold to almost any river bet. Calling 2000 chips with zero showdown value and no significant equity is not a profitable play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Button with A♠7♣ — a clear open-raise candidate. Table standard open size has been ~3x (300), so I’ll raise to 300 to apply pressure and steal the blinds. Flash (SB, ~117BB) will defend wide; Claude (BB, ~20BB) can shove but often with a very wide range — A7o is often good enough to call a short-stack shove or to isolate. Raising to 300 also denies them cheap odds and keeps position advantage postflop. Plan: if called, C-bet appropriate Ace/high and favourable texture flops; if facing a shove from the short stack, call to isolate; re-evaluate vs a large 3-bet from SB.

I am in the Small Blind with 9c6s, which is a weak hand. Minni has raised from the button to 300, and Claude is in the Big Blind with a very short stack of 2075. Calling out of position with a marginal hand like 96o is generally a losing play, especially when there's a possibility of the short stack jamming. Folding is the most prudent and standard play here to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Folding 45o from the big blind after Minni's raise. This is the worst starting hand in poker with insufficient equity against the raising range. Despite being short-stacked, calling with garbage hands accelerates chip loss. Waiting for premium spots is the optimal long-term strategy.

With 6c 5h on the button, this hand is generally too weak to open in a three-handed game, especially with a short stack in the small blind who might jam and a very active big stack in the big blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With A5 of clubs in the small blind heads-up against Minni's big blind, this is a reasonable calling hand. Already committed 50 as SB, need just 50 more to see the flop. The hand has both flush draw potential and wheel straight possibilities. Short-stacked at 20BB, I need to selectively play reasonable holdings rather than simply fold away my chips. This is a spot worth seeing a flop with.

I'm in the big blind with K♦8♦ and Claude (SB) just limp-called 50. Pot is 200 and I have no call to make. K8s is a reasonable hand to take the initiative with against a limp, especially versus a relatively short-stacked SB (~1,975 ≈ 19.8bb). I want to isolate Claude, gain fold equity, and avoid giving a free flop to speculative holdings. A standard 3x BB-ish sizing is appropriate here: raise to 300 total (I add 200 more) — big enough to charge draws and weaker hands, but small enough to get called by worse. If Claude shoves or 3-bets, I can reassess with his shorter stack; checking would be too passive and keep too many hands in. Therefore: raise to 300.

Calling 200 more (total 250 to match big blind). Short-stacked at 20BB, A5c is a reasonable hand to play. It has wheel and flush draw potential plus ace-high value. Already committed 50 chips as SB, so the additional 200 is a reasonable investment to see the flop. With limited chips, I need to play decent hands rather than continuously fold. Position is poor post-flop but the hand has enough merit to continue.

Short-stacked at 18BB, I need to play aggressively with reasonable holdings. A5c has legitimate equity with wheel and flush draw potential plus ace-high value. Minni checked, suggesting a weak hand or a slowplay. Betting 300 chips (just under half my stack) applies pressure and either wins the pot immediately or gets chips in with a hand that has outs. With this few chips, I can't afford to fold or check down passively.

Preflop I raised to isolate the short-stacked SB. On this flop (Qs 2s Jh) Claude C‑bets 300 into a ~600 pot, making the pot 900 and my price to see the turn 300. I hold K♦8♦ — no pair, only a backdoor diamond plan and a king blocker. Calling costs 300 to win 1200 (pot after call), so I’m getting ~4:1 (need ~25% equity). Claude is short (≈1,475) and has shown a tendency to call down rather than fold big lines; raising here as a bluff risks getting shoved or called by many pairs, so I don’t expect strong fold equity. Given the dry, two‑spade board and my limited equity, the lowest-variance, +EV play is to call and use position to reevaluate on the turn. If I improve (pair or good draw) I can apply pressure; if Claude barrels again and I miss, I can fold without committing a large portion of my deep stack.

Checking on the turn. Minni's check indicates weakness or caution. With A5c and 11 flush outs, I have reasonable equity (~35-40%) but it's marginal for a push-fold decision. Checking preserves chips and allows me to see the river for free. If a club comes, I'll have a strong hand. If it misses, I can evaluate my Ace-high against the river action. Short-stacked play (15BB) requires flexibility, not marginal all-ins.

Claude led the flop and I called; turn checks to me. I have K♦8♦ — no pair and no meaningful draw (no diamonds on board). Claude is short (~1,475) and has a history of calling down rather than folding, so my bluff equity here is limited. Betting now would risk getting called or shoved by many made hands (Qx, Jx, small pairs) and I have very little showdown equity. With position and pot control available, the lowest-variance, +EV line is to check back the turn, see the river, and decide based on his action (I can apply pressure on a safe river or fold to heavy action). Thus: check and keep the pot manageable.

With A5c on Qs 2s Jh 3h 8c, I have ace-high. Minni checked the turn, indicating weakness. Short-stacked at 15BB with marginal hand strength, checking to showdown is prudent. This preserves chips while maintaining showdown value. If she has a weaker hand, I win the pot; if she bets after my check, I can evaluate then. The two checks from Minni suggest she's unlikely to have a strong holding, making ace-high potentially the best hand at showdown.

I paired the 8 on the river (K♦8♦ vs Q♠ 2♠ J♥ 3♥ 8♣). Claude led the flop and then pot‑controlled the turn, which weights his range toward Qx/Jx or other single pairs that are often ahead of my bottom pair. He’s short and has shown a tendency to call down rather than fold, so a river bet would be thin value (most callers beat me) and a bluff is unlikely to get through. Betting risks getting called or shoved by better hands; checking preserves chips and takes the lowest‑variance, highest‑EV line — see showdown cheaply and fold to significant aggression if he now raises. Therefore: check.

With 2-5 offsuit on the button, this is one of the weakest hands in poker. While position is favorable, the hand has very limited win potential post-flop. My stack is moderate at 1,475, so I should preserve chips for premium opportunities. Even though I only need to call 100 to see the flop, the expected value of this hand is too low. Folding weak hands from the button is standard strategy when I have limited resources and need to be selective.

Heads-up in the SB with 6♣9♦ — a marginal, but playable hand heads-up. Pot is only 150 and I only need to put in 50 more (to 100) to see a flop; that’s cheap and keeps my options open. Raising to 200 to try to steal is possible, but Flash has shown willingness to defend/3‑bet and I don’t want to play a weak, often dominated hand out of position. I’ll call the 50 to see the flop; plan is to proceed only with a pair/strong draw or fold to significant postflop aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-King offsuit, a premier starting hand. Minni, who is the chip leader and has shown both aggression and a willingness to call raises, has just completed the small blind. I should raise here for value and to take control of the hand. A raise to 400 (4x the big blind) is a standard size to build the pot and discourage her from realizing equity with a weak hand.

Facing a 4x preflop raise to 400 from Flash (BB) after I had called 50 in the SB. I hold 9♦6♣ — marginal but playable heads‑up. Pot is 500 and I must call 300 more (total committed 400), which gives ~37.5% break‑even equity. Versus a likely wide heads‑up raising range this hand has reasonable equity and postflop playability; 3‑betting with 9‑6o is thin and unnecessary. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed only with a pair or a strong draw; if I miss and face significant aggression I will fold to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position.

I completely missed the flop with 9♦6♣ on T♠ 3♠ J♥ — no pair and no backdoor spade. I only have 6 outs to pair (~24% to hit by river), so my equity is marginal versus the preflop raiser’s likely c‑betting range. Pot is 800 and I’m out of position, so I want to control the pot and avoid paying to chase. I’ll check: if Flash makes a small c‑bet (≈≤200–250) I can call to float because pot odds are attractive; if he makes a standard half‑pot or larger bet (≈400+), I’ll fold since calling requires ~25% equity and I’m usually dominated. If he checks back I’ll see a free turn and re‑evaluate. Check now to keep options and minimize losing more with a weak hand.

I have Ace-King offsuit on a board of Ts-3s-Jh. While I haven't paired up, I have two overcards and the Ace of spades gives me the nut flush backdoor draw. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will put out a continuation bet of 400 (50% of the pot). This bet serves to take down the pot immediately against hands that missed, while also pricing in any draws Minni might be holding. If she calls, I have plenty of equity to improve on the turn.

I have 9♦6♣ and completely missed the T♠ 3♠ J♥ flop — no spade and no pair. I only have 6 outs (three 6s + three 9s) ≈24% to hit by the river. Flash bet 400 into a 1200 pot; calling 400 would win 1600 so break-even equity = 400/1600 = 25%. My actual equity is slightly below that, and I’m out of position against an aggressive preflop raiser who will barrel good portions of his range. Even if I pair, I can still be behind two-pair/sets/overpairs or be denied value on later streets. I would consider calling a much smaller c‑bet to float, but facing this 400-sized bet I fold to avoid committing money to a marginal, often dominated spot.

Facing a preflop raise with 9♠4♠ on the button. This is a weak, low-suited connector with poor playability postflop against an opening raiser who has shown aggressive tendencies (Flash) and with a very short-stacked big blind (Claude) who can easily shove and create awkward multiway situations. Calling 100 into a 150 pot gives marginal pot odds but my hand has poor equity and little postflop fold equity; a light 3-bet/raise with 9♠4♠ would be overly exploitable. I’ll preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot — fold here.

I hold King-Queen offsuit in the small blind. Since the button (Minni) has folded, I am heads-up against the big blind (Claude), who is very short-stacked with only 1375 chips. KQo is a premium hand in this situation. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Claude and build a pot, as I likely have the best hand.

Analyzing Hand #101 from the big blind with A9o against Flash's small blind raise.

Flash raised to 300 from the small blind (only a 3BB raise), which indicates a wide range. I'm holding A9 of clubs, which is a solid hand in heads-up play - ace high with a reasonable kicker.

Stack considerations: I have 1375 chips and need to call 200 more, which is reasonable pot odds (2:1 into a 400 chip pot). After calling, I'll have 1175 chips remaining.

Given that:
1. A9 is reasonably strong in heads-up situations
2. Flash's raise was relatively small (suggests a wide range)
3. I'm getting favorable odds to call
4. I'm at hand 101 of 150, so I have room to play hands appropriately

I should call and see the flop. If the board is favorable, I can play aggressively. If not, I can reassess on the flop with my remaining stack.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on a relatively dry board. My opponent, Claude, is very short-stacked with only 875 chips remaining. I will place a continuation bet of 300 to extract value from any middle pairs, draws, or weaker Queens, and to simplify the decision-making process for the rest of the hand given the stack sizes.

Calling with A9 on Qs 8d 5h. Flash's moderate flop bet into a 900 chip pot suggests he could have many hands (Q, pair, draw, c-bet). My hand has legitimate outs (gutshot, overcards) and I'm getting 3:1 odds. Raising would commit most of my remaining 875 chips on a marginal hand. Calling lets me see the turn with good position and make a better-informed decision. Stack depth (1,175) makes a call appropriate rather than a fold or all-in raise.

I currently hold top pair with a King kicker (Qh Kc) on a board of Qs 8d 5h 5c. The turn card, a second 5, is unlikely to have improved Claude's hand given he called a pre-flop raise and a flop bet. Claude is extremely short-stacked with only 875 chips remaining, while the pot is 1200. I will bet 875 to put Claude all-in, as I am heavily favored to have the best hand and want to extract the maximum value from his remaining chips.

Folding A9o on the turn after Flash's aggressive raise to 875.

Analysis:
- Current situation: Flash raised to 875 on the turn after the board paired (Qs 8d 5h 5c)
- My hand: A9 is marginal at best here
- The 5 on the turn is unlikely to have helped me and likely improved Flash's range significantly
- Pot odds: I need to call 875 to win 2950, giving me 3.37:1 odds, requiring ~23% equity
- My equity assessment: Against Flash's range (likely Q, 8, 5, or a draw), I probably have 18-24% equity at best

Decision rationale:
1. This is a marginal situation with my remaining stack at 875 chips
2. Calling would commit nearly all my chips with a weak hand
3. I'm at hand 101/150, giving me plenty of opportunities for better spots
4. Flash's turn raise suggests strength; the pair on board likely favors his range
5. Long-term poker strategy favors folding marginal hands when near-stacked

Better to preserve chips and find stronger spots in the remaining 49 hands.

I am on the button with 8-5 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Claude, the small blind, is extremely short-stacked with only 8.25 big blinds remaining. If I raise to steal, I am very likely to face an all-in shove from Claude, and 8-5 offsuit does not have enough equity to comfortably call a shove even at those odds. Additionally, Minni has a massive stack and has been playing aggressively. Folding is the most prudent play to avoid unnecessary variance with a trash hand.

With a critically short stack of 825 chips and a weak hand like Jc5o (unsuited, unpaired, weak kicker), folding is the correct play. Although pot odds look favorable on the surface (3:1), my hand has very limited equity and postflop playability against Minni's big blind. With such limited chips remaining, I should preserve my stack for premium hands where I have genuine equity. Jc5o will likely miss the flop and put me in a difficult spot if Minni bets.

4-7 offsuit is too weak to play even from the button in a short-stack situation. With 825 chips (8.25BB), I need to preserve my stack for premium hands where I can be a stronger aggressor. Against Minni's large stack and Flash's medium stack, this hand plays poorly post-flop. Folding maintains my chip stack for better opportunities in the remaining 47 hands.

Holding 3♠6♦ in the small blind is a very weak, unsuited, low hand and I’ll be out of position post‑flop against Flash (deep and historically aggressive). The pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to continue, but this hand has poor playability and real EV is negative OOP — calling or min‑raising to 200 would put me in difficult spots or get called/3‑bet. Better to preserve stack and fold the small blind (forfeit the 50) and wait for a much stronger spot.

Three-handed, I’m on the button with 8h6d offsuit. It’s a weak hand but in position and facing only the blinds it’s a standard spot to open a wide range. Claude (BB) is extremely short (725) so raising here gives me fold equity and can either pick up the blinds or isolate the short stack who may shove or fold. Limping/calling 100 surrenders position and invites the SB to see a cheap flop. I’ll open to a standard 3x-ish size (300 total) — big enough to apply pressure and discourage cheap calls, small relative to my deep stack, and consistent with exploitative play in this spot. If Claude shoves I’ll re-evaluate (86o has poor equity vs a shove and I’ll likely fold), but open-raising maximizes my expected value from the button here.

I am in the Small Blind with 6s 2s. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300. The Big Blind (Claude) is extremely short-stacked with only 725 chips total. If I call, I risk a shove from Claude, and 62s is a very weak hand that does not play well out of position or in multi-way pots. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack.

Short-stacked with 725 chips at big blind. With J5o, I face a 200 call into a 650 pot (3.25:1 odds). Though J5 is weak, it has approximately 30-35% equity against Minni's button raising range and I need only ~24% equity to call profitably. More importantly, my stack size dictates I need to accumulate chips, and folding marginal hands only accelerates my blind decay. In heads-up against a button raiser, J5 has playable equity. Calling keeps me alive while maintaining fold equity on the flop if I miss.

With a short stack of 525 chips and having flopped a pair of 5s with a Jack kicker on 3h-As-5d, I'm in a critical situation. The pot stands at 650, and I've already committed 200 pre-flop from button aggressor Minni. My pair of 5s is a made hand with a reasonable kicker, which is strong relative to my stack size and the dynamic at this stage. Given my short-stack status, pot commitment is a major factor - folding marginal-to-decent hands accelerates my blind decay. Going all-in accomplishes several things: (1) I control the action rather than allowing Minni to dictate terms with a bet; (2) I give myself fold equity if Minni was bluffing or has a weaker hand; (3) If called, my pair likely has decent equity against Minni's wide button-raising range; (4) I'm committing a stack that's already close to pot odds anyway. Against Minni's likely range (which includes many hands given button positioning), my pair has approximately 40%+ equity, well above the 33% threshold I need to break even on this all-in. With hand 104 of 150, I need to accumulate chips now rather than slowly bleed out. This is the aggressive, stack-building play.

Claude (very short) effectively shoved his remaining ~525 on the A♠-3♥-5♦ flop after calling my preflop raise. I hold 8♥6♦ — no pair and no realistic draw (only a remote runner‑runner straight). The pot is 1,175 and I must call 525, so I need about 525/(1,175+525) ≈ 30.9% equity to break even. Against a short‑stack shove range that includes any Ace and many pocket pairs, my equity is far below that threshold. As the chip leader in a three‑handed game, this is a clear fold — preserve the stack and pick better spots.

I am on the button with Ace-Queen offsuit, which is a very strong premium hand in a 3-handed game. Claude in the Small Blind is extremely short-stacked with only 1,125 chips (about 11 big blinds). I should raise here to extract value and potentially isolate Claude. If Claude shoves his remaining stack, I will certainly call. Minni is the chip leader and plays aggressively, but AQo is well ahead of her general defending range. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate.

Folding J6o in the small blind facing a raise from Flash. Despite being short-stacked at 1125 chips, this hand is too weak to commit additional chips to. J6o has poor equity against Flash's raising range and would be out of position post-flop. Better to preserve my short stack for situations where I can get all-in with premium cards (pairs, AK, AQ, etc.).

Flash opened to 300 on the button. I’m in the big blind with A♦6♥. Calling costs me 200 more (300 total) into a 450 pot — I’m getting ~30.8% pot odds, and A6o has reasonable equity vs a typical button opening range heads-up. I’m out of position, so a light 3‑bet bluff is unattractive (it’ll often be called and I’d have to play a bigger pot OOP). With deep stacks I prefer to see a flop and play post‑flop hands for value or as situational bluffs. Plan: call to 300, fold to a large 3‑bet, and on the flop proceed depending on texture (value/defend top pair, check/fold or check‑call on dry boards, look for opportunities to bluff when appropriate).

Flop is 5♦-4♠-5♠. I hold A♦6♥ — just Ace-high with no spade and no meaningful draw. Flash opened the button preflop, so his range is wide (lots of overcards, small pairs, and some 5x/4x combos), but I have very little fold equity and no turn equity to speak of if called. Out of position and with only showdown value, I want pot control: check and see what Flash does. Plan: call small C-bets to realize equity (and re-evaluate on the turn), but fold to large, polarized bets. Checking keeps the pot manageable and preserves my stack for better spots.

On a paired board of 5d-4s-5s, I have Ace-high with AQo. This is a standard spot for a continuation bet after being the pre-flop aggressor. The board is relatively dry, and while it doesn't improve my hand, it likely missed a significant portion of Minni's defending range in the Big Blind. By betting, I can fold out her 'air' and deny equity to hands like K-J or T-9 that have six outs against me. I will bet 300 into the 650 pot, which is slightly less than half-pot, to keep the price for a fold reasonable while still exerting pressure.

Call 300. Flash opened wide on the button and c-bet 300 into a 650 pot (pot now 950). I have A♦6♥ — only Ace-high with no spade and no real draw. Calling 300 into 950 gives ~24% immediate pot odds and my chance to improve to at least a pair by the river (3 Aces + 3 sixes = ~6 outs) is about 24%, so this is a marginal but acceptable call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable out of position. A bluff-raise OOP is unattractive (limited fold equity, likely called and I’d face a larger OOP pot); folding is too passive given the odds. Plan: call, see a turn, and re-evaluate — fold to large, polarized aggression; if I hit an Ace I’ll look to extract value, otherwise control the pot.

I’m on Ad6h against a button opener who c-bet the flop; board is 5♦-4♠-5♠-J♥. I only have ace-high with no spade and only ~6 outs (3 Aces + 3 sixes ≈ 24% to pair by river). After calling the flop I have very little fold equity OOP and the turn Jack didn’t improve me. Best play is pot control: check and see what Flash does. If he fires a small turn bet I can call to realize my equity; if he barrels large and polarized I’ll fold. Bluff-raising or betting for thin value OOP here is unnecessary and commits me to a bigger pot with a weak hand.

The turn is a Jack of Hearts. Minni has checked to me for the second time. While the Jack is an overcard, it is also a card that could have easily improved Minni's range if she was floating with hands like J-T, Q-J, or K-J. Since I still only have Ace-high and the board is paired, I'm happy to check back here to control the pot size and see a free river. Ace-high still has some showdown value against her missed draws (like the spade flush draw), and I have outs to an Ace or Queen if she has a pair of Jacks or Fours. Checking allows me to keep the pot manageable against the chip leader.

Check. River (5♦-4♠-5♠-J♥-T♦) gives me only ace‑high (A♦6♥) with no backdoor suits or meaningful showdown improvement. Flash opened wide preflop and c‑bet the flop (I called) but checked the turn and river — a line consistent with missed hands or medium strength that’s unwilling to build a pot. Betting as a bluff here OOP has very thin fold equity and will often be called by any pair or two pair; I should control the pot and take the free showdown. Plan: check to see showdown; if Flash now bets a small probe I’ll consider a call depending on sizing, but fold to medium/large polarized bets since my hand has virtually no value.

On the river board of 5-4-5-J-T, I still hold Ace-Queen high. Minni has checked to me for the third time (flop, turn, and river). While I was the pre-flop and flop aggressor, the turn and river cards (Jack and Ten) are in the range of hands Minni might defend with pre-flop or float with on the flop. However, my Ace-high has significant showdown value against her missed draws, such as the spade flush draw or straight draws (6-7, 2-3). Betting here is unlikely to get a call from a hand I beat, and better hands like a pair of Jacks, Tens, or Fours are unlikely to fold. Checking back is the most prudent play to realize my equity and win the pot at showdown.

TJ suited is a playable hand from the button. With a short stack (1,125), committing more would be overcommitting. Calling 100 to see a flop is prudent - the hand has decent equity and I'm in position. Pot odds are favorable at 2.5:1.

Facing a limp from a short-stacked Button (Claude ~10bb) with a deep, often-aggressive Big Blind behind (Flash ~133bb), 3h6h is a speculative, marginal hand and I'm out of position. Raising to isolate is unnecessary here — it risks a shove from Claude or a 3-bet from Flash and bloat the pot with a weak holding. Completing to 100 total (calling the extra 50) gives excellent pot odds (50 to win ~300, need ~16.7% equity) to see a cheap flop and look for heart/straight potential. I'll call and re-evaluate on the flop; fold to significant preflop/large postflop aggression since this hand plays poorly multiway OOP.

I have AcQc in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand. Both Claude (Button) and Minni (Small Blind) have limped into the pot. Claude is very short-stacked with only 1,025 chips remaining. I should raise here to build value and potentially isolate or force a decision from the short stack. A raise to 500 is a standard and strong sizing to punish the limpers and take control of the hand.

TJ suited is a strong holding for this spot. I'm on the button with position, and facing Flash's 500 raise from the BB. The pot is now 700 and I need 400 more, giving me 1.75:1 odds (36% equity needed). TJ suited has approximately 40-45% equity against Flash's likely 3-bet range, making this a profitable call. My position is crucial - I can react to any flop action and have fold equity with my short stack. While my remaining stack (525 after the call) would be tight, TJ has excellent playability post-flop with multiple draw possibilities (flush draw, straight draw, overcard potential). This is a spot where equitable, marginal hands should see the flop given position and stack-to-pot ratios.

Call 400. 3♥6♥ is a speculative, backdoor-heavy hand that plays poorly multiway and out of position, so isolating with a raise is unnecessary — it risks a shove from Claude (short ~6–7bb) or a 3-bet from Flash and bloats the pot with a weak holding. Calling 400 to see a flop keeps the pot controllable while giving me a chance to hit hearts, a pair, or a straight. Pot odds: calling 400 into the current 1,100 (pot becomes ~1,500) requires ~26.7% equity — reasonable against a wide opening range from the deep, aggressive BB and a limp/caller from Claude. Plan postflop: proceed only with real equity (pair/flush/strong draw); check-fold to heavy aggression on dry boards and look to realize equity or apply pressure only when appropriate. I will fold to significant preflop 3-bet or large postflop raises.

Flop: 5♠-4♥-K♦. I hold 3♥6♥ — no pair but a very strong open‑ended straight draw (3-4-5-6 needing a 2 or 7; 8 outs, ~31.5% to hit by the river) and a tiny backdoor heart chance. This is a three‑way pot and I'm out of position vs a deep, aggressive BB (Flash) and a short‑stacked Button (Claude). I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloating it OOP with a draw, so I check. Plan: call reasonable c-bets (small to ~half‑pot) to see the turn where my straight can materialize; fold to large, polarizing bets/raises from the deep player. If Claude (the short) jams a small all‑in (~625) I will call — pot odds (~29%) are roughly in line with my ~31% straight equity — but I will be cautious about any further big action from Flash.

I was the pre-flop aggressor and the board came King-high and relatively dry. This is a standard situation to continuation bet to represent the King. Claude is very short-stacked with only 625 chips remaining, so a bet of 600 effectively puts him all-in. I want to put pressure on Minni and potentially isolate Claude or take the pot down now. Even if I am called, I still have two overcards (Ace and Queen) which could be the best hand or provide outs on later streets.

T♥J♥ on 5♠4♥K♦ is a strong drawing hand with approximately 35-45% equity against Flash's likely range. With a pot of 2700 after my call, I only need 22.2% equity to break even. The hand offers multiple outs (10 hearts for flush draw, potential straight draws with 3 or 6). Being short-stacked at 625 chips and needing to accumulate chips to survive, calling with this equity is the correct play. This commits me nearly all-in, but that's appropriate given the hand strength and pot odds.

Flop: 5♠-4♥-K♦, my hand 3♥6♥. I have an open‑ended straight draw to a 2 or 7 (8 outs, ≈31% to hit by the river) plus a tiny backdoor heart. Flash (deep, aggressive) made a relatively small C‑bet (600) and Claude (now effectively short/committed) called — this is effectively a heads‑up decision against Flash for the bulk of the pot. Pot = 2700, call = 600 → I need roughly 600/(2700+600) ≈ 18% equity to break even. My ~31% equity comfortably exceeds that, so a call is +EV and lets me realize equity without bloating the pot OOP. Raising would be risky — it bloats the pot vs a deep, aggressive opponent and turns a playable draw into a high-variance spot; folding is too tight given the pot odds. Plan: call to see the turn; if I hit the straight (2 or 7) I’ll extract value aggressively, if I miss and face a large, polarizing raise from Flash I’ll fold, otherwise continue selectively (check/call or semi-bluff only when appropriate).

Board 5♠-4♥-K♦-9♠, my hand 3♥6♥. I have an open-ended straight draw (need a 2 or 7) — 8 outs. With only the river left my chance to hit is 8/46 ≈ 17.4%. Pot is 3,300 and it’s checked to me, so there’s no reason to inflate the pot OOP against a deep, aggressive BB (Flash) with only ~17% equity. Leading or raising here is high-variance and unnecessary — I want to realize equity cheaply and keep control. Plan: check and see what Flash does. If he bets I’ll call small bets (break-even bet size ≈ 695 chips — B/(3,300+B) ≤ 17.4%), but fold to larger, polarizing turn bets. If Claude shoves his ~25 chips I’ll call (negligible risk / great pot odds). If checked through I’ll evaluate the river and extract value if I hit or fold to heavy river aggression when missed.

On the turn, the 9s is a blank for my hand. Minni has checked to me. Claude is effectively all-in with only 25 chips remaining. Since Minni called both my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, she likely has a piece of this board, probably a King or a middle pair. My Ace-high currently has some showdown value but is likely behind. By checking, I can see a free river card and potentially improve to the best hand without risking more chips against Minni's likely stronger range. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a King and would only serve to bloat the pot when I am likely trailing.

With 25 chips remaining on the turn, I'm betting all-in with T♥J♥ on a 5♠4♥K♦9♠ board. I have an open-ended straight draw (10-12 outs to make a straight with any Q or 8) plus two overcards, giving me approximately 25-35% equity. Both opponents checked the turn, suggesting relative weakness. Since I'm critically short-stacked, committing my final chips is correct - the pot odds are overwhelmingly favorable (3,300:25 ratio) and my hand has legitimate equity to improve on the river. This maintains fold equity against weaker hands and maximizes my tournament survival chances.

I call the 25. With 3♥6♥ on 5♠-4♥-K♦-9♠ I have an open-ended straight draw (need a 2 or 7 – 8 outs ≈17.4% to hit the river). The price to see the river is trivial: pot ≈3,325 and I only need to call 25 (break-even equity ≈0.75%), so this is an easy +EV equity-realization call. Claude’s tiny raise looks like a short-stack/block action and Flash (deep, aggressive) checked the turn, so I want to keep the pot small and realize my equity rather than inflate the pot OOP. Plan: call to see the river; if I hit the straight I’ll extract value (bet or raise depending on sizing/read). If I miss and face a large, polarizing river bet from Flash I will fold; I’ll call or raise small river bets selectively based on the sizing and read.

Claude is all-in for a negligible amount of 25 chips. The pot is already very large (over 3,300 chips), so folding is out of the question as I am getting astronomical pot odds to call with my Ace-Queen high. Minni has been sticky throughout the hand, calling pre-flop and on the flop, suggesting she likely has a piece of the board, potentially a King. By simply calling the 25, I can see the river card for the minimum cost and potentially improve to an Ace or Queen, or win at showdown if my hand is currently best against Claude's short-stack range and Minni's range if she is drawing or has a weak pair.

River: 5♠-4♥-K♦-9♠-5♦. I hold 3♥6♥ — I completely missed my open‑ended straight (needed a 2 or 7). I have no pair and no legitimate showdown equity except as a pure bluff. Pot is 3,375 and the only live opponent is Flash (deep, sticky, has shown willingness to call/catch down). To bluff profitably I’d need meaningful fold equity (e.g. a 1,000 bet needs ~23% folds); against this opponent and line (raised/c-bet on flop, checked turn) I expect too many hands will call. With a large stack and 44 hands left in the tournament, I’ll avoid a high-variance bluff — check and preserve chips. Plan: check now; fold to any river bet (I have essentially 0% showdown equity).

On the river, the board pairs with a second five. Claude is already all-in for a tiny amount (25 chips). Minni has checked to me on the river after calling throughout the hand. My Ace-high (AcQc) has showdown value against Claude's range, but is likely behind any pair or King in Minni's range. Since Minni checked, there is no incentive to bet as a bluff, as she is likely to call with any marginal hand given the pot odds, nor can I bet for value. Checking allows me to see the showdown for the main pot and side pot without committing further chips.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦J♦ — a very playable, aggressive heads-up hand (suited broadway) with good equity and postflop playability. I want to take the initiative, deny a free flop, and extract value from the big blind’s wide defending range. Flash has shown a tendency to defend/call fairly often from the BB, so a standard 2x raise is appropriate: big enough to build the pot and price drawing hands, but small enough to keep weaker hands in and preserve deep-stack postflop maneuvering. Plan: if called, c‑bet frequently on favorable flops and use positional advantage to apply pressure; if facing a large 3‑bet, re-evaluate (fold/discard marginal hands). Raise to 200 total.

In this heads-up situation, I'm holding 8c 6c in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind, which is a standard 2x open. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 3-to-1 pot odds. 8-6 suited is a solid hand to defend with in a heads-up match, as it has good playability and can flop straight and flush draws or mid-pairs. Given the pot odds and the hand's potential, calling is the most standard and effective play here.

In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first on all post-flop streets. I have 8c 6c on a 4s 7s 3c board, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (to the five). While I have some equity, leading out into the pre-flop raiser on this texture is generally less effective than checking to see their reaction. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's bet size and my potential to improve on the turn.

Heads‑up I opened to 200 with Q♦J♦ and the flop is a very dry 4♠‑7♠‑3♣. As the preflop aggressor I have a strong initiative and many of BB’s defending hands (weak aces, low pairs, Kx, single overcards) miss this flop. This is an ideal c‑bet spot to fold out those holdings and to extract value from worse cards that will call (Qx/Jx, Kx, mid pairs). Pot is 400 — I’ll make a standard ~60% pot continuation bet (250): big enough to generate fold equity and build the pot when ahead, but small enough to keep worse hands and draws in. Plan: if called, re‑evaluate on the turn and continue barreling on safe, non‑spade, non‑Q/J runouts; if the turn brings a spade or heavy action (raise/3‑bet), I will tighten up and likely give up with QJ unless I improve. Stack depths are deep enough to maneuver postflop, so this size preserves flexibility.

On this 4s-7s-3c flop, I have 8c 6c, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5). Minni has made a continuation bet of 250 into a pot of 400. This is a standard sized bet. I am getting 650:250 or 2.6-to-1 odds to call. In a heads-up game, defending with a gutshot and two overcards to the board (8 and 6) is standard, especially since I have backdoor possibilities. I will call the bet and look to improve on the turn, or potentially bluff if the board texture changes in a way that favors my range.

On the turn, the 6s improves my hand to a middle pair (pair of sixes). However, the board has become significantly more dangerous as three spades are now present, completing potential flushes. Additionally, any 5 in Minni's range has completed a straight. Despite my improvement, leading into the pre-flop aggressor who also bet the flop is generally not advisable on this texture. Checking allows me to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to the third spade. If she bets, I will have to evaluate her sizing and the likelihood that she has a flush or a better made hand before deciding to continue.

Check. I opened preflop with Q♦J♦ and c‑bet the dry 4♠‑7♠‑3♣ flop (250 into ~400) which was the right line. Flash’s call on the flop keeps a very wide range here — small pairs, weak Kx/Qx/Jx, single spade hands and importantly two‑spade combos that already had a flush on the flop. The turn 6♠ is a clear danger card: it completes any two‑spade holdings and generally makes my unpaired QJ a marginal hand with almost no showdown value and very poor equity versus the portion of villain’s range that called the flop. Betting the turn would be a thin bluff with limited fold equity and high reverse implied risk (I can easily run into a made flush or a pair that now calls or raises). With deep stacks and only two players I prefer pot control — check to avoid bloating the pot, see what Flash does, and reevaluate on the river. If Flash checks back I get a free river card; if Flash bets I will fold to a meaningful bet and only consider calling very small probes to realize showdown equity. Given the board texture, my lack of spades or a pair, and Flash’s calling tendencies, checking is the prudent line.

The river brings a fourth spade to the board (Js), meaning any single spade in Minni's hand now makes a flush. I have a pair of sixes, but with four spades visible, my hand has very little showdown value. Given that Minni checked the turn, she might not have a high spade, but the risk of her holding any spade is significant in heads-up play. Leading out as a bluff is risky because she could easily call with a small spade or a better pair. Checking is the most prudent play to see if we can get to a cheap showdown or to fold if she makes a significant bet.

I improved on the river — the Js pairs my Jd, giving me top pair with a Q kicker. The board has three spades (4s,7s,6s), so flushes beat me, but Flash’s line (calling my flop c‑bet then checking the turn) makes a turned flush less likely and instead points to medium showdown hands (small pairs, one‑pair, missed draws). With position and a likely calling range of worse one‑pair hands, a value bet is correct: I size to ~½ pot to extract calls from second pairs and weaker one‑pair hands without bloating the pot against the portion of his range that contains spades. If I face a large raise I’ll relinquish the hand, but a half‑pot bet should maximize value when called and fold out pure air.

The river has brought a fourth spade to the board, making any single spade in Minni's hand a flush. I hold a pair of sixes (8c 6c) with no spade blocker. Minni bet 450 into a 900 pot (offering me 3:1 pot odds). While Minni's check on the turn could suggest she didn't have a flush then, the appearance of the fourth spade on the river is an ideal bluffing card for her. However, in a heads-up scenario, the likelihood of her holding at least one spade in her range is quite high. Even if she is bluffing, my pair of sixes is extremely vulnerable to many of her 'air' hands that might have improved or simply contain a higher pair. Given the board texture, calling without a spade is very risky, and preserving my stack is more important in this high-roller format. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Ten-Five offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. While I would have position post-flop as the dealer/small blind, the hand's equity is low, and Minni currently holds a significant chip lead. I will fold to conserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button with Q♣ J♠ — a strong, playable hand in this spot. I’ve already posted the SB (50) and the pot is 150 with 50 to call. Against a single opponent with deep stacks, I want to seize initiative and build a pot with position. A standard 3x BB open to 300 total achieves that: it denies cheap odds to speculative hands, applies pressure, and lets me play postflop last. I avoid the tiny min-raise (200) which invites too many callers and cheap flops from the BB. If Flash 3-bets I can still fold or continue depending on sizing; with a call I’d be giving him the initiative. Therefore I raise to 300 total.

In this heads-up situation, Minni has raised to 3x the big blind. While I am getting 2:1 odds to call with 4h 5s, this hand has very poor playability out of position. Given the stack sizes and the aggressive nature of the opponent shown in previous hands, I will fold and wait for a hand with better post-flop potential to defend my big blind.

I am in the Small Blind with 5-3 offsuit, which is a very weak hand. Although it only costs 50 more to call, the hand is near the bottom of my range and Minni has shown significant aggression recently. To preserve my stack for better opportunities in this heads-up phase, I will fold.

Heads-up on the button with A♥8♠ — a strong, positionally advantaged hand against a very wide BB range. Limping/calling 50 is too passive (gives the BB good odds and initiative postflop).
