I want to take the initiative, build a pot with a lot of dominated hands in his calling range (we beat many worse aces and broadways) and charge draws. Raising to 300 (≈3x the BB) makes the pot ~400 before he acts and gives us fold equity while keeping postflop playability. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (A8o is often fold vs a large 3-bet, but I’ll call smaller 3-bets to play position). Raise to 300 to maximize EV here.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Seven offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind (the button in this heads-up situation). While Q7o is a frequent defense in heads-up play, a 3x open from a player who has shown consistent post-flop aggression makes defending out of position difficult. Given the stack sizes and the desire to find better spots to commit chips, I will fold and wait for a stronger starting hand or a better position.

I am in the Small Blind/Button position with 9s 6d. This is a relatively weak hand. In heads-up play, while many hands are playable from the button due to post-flop position, 96-offsuit is at the bottom of the range. Given Minni's frequent aggression and the current tournament stage, I prefer to fold this hand and look for more profitable opportunities, especially since I am trailing in chips and want to avoid unnecessary losses with marginal holdings.

Heads-up on the button (SB) with Kc Kh — a premium pair. Stacks are very deep (~250bb), so I want to take the initiative and build the pot while isolating the big blind. A standard raise to ~3x the BB (300 total) extracts value from worse aces and broadways, prices out some speculative hands, and keeps us in position postflop. Folding or just completing to 50 is too passive; I’ll open to 300 and re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets (call or 4-bet depending on sizing/read).

I am holding Ace-Eight of spades in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni, who has a significant chip lead, has raised from the Button. While a 3-bet is a strong option to take the lead, I choose to call to keep the pot controlled and realize my equity, as I am out of position for the rest of the hand. This hand is well ahead of Minni's typical opening range, and I can comfortably play most flops.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor flush draw on a board that likely favors Minni's range (containing broadway cards and straight draws). Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni reacts. If she bets, I will re-evaluate based on the sizing, but for now, checking allows me to control the pot size out of position.

I’m heads-up as the preflop aggressor with KK on a J♦ T♦ 2♦ flop. This is a clean overpair to the board; villain’s defending range from the BB will include many worse hands (Axs, Kx, Jx, Tx, small pairs) plus some diamond and straight draws. I want value and to protect against those draws, so I’ll c-bet about half-pot to charge draws and extract calls from worse pairs and broadways while keeping the pot controlled with deep stacks. Bet 300 into 600. Plan: if called, continue evaluating turn — barrel non-diamond, non-scary cards for value; if I face a large check-raise I’ll re-evaluate (call most reasonable raises to probe, fold only to highly polarized overbets or clear turn/river monsters).

On a board of Jack-Ten-Deuce with two diamonds, I have Ace-high and a backdoor nut flush draw with the Ace of spades. Minni has bet 300 into a 600-chip pot, giving me 3-to-1 odds to call. While this board favors the pre-flop raiser's range, Ace-high still has reasonable showdown value and several ways to improve on the turn, including an Ace, an Eight, or any spade to pick up a flush draw. Folding is too conservative given the price, and raising is unnecessary as I want to keep the pot manageable while out of position. I will call to see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the Queen of Spades significantly improves my hand's potential, providing me with the nut flush draw and a gutshot to the nut straight (needing a King). Since I am out of position against the chip leader, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how Minni reacts to the board texture, which now contains multiple straight and flush possibilities. If she bets, I have more than enough equity to call and attempt to realize my draws on the river; if she checks behind, I get a free card to hit my hand.

I opened preflop and c‑bet the flop, so I’m the aggressor with a very strong overpair (KK). The turn Q♠ does give my opponent some Qx two‑pair/ top‑pair combos that beat me, but Flash checked, which indicates more often weakness or marginal hands (Jx, Tx, medium pairs, single‑diamond/paired draws, or floats). I want value from those hands and to charge diamonds/backdoor draws while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks, so I’ll bet a standard half‑pot to extract calls from worse one‑pair hands and force some thin hands/draws to fold. If I face a large check‑raise I’ll re‑evaluate (call small/medium raises to probe, fold to highly polarized overbets or shove attempts).

On the turn, the Queen of Spades has significantly increased my equity. I now have the nut flush draw (7 remaining spades) and a gutshot to the nut straight (4 Kings). With 11 outs to the nuts, I have approximately 24% equity. Minni has bet 600 into a 1200 pot, meaning I need to call 600 to win a total pot of 1800 (offering me 3-to-1 odds). My immediate pot odds (25%) are almost exactly in line with my drawing equity, and the implied odds are excellent if I hit my hand on the river against a chip leader who has been playing aggressively. Calling is the most mathematically sound and disciplined play here to see a river card.

On the river, the Seven of Hearts is a complete brick for my hand. I have missed both my nut flush draw (spades) and my gutshot straight draw (King). The board is extremely coordinated (Jack, Ten, Queen), meaning that much of Minni's calling and betting range from previous streets has now improved to at least a pair or a straight (AK or K9). Since I am out of position and have no showdown value with Ace-high on this board, betting would be an ill-advised bluff against a chip leader who has shown consistent aggression. Checking is the only logical play; I will likely have to fold if Minni bets, as my hand cannot beat anything but a total air bluff, which is unlikely given the board texture.

I have KK heads‑up on J♠ T♦ 2♦ Q♠ 7♥. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet/fired the turn; villain called both streets and checked the river. Villain’s continuing range is wide (Jx, Tx, Qx, A‑high, medium pairs, missed diamond draws) — almost all of which I beat. Only realistic hands that beat me are JT (two pair) or sets (JJ/TT/22), which are relatively few and would often have played more aggressively earlier. Given the board and the river check, I should extract value. I’ll bet about half the pot (~1200) to get called by top‑pair and pocket‑pair hands and charge stubborn missed draws. If I face a significant check‑raise on the river it’s likely a very strong holding (two‑pair/trips) and I’ll fold to large/overtly polarized raises.

The river card (7 of hearts) is a complete brick for my hand, as I have missed both my nut flush draw and my gutshot straight draw. I am left with Ace-high on an extremely coordinated board (Jack, Ten, Queen, Seven). Minni has shown aggression on every street, and her 1,200 bet into a 2,400 pot suggests she has at least a pair or a straight (AK, K9, or 98). Given that this board texture hits a standard opening range very well and I have no showdown value, calling with Ace-high against a triple-barrel is mathematically unsound. I will fold and preserve my remaining 12,425 chips for a better opportunity.

In a heads-up situation, holding King-high on the button is a strong enough hand to warrant a raise. I want to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot early. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to test Minni's defense.

I'm in the big blind with J♥7♣ facing a button open to 300. This is a marginal hand but heads-up versus a likely wide opening range it's playable. Pot is 400 and I must call 200 more (total committed 300) — that gives me to win a 600 pot and requires ~33% equity to break even. The cost is small relative to my deep stack (26,275) and the opponent (13,325), so it's a low-risk way to see a flop. I'm out of position postflop, so I don't want to 3-bet light (min-raise to 500 is available but unnecessary with this hand). Plan: call to see the flop; if I hit a jack or a strong draw I'll continue for value or leverage; on dry boards I'll exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression. Calling keeps the pot manageable and preserves future maneuvering.

I’m out of position with J♥7♣ on a dry 10♦-6♣-5♥ flop. I have one overcard (the J) and three jacks + three sevens = 6 immediate outs (~24% to pair by river); runner-runner straights are negligible. The board favors the preflop raiser’s c-bet, and stacks are deep so there’s no need to force the action now. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me see how Flash plans to proceed, and preserves the option to call a reasonable c-bet or fold to heavy aggression. Plan: check; call small-to-medium continuation bets (1/3–1/2 pot) to realize equity, fold to very large bets that commit too much with only 6 outs, and if I hit a pair or pick up additional equity on later streets I’ll proceed for value or consider a raise.

I have middle pair (sixes) with a King kicker on a relatively dry board of 10-6-5. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet is standard here to protect my hand and extract value from drawing hands like 78 or lower pairs. A bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard sizing to maintain pressure.

Call 300. Flash’s flop bet is small (≈1/3 pot) on a very dry 10-6-5 board; I have ~6 clean outs (any J or 7) for roughly 24% to hit by the river. The pot is 900 and calling 300 gives a break-even equity of about 300/1500 = 20%, so this is +EV to realize equity. I’m out of position and stacks are deep, so I want to keep the pot controlled and see a turn — I’ll proceed for value if I pair or pick up additional equity, and fold or concede to very large aggression if I don’t improve. A small call preserves maneuverability and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I’ve turned top pair (J♥7♣) on a fairly dry 10♦-6♣-5♥-J♠ board heads-up versus the preflop raiser. Flash’s small flop aggression is consistent with a wide range that includes tens, weaker pairs, some Jx, and a few straight draws (notably 7-8). Out of position I prefer to bet for value and to deny/charge draws rather than check back and give a free river card. I size to ~1/2 pot (600 into 1200): big enough to get called by worse (T‑x, second pairs, draws) and to fold some pure floats, but small enough to control the pot against a possible check-raise or big shove. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll re-evaluate the river and continue for value on safe runouts; if Flash raises large I’ll give credit to a very strong holding and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing.

On the turn, the Jack is a significant overcard that likely improves many hands in Minni's big blind calling range, such as JT, QJ, or KJ. When Minni leads out (donk bets) for 600 into the 1200 pot after check-calling the flop, it often indicates she has either improved to a pair of Jacks or is betting a Ten for protection against the overcard. My pair of sixes is now third pair and very vulnerable. While the pot odds are 4:1, calling is risky because I will likely face another bet on the river and have very few outs (only three Kings and two Sixes) if I am behind. Given the tournament context and Minni's aggressive tendencies to follow through on later streets, folding here is the most disciplined way to preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button with pocket 3s and very deep stacks — this is a good spot to open for value and fold equity. The BB defends widely here, so a standard 3x-ish open will either take the pot preflop or put us in position postflop with a small pair and good implied odds to hit a set. Min-raise is 200, so I’ll raise to 300 total (3bb) to isolate and deny free cards while keeping the pot manageable. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold against a big 3-bet); if called I’ll play in position — c-bet favorable flops and check/slow-play or set-mine on dry boards.

I am holding KQs in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been opening frequently from the button. Given the strength of my hand, I want to raise for value and take initiative rather than just calling and playing out of position. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard size that allows me to build a pot with a high-equity hand or take it down immediately.

I opened to 300 with pocket 3s and Flash 3‑bet to 1000. With effective stacks (~12k) and position postflop, this is a clear set‑mining spot — calling preserves implied odds to hit a set while avoiding bloating the pot with a weak pair. 4‑betting as a bluff or for thin value with 33 is unattractive; folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: call (total 1000). If I flop a set I’ll extract value; on dry low flops I can continue or check‑raise; on high/connected boards I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy aggression.

On a flop of 6h-Jc-8d, I am currently holding King-high with two overcards (K and Q). As the pre-flop 3-bettor, I have a range advantage that includes overpairs and strong Jacks. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 700 (35% of the pot) to maintain initiative, represent a strong range, and potentially force folds from hands that missed or smaller pocket pairs. This size allows me to exert pressure while keeping the pot manageable given that I only have overcards.

Flop: 6h Jc 8d. I hold 3♦3♠ — an underpair. Flash 3‑bet pre and now fires a very small probe (700 into a ~2700 pot ~25%); this is exactly the kind of spot to set‑mine rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Calling 700 preserves my implied odds to hit a set (by river ~8.4%) and keeps the pot manageable against a 3‑bettor who can be c‑betting a wide range. Raising is thin (I’m behind most of his value range and would just inflate the pot) and folding surrenders too much equity for a cheap price. Plan: call the 700 to see the turn; if I hit a set I’ll extract maximum value, if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy turn aggression.

The turn Ten of Diamonds is a highly coordinated card that likely connects well with Minni's pre-flop and flop calling range, which can include hands like J-T, T-9, or even 9-7 for a straight. My K-Q now has a gutshot straight draw (to the nut straight if a 9 falls) and two overcards, but I am currently holding only King-high. Against a range that has likely improved, checking is the best play to control the pot and attempt to realize my equity. If Minni checks back, I get a free look at the river; if she bets, I will evaluate the price offered to draw to my straight or overcards.

Flash checked the turn after c‑betting the flop, which reads like pot control/weakness. I have an underpair (33) with only 2 outs to a set on the river (2/46 ≈ 4.35%). The pot is ~3,400 and both stacks are deep, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. A small bet here will get called too often by Jx/Tx/overcards and a larger bet is reckless; raising is out of the question. I’ll check back, realize my showdown equity for free and see the river — if I hit a set I can extract value, if I miss I’ll be prepared to fold to significant aggression or consider a small river bluff only in the right spot.

The river Queen improves my hand to top pair with a King kicker. However, the board is highly coordinated, and any hand containing a 9 has now made a straight (8-9-T-J-Q). After the turn went check-check, it is possible that Minni was pot-controlling with a marginal hand or a draw. By checking the river, I am playing defensively against a potential straight while remaining in a position to check-call a bet to catch bluffs or value bets from weaker hands like a Jack or a Ten. This minimizes my risk on a dangerous board while still allowing me to reach a showdown with a relatively strong hand in a heads-up context.

I hold 3♦3♠ and never improved — an underpair on a 6♥ J♣ 8♦ T♦ Q♣ board. Flash 3‑bet preflop, c‑bet the flop, then checked turn and river: that line looks like pot control/medium strength (Jx/Tx/pair) or a check‑back trap with a strong pocket pair. Against a 3‑bettor I have very little fold equity on the river; a small bluff will be called by most of his calling range and a large shove is unnecessarily high variance for a thin chance to win. With the pot only 3,400 and no bet to call, the lowest‑risk, highest‑EV play is to check and realize showdown equity rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand.

In this heads-up situation, I'm on the button/small blind with Ten-Deuce offsuit. While heads-up strategy often dictates playing a wide range of hands from the button, T2o is one of the weakest possible starting hands. Given Minni's aggressive history in the big blind, defending or raising with this hand is likely to lead to a difficult post-flop situation where I'm at a significant range disadvantage. With only 35 hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial chip deficit, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a more favorable opportunity to accumulate chips rather than bleeding chips on low-equity holdings.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 6h2s is a weak holding, but in heads-up play you should open very wide. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 total to seize the initiative, apply pressure on the BB and use my positional advantage post-flop. The raise is small relative to our 25k stack so it preserves chips while maximizing fold equity — if Flash folds I pick up the blinds; if he calls I have position to c-bet on favorable textures or check/control the pot on bad boards; if he 3-bets large I can fold and wait for a better spot. Raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Trey offsuit. Minni opened with a minimum raise to 200 (a 100-chip increase for me). Given the pot odds of 3:1 and the fact that King-high has decent equity against a wide button-opening range, I will call to see a flop. I need to be careful post-flop given the weak kicker, but a fold would be too tight against a 2x raise.

On a flop of 7h-6d-Tc, I have missed the board entirely, holding only King-high. This board texture is quite connected and likely favors the range of the pre-flop aggressor or contains cards that hit a standard button opening range. My strategy here is to check to the aggressor to see how they react. Leading out into this board with only overcards is too risky out of position.

I have middle pair (6♥) with a very weak kicker on a 7♥-6♦-T♣ flop in heads-up. I was the preflop aggressor so I should continue to apply pressure — Flash’s check indicates a wide range that contains many missed overcards, some medium pairs, and some tens. My 6s are ahead of a lot of that range and I want to both get value from worse hands and fold out overcards that have equity. I’ll make a standard c-bet of about half to two‑thirds pot to charge draws and weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (check back or barrel small with improvement); if he check-raises large I will fold — my kicker is poor and I’m not willing to commit a big portion of my stack.

On a board of 7h-6d-Tc, I have missed entirely with my King-high. The board is quite connected, offering many potential draws and pairs for the pre-flop aggressor's range. While the pot odds are roughly 3.6:1, calling with only King-high and a weak kicker out of position is a losing play in the long run, as I will have little to no clarity on future streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Holding pocket sixes heads-up, I have a very strong starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 from the button to build the pot and take the initiative in the hand.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I’m in the Big Blind with 2♦9♥ — a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand that plays badly out of position. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (need ≈33% equity) but 2-9o is frequently dominated by the opener’s range and has poor postflop playability against likely c-bets. A tiny min 3‑bet to 500 wouldn’t fold enough of their range and a larger 3‑bet is a bluff I’d rather use with blockers (A/K) or better equity. With deep stacks and plenty of hands left to play, folding and preserving my stack is the +EV line.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening very wide. 3♠7♣ is a very weak hand, but with deep stacks and positional advantage I prefer to take the initiative and try to steal the BB rather than passively limp. The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but a min-raise to 200 (standard 2x BB here) buys fold equity and keeps the pot controllable. Plan: min-raise to 200; if Flash 3-bets significantly I’ll fold this hand, if he calls I’ll play postflop in position, c-betting selectively on favorable textures and checking back on dry, unconnected boards.

I am in the big blind with 8c 3d, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised from the small blind to 200, making it 100 more for me to call into a pot of 300. Given the weak nature of my hand and the fact that I would be out of position for the rest of the hand, it is most prudent to fold and wait for a better opportunity, especially since I have a comfortable stack and there are still hands left in the tournament.
Holding Ace-Deuce offsuit on the button in a heads-up scenario, this is a standard opening hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure and take control of the pot early.

Heads-up in the big blind with pocket 5s facing a standard button open to 300. This is a clear defend — deep stacks and a small open mean Flash's range is wide. I prefer to call and see a flop rather than thin 3‑bet (which would bloat the pot out of position and be awkward multi‑street). Plan: call to set-mine / play post‑flop; keep pot controlled on overcardly flops, value/raise big on sets or strong turn improvements, fold to heavy aggression if I never connect. Calling keeps weaker hands and bluffs in and preserves implied odds.

Heads‑up on a J‑6‑3 rainbow flop with pocket 5s — I’m an underpair and I called preflop to set‑mine. Checking is the right line: keep the pot small, allow Flash to barre l with a wide c‑bet range, and realize my equity (I beat many bluffs and small pairs while preserving implied odds to hit a 5). If Flash fires a small/medium bet I’ll call and re‑evaluate on the turn; if he jams or barrels big on later streets without giving me the draw or improvement I’ll fold. Betting here would bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand, so I check to control the size and follow my preflop plan.

I am holding Ace-high on a very dry board (6s Jd 3c). As the pre-flop aggressor, this board favors my range significantly more than Minni's, who merely called from the Big Blind. Since Minni has checked, I will fire a standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot). This allows me to take down the pot immediately if she missed, while representing a strong Jack or an overpair. If she calls, I still have some showdown value with my Ace and the potential to improve.

Call. Flash's flop bet is small (~1/3 pot) and I'm heads‑up with a medium pocket pair — this is textbook set‑mining/realize‑equity territory. The price is about 300 to win ~1,200 (≈20% required), and with deep effective stacks (≈13.9k) the implied odds justify seeing another street. Raising OOP would bloat the pot with a marginal hand; calling keeps worse hands and bluffs in and lets me extract maximum when I hit a 5 or he shows weakness. Plan: call the flop, reevaluate on the turn — fold to large, unambiguous pressure if I never improve; extract value or raise only with a set/clear strength.

Heads‑up on the button vs my big blind. I called pre and the flop c‑bet to set‑mine with 55; that was the plan. The turn pairs the board (6s Jd 3c 3d) and I still only have an underpair with just two outs (the two remaining 5s) — ~4.3% to improve on the river. Flash's flop raise was small and wide, so his range contains lots of air and single‑pair hands, but calling a sizable turn bet with only two outs is a losing line. Betting or check‑raising OOP with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and be exploitative in the wrong direction. Best play is to check and force Flash to show strength or bluff; if he makes a small turn bet I can call to see the river occasionally vs bluffs, but I will fold to large, committed bets. Plan: check now, re‑evaluate on his action — only continue with a clear read, cheap bet, or if I hit a 5 on the river.

On the turn, the board pairs the three (6s Jd 3c 3d). Minni has checked to me for a second time. My Ac 2s has improved to a pair of Threes with an Ace kicker. While I could represent a Jack with a second barrel, checking back allows me to control the pot size and realize my showdown value. If Minni is holding a Six or a weak Jack, she is unlikely to fold to one more bet, and I'd prefer not to be check-raised off my hand. By checking, I can see the river for free and potentially win the pot at showdown or evaluate based on her river action.

I have a weak underpair (55) on J-6-3-3-2. Villain made a small flop c-bet and then checked the turn, which looks like pot-control/weakness rather than a strong, value-heavy line. The river doesn’t improve me (only two outs) and brings a third diamond — possible but uncommon two-diamond flushes exist. Betting here for thin value is marginal (I’ll get called by many Jx/6x and fold to a raise), so the lowest-variance, most +EV line is to check and take it to showdown. I’ll check and evaluate only if Flash leads into me (fold to significant pressure).

On the river, the board is Jd 6s 3c 3d 2d. My hand is Ace-Deuce, which gives me two pair: Threes and Deuces with an Ace kicker (3-3-2-2-A). However, I must be cautious. Minni called my flop continuation bet, which suggests she has at least some connection to the board. If she has a 6 (middle pair on the flop), she now has a better two pair (6-6-3-3-J). If she has a pocket pair like 44 or 55, she also has a better two pair (e.g., 5-5-3-3-J). My improvement on the river only helps me beat her missed draws or lower high-card hands. Since she has checked to me, I will check back to realize my showdown value. There is no merit in betting here, as I likely won't get called by worse hands, and I don't want to be check-raised by a Jack or a better two pair.

Heads-up on the button with J♣8♥ — this is a playable, aggressive-opening hand. In heads-up play the button should open very wide; I have position and a deep stack edge, so I want to take the initiative and build the pot or pick up the blinds. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — big enough to get fold equity and to discourage very loose calls, but small relative to stacks so I can continue post‑flop without committing myself unnecessarily. If called I’ll continuation-bet on dry boards and play pot-control on coordinated/ace-heavy boards; I’ll fold to large, polarized 3-bets and re-evaluate small 3-bets depending on sizing and flop texture.

In heads-up play, K4o is a reasonable hand to defend from the big blind against a standard 3x open. Getting 2:1 odds on a call, I should see a flop and try to realize my equity. Minni has been very active from the button, and my King-high is ahead of a fair portion of their opening range.

I am out of position as the Big Blind in this heads-up pot. The flop of 9h-2h-Td does not connect with my K4o. While King-high has some showdown value, it is not strong enough to lead out into the pre-flop aggressor. The most standard play is to check and see how Minni proceeds. If Minni bets, I will evaluate based on the bet size, but for now, checking is the most disciplined move.

Heads-up I opened and Flash just checked a relatively dry flop (9♥ 2♥ T♦). I hold J♣8♥ — no pair but with backdoor hearts and two clean straight-completing ranks (Q or 7 = 8 clear outs) plus additional pair-up outs. Against a wide BB calling range many hands missed this board, so a continuation bet will pick up lots of folds and extract value from draws. I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (300) to apply pressure while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand. Plan: fold to a large check-raise, call small raises depending on sizing, and if called evaluate the turn (value-bet if I pair or make the straight, pot-control otherwise).

Minni's continuation bet on a 9-2-10 board with two hearts puts my King-high in a difficult spot. I have no draws and very little chance of improving to the best hand. Calling out of position against an aggressive opponent with just K-high is likely to lead to more difficult decisions on later streets. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

In a heads-up situation, King-Three offsuit is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I want to maintain aggression and take the initiative in the hand, especially since I am currently trailing in stack size. Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that puts pressure on the big blind while keeping the pot manageable if they decide to call or re-raise.

Flash (SB) opened to 300 into a 50/100 game — pot is 400 and I have to call 200 more (my total committed will be 300). Holding K♥8♦ heads-up, this is a defendable hand: it has decent showdown equity vs a button opening range and good postflop playability. Pot odds: I'm risking 200 to win 400 (33% break-even), and K8o clears that vs a typical button range, so a call is +EV. Stacks are deep (~263BB for me), so I can realize implied odds and maneuver postflop; 3‑betting OOP with K8o is thin and often creates awkward spots. I'll defend the big blind and call to see a flop.

I called preflop with K♥8♦ and completely missed this Ad‑Th‑7s rainbow flop. Flash (wide SB opener) checked first which shows weakness — good spot to apply pressure heads‑up. The board is dry with few draws, so a sizable bluff will fold out broadway junk and small pairs while still leaving me room to continue if called. I'll bet ~2/3 pot to maximize fold equity (400 into the 600 pot); if called I proceed cautiously OOP on the turn and re‑evaluate.

On this Ace-high board, Minni has led out with a donk bet after calling my pre-flop raise. King-high has very little equity here, and I have no draws. Given that the board favors an Ace-heavy range and Minni is showing initiative, there is no reason to put more chips into the pot with a hand that is likely crushed and has almost no chance of improvement.

Heads-up on the button with A♣Q♥ — a very strong hand in this spot. Pot is 150 and I only need 50 to call, but raising for value and initiative is standard heads-up: Flash's range is wide so I can get value from worse Axs, Kx, Qx and force many non-A hands to fold. I’ll raise to 300 total (≈3× the BB). That commits 250 more from me, makes the immediate pot 400 and would be 600 if he calls — good value given my stack (26,675) and the opponent’s stack (13,175). Size is large enough to get folds and extract value, but small relative to our stacks to avoid unnecessary bloating if he 3-bets. Plan: take the lead postflop if called; re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets big.

Q9o is a strong enough hand to defend the Big Blind against a button raise in heads-up play. I am getting 2:1 odds to see a flop, and while Minni has been aggressive, folding this hand pre-flop would be too tight.

On a dry, paired board like K-2-K, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading here wouldn't make much sense as Minni is likely to continuation-bet a large portion of her range anyway. By checking, I can see how she reacts and potentially reach a cheap showdown with my Queen-high, which still holds some value against her air.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor with A♣Q♥ and Flash just checked the K♦ 2♠ K♣ flop. My hand has good showdown value versus a wide checking range (many missed broadways, small pairs, and bluffs) and I block some A‑K/Ax combinations by holding an Ace. There are no meaningful draws to worry about, so a standard c-bet both extracts value from worse hands and gets folds from many non‑pairs. I’ll make a medium (~50–60% pot) continuation bet to apply pressure but keep the pot manageable: bet 350. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll fold or heavily re‑evaluate (my A♣Q♥ is too vulnerable vs strong aggression); if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and re‑assess based on the card and his action.

On this extremely dry, paired board (K-2-K), Minni's continuation bet is expected regardless of whether she hit the King. My Queen-high has some showdown value in heads-up play and beats all of her lower-card air. Given the pot odds of nearly 3.7:1, I will call the flop bet to see how the turn develops. If she checks the turn, I may be able to reach a showdown or take the pot away, while folding now would be too exploitable on such a board texture.

On the turn, the board pair of Kings remains the dominant feature, and the Jack of Clubs is a card that could potentially improve Minni's range (hands like AJ, QJ, or JT). As the out-of-position player who called a continuation bet on the flop, I should continue to play cautiously. Leading into Minni doesn't accomplish much here, as she will likely only call with hands that beat my Queen-high or fold hands she might have bluffed with. By checking, I allow her to continue her aggression with a wider range of hands or check back if she has given up on the pot. This keeps the pot manageable while I evaluate her reaction to the turn card.

I’m on the button with A♣Q♥. I was the preflop raiser and c-bet the K♦ 2♠ K♣ flop (350); Flash called. Turn J♣ checked to me. I only have Ace-high — I block some A‑K combos but I don’t beat Kx or a lot of pairs he would call the flop with. There are no meaningful draws to deny and betting here will mostly get called by better hands (Kx, pocket pairs) or run into a check‑raise trap. With the pot already 1,300 and deep stacks, I want to control the pot size and avoid bloating it with a marginal hand. I’ll check and see what he does on the river; call small/medium river bets for showdown if he barrels, but fold to large overbets or a check‑raise line that represents a very strong hand.

On the river, the board is Kd-2s-Kc-Jc-7s. I have Queen-high, which holds very little showdown value even in a heads-up pot. After Minni checked back the turn, it's possible she has a weak showdown hand like Ace-high or a small pocket pair that she didn't want to value bet on the turn. While I could attempt a bluff to push her off those hands, Minni has shown a willingness to call in previous hands, and my line of check-calling the flop doesn't strongly represent a King. By checking, I am prepared to fold to a bet, as Queen-high is unlikely to be the best hand if she puts more chips in, but checking allows me to reach a free showdown if she also checks back.

Check. I’m only showing Ace‑high on K♦ 2♠ K♣ J♣ 7♠. I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop; Flash’s call then two checks (turn & river) is exactly the line of Kx or a medium pocket pair that beats my hand. I block some A‑K combos but not kings, so a river bluff will rarely fold out the hands that currently beat me and will mostly get called. Pot control is correct here — no value hands to extract and no good bluffs to shove into a deep stack. I’ll check and take showdown rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a heads-up situation, A7o is a very strong starting hand and should be raised almost 100% of the time to put pressure on the big blind. My standard open raise has been to 300, which is what I will continue with here.

Heads-up in the big blind with Qc Jd — a very playable, often-favored hand against a wide SB open. Flash opened to 300; I want to seize initiative, apply pressure to a likely wide opening range, and avoid letting a cheap flop see with speculative hands. 3-betting to 900 (total) is a standard, value-oriented sizing here: it gives fold equity, isolates the opponent, and still leaves both stacks deep enough to play postflop. If Flash 4-bets large I will strongly consider folding (or re-evaluating based on sizing); if he calls I’ll take the lead with c-bets on favorable flops and play for value/protection on connected boards.

In a heads-up match, Ace-Seven offsuit (A7o) is a strong hand that should rarely be folded to a single 3-bet, especially when we have the benefit of being in position. Minni has been relatively active, and the 3-bet to 900 is a standard size. By calling, I can see a flop while maintaining the initiative of being the button player. 4-betting is an option but risks inflating the pot with a hand that has good showdown value but might be dominated if all the chips go in pre-flop. Calling allows me to use my positional advantage to outplay my opponent on later streets.

Check. I 3-bet preflop to take the initiative, but this flop (A♦ 9♣ 4♠) actually favors the caller’s range — lots of A‑x, some 9x/4x and pocket pairs. I’m out of position and holding Q♣J♦ with no pair and very little backdoor equity, so barreling here as a naked bluff is low‑EV: many hands that call a 3‑bet will continue, and I don’t want to build a big pot OOP with a marginal hand. Checking controls the pot, lets me see how Flash reacts, and preserves stack depth to maneuver on the turn. Plan: check‑call a small/standard c‑bet to realize equity against bluffs and weaker holdings, fold to very large turns bets/raises, and only start leading/raising if I improve (Q/J) or the board becomes much more favorable.

On this Ace-high dry board, I have flopped top pair with a seven kicker. After my opponent (Minni) 3-bet pre-flop and then checked this board, I have a few options. While I could bet for value against middle pairs or pocket pairs like KK-TT, checking back is a very strong play here. It allows me to control the size of the pot with a hand that is strong but vulnerable to better Aces. Checking also disguises the strength of my hand and might induce Minni to bluff on the turn with hands I currently dominate. I'm happy to take a free card and see how the turn develops.

I check. Holding Q♣J♦ on 4♠-9♣-A♦-T♠ gives me an open‑ended straight draw to any K or 8 (8 outs, ~31–32% to hit the river) but no backdoor flush and I’m out of position. Flash’s calling range vs my 3‑bet contains a lot of A‑x, 9x and medium pairs that will usually call or occasionally raise — so my fold equity betting OOP is limited. Both of us checked the flop and the pot is still only 1,800, so pot control and realizing my equity is the higher EV line: check to see the river, only call reasonable bets to realize the ~32% to hit, fold to large turn/river aggression, and if I hit a King or 8 on the river I’ll extract value.

On the turn, the board is 4s-9c-Ad-Ts. I have top pair with an Ace and a 7 kicker. My opponent, Minni, 3-bet pre-flop but has now checked twice on an Ace-high board. This suggests she either has a very strong hand she is trapping with, a marginal hand like a middle pair (9x, 10x) or a pocket pair, or a complete airball that she is giving up on. Since I checked back the flop to control the pot and disguise my hand strength, I should now bet for value. I want to get called by weaker Aces, 9s, 10s, or straight draws like QJ. A bet of 900 (half pot) is a standard value-sizing that allows me to build the pot while still being able to fold if she check-raises significantly.

Fold. Pot is 2,700 and Flash has bet 900 into the 1,800 pot (to call 900). That requires ~33.3% equity to justify a call. With Q♣J♦ on 4♠-9♣-A♦-T♠ I have 8 clean straight outs (K or 8) → 8/46 ≈ 17.4% to hit the river. I also have 6 pair outs (Qs or Jd) so the chance to improve to at least a pair/straight is 14/46 ≈ 30.4% — still below the 33.3% break‑even. Crucially, many of those pair outs (Q/J) will still lose to the opponent’s likely calling range (heavy A‑x, 9x and small pocket pairs after calling a 3‑bet and checking the flop). Raising is a possible semi‑bluff, but it risks getting called or 4‑bet by hands that dominate me and would bloat a pot OOP with marginal equity. With a big stack advantage heads‑up I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better, +EV spot. Fold.

I'm on the button (SB) heads-up with Ad Kc — the best non-pair hand. With deep stacks (26k vs 13k) and positional advantage postflop, I want to build the pot and apply pressure while denying cheap odds. A standard 3xBB sizing (300 total) is appropriate here — bigger than the min-raise so I don't invite too many cheap calls or exploitative 3-bets, but small enough to keep weaker hands and draws in. Plan: if called use position to c-bet favourable textures; if 3-bet, re-evaluate based on sizing and opponent tendencies (call or 4-bet pot-control vs shove).

I am in the Big Blind with 8s4h. Minni, who has a larger stack, has raised to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. In heads-up play, 8-4 offsuit is quite weak and does not play well against a standard opening range. Given the tournament status (hand 125 of 150) and the fact that I am trailing in chips, I should look for better spots to commit chips rather than defending with a sub-optimal hand out of position.

I am on the button with K6o, which is a marginal but playable hand in a heads-up situation. The pot is currently 150 and it is 50 for me to call. Given the position advantage and the fact that I want to keep the pot controlled with a medium-strength hand, a standard raise to 300 is a good way to take the initiative and potentially take down the pot pre-flop or set up a post-flop play.

Heads-up vs the button’s open to 300: J♣2♣ is weak but acceptable to defend from the big blind. I’ll flat-call rather than 3‑bet — I’m out of position, the stacks are deep so I want to see a flop and realize equity with a suited jack, backdoor flush/straight potential, and occasional two pair. A 3‑bet to 500 would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and commit me out of position. Postflop plan: continue aggressively if I hit a jack, two, or strong draw; otherwise exercise pot control and fold to large continued pressure.

I missed the flop — J♣2♣ has no pair on K♦7♥4♠, only backdoor club/runner-runner equity. I’m out of position in a heads‑up pot and the stacks are deep, so I want to control the pot and realize equity rather than bloat the pot with a marginal hand. Flash opened wide preflop and will c‑bet a lot, so checking lets me see his action cheaply. Plan: check and call small continuation bets to try to hit a jack, deuce or backdoor club; fold to large, committed pressure unless I improve. If Flash checks back I can take a free turn or look for a cheap bluff on a favorable card (club or low card).

I have flopped top pair with a King on a dry board of Kd-7h-4s. My opponent, Minni, has checked to me. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, a continuation bet is standard here to extract value from weaker pairs (like a 7 or pocket pairs) or from ace-high hands that might call one street. Given the board is dry, a bet of 300 (half the pot) is a balanced sizing that allows me to build the pot while keeping her range wide.

I’ll call 300. I’m out of position with J♣2♣ on K♦7♥4♠ — no pair, only backdoor club/runner-runner equity. My preflop plan was to flat and check-call small flop continuation bets to realize equity and keep the pot manageable, and Flash’s wide, aggressive opener will c‑bet this board a lot. Calling 300 is a small price with deep stacks: it gives proper pot odds to see a turn, preserves flexibility, and lets me continue only if I improve (pair a J/2 or pick up a club) or if I get a favorable, cheap turn card to bluff. If Flash ramps up the pressure with a large raise/overbet I’ll fold — I don’t want to bloat the pot with this marginal hand. Re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed aggressively only when I’ve improved or the board/frequencies indicate a profitable bluff.

Heads‑up with J♣2♣ on K♦7♥4♠6♠. I missed — no pair, only backdoor clubs — but Flash c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, which is weakness. This 6 is the “low” turn I said I’d use for a cheap bluff. With pot = 1200 and deep stacks I want to seize the initiative without overcommitting: make a small probe to fold out missed Kx/7x/4x and marginal floats while keeping the price reasonable if I’m called. I’ll bet ~1/3 pot (400). If he raises I’ll fold and preserve stack; if called I can bluff or improve on the river (prefer a club or a jack to continue).

I have improved to two pair (Kings and Sixes) on the turn. My opponent's lead of 400 into a 1200 pot (a 'donk bet') often signifies a marginal hand like a King with a weak kicker, a draw, or a blocking bet. Given the strength of my hand, I should raise for value and to protect against potential draws (spades or straight draws like 58). Raising to 1200 allows me to build the pot and charge her for any draws or weaker Kings that she is unlikely to fold.

I opened the turn as a small probe (400) with Jc2c after checking the flop to fold out missed floats. Flash responded by raising to 1200. The pot is 2800 and it costs me 800 to call (making the pot 3600) — I need ~22% equity to call. With only three remaining Jacks and three remaining Deuces I have 6 outs (6/46 ≈ 13% equity), and the backdoor club flush is already impossible. Flash’s line (preflop open, flop c-bet, then a strong turn raise) is weighted toward value (Kx, 7x, 4x, sets, two pair) or a difficult-to-exploit bluff; calling is -EV and raising as a bluff has poor fold equity and bad blockers. Per my plan to preserve stack and fold to significant turn aggression with a marginal hand, I muck.

Heads-up on the button with 7♠2♥ — a very weak hand, but being on the button (positional advantage) and the pot only requires a 50 call. Standard heads-up strategy is to open-raise very wide to steal the blind and build fold equity. A small, 2BB open (raise to 200 total) keeps the raise size minimal relative to our deep stack while applying pressure to Flash in the big blind. Plan: raise to 200 to pick up the pot often; if called I’ll use position to c‑bet favorable flops and fold to heavy resistance unless I improve. If Flash 3-bets big, I’ll re-evaluate and usually fold this holding.

I have 6s 5s, which is a very solid suited connector in a heads-up situation. Minni has made a min-raise to 200, which gives me excellent pot odds (3:1) to see a flop. Since I am in the big blind, I only need to commit 100 more chips to call. This hand has good post-flop playability and equity, so calling is the most standard and effective play here.

On this flop of 4c-Tc-5h, I have middle pair with 5s 6s. Since Minni checked to me, checking back is a solid play to control the pot size and realize my hand's equity. Middle pair is often ahead in a heads-up pot, but it is vulnerable and doesn't warrant building a large pot yet. Checking allows me to see the turn for free and evaluate Minni's next move.

I opened very wide from the button with 7♠2♥ preflop and Flash just called in the big blind and checked the flop. The board 4♣ T♣ 5♥ is relatively dry and disconnected from my actual holding, so heads‑up this is a high-frequency continuation‑bet spot to leverage position and fold equity. A half‑pot c‑bet (200 into the 400 pot) will force many of Flash’s missed hands (A‑highs, KQ type hands, small pairs and weaker draws) to fold while keeping the pot manageable if called. If Flash raises or applies significant pressure I’ll fold—I have almost no showdown equity—whereas if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and give up unless I pick up real equity or further weakness appears.

I have middle pair (5s) on a 10-5-4 board. In heads-up play, middle pair is a solid hand that should not be folded to a single half-pot bet. I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call. I will call to see the turn and evaluate further, keeping the pot at a manageable size while realizing the equity of my pair and backdoor straight draws.

On the turn, the King of Diamonds is a card that likely favors Minni's range as the pre-flop aggressor. I currently hold third pair (fives) with a six kicker. This hand has some showdown value but is not strong enough to lead out with a bet into the person who has shown aggression. By checking, I am looking to keep the pot manageable and potentially see a river card for free or evaluate if Minni decides to bet. Betting here would likely only result in being called by better hands (Tens, Kings, or better) while folding out hands I already beat.

Flop c-bet worked but I have essentially zero equity with 7♠2♥. The turn K♦ is a very dangerous card — it helps any Kx, many Ax, tens, and a lot of hands that called the flop — and it also doesn’t improve my hand or give me a draw. Pot is already 800 and I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a pure bluff that is likely to be called or check-raised. Best play is to pot‑control: check and give up to any meaningful bet. If Flash checks back I keep the pot small and can re-evaluate the river; if he bets I’ll fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

On the river, the board pairs the King, giving me two pair: Kings and Fives with a Six kicker. Minni's check on the turn suggests she likely does not have a King, as a pre-flop aggressor would typically bet a King for value or protection on that texture. My hand has some showdown value against her missed draws or Ace-high hands. Betting here is unlikely to get a better hand like a Ten or a higher pocket pair to fold, and it's unlikely a worse hand will call. Therefore, checking to see a cheap showdown is the most logical play.

I have essentially no showdown value with 7♠2♥. I opened wide and c‑bet the flop but the turn K♦ changed the texture — the paired king on the river (K♥) greatly reduces my bluff equity and makes any small river bet much less credible. Flash checked the river as well; that line can represent weakness but also a slow‑played K/Tx or a hand that will call a small bet. Pot is already 800 and stacks are deep, so risking a large chunk of my stack on a low‑EV bluff is unnecessary. Best play is to check and fold to any bet, preserve chips, and wait for a better spot where my range and fold equity are stronger.

I am on the button with 5-3 offsuit. While heads-up play often requires playing a wide range from the button, 5-3 offsuit is extremely weak. Minni has shown significant aggression throughout the tournament, and with only 22 hands remaining, I prefer to fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to defend or attack rather than entering a pot with a bottom-tier hand and potentially facing a raise from the big blind.

Heads-up on the button with A♥ J♠ — a strong, highly playable hand with position postflop. Opponent (Flash) is the big blind with a deep stack (14,675). I want to take the initiative, extract value from worse Ax/Kx/Jx hands, and apply fold equity against weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. A standard 3xBB open to 300 (total) accomplishes that: it’s a clear raise size at this table pattern and keeps postflop options open. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and his tendencies (call small-medium 3-bets to play in position, consider folding or a polarized 4-bet vs very large/obvious ranges). For now: raise to 300.

In a heads-up situation, 87o is a standard hand to defend from the big blind against a button raise. I am getting 2:1 odds to call (200 to win 400), and the hand has decent playability on many board textures. I will call to see a flop and proceed from there based on the board development and Minni's post-flop aggression.

I have no piece of this board (9h-9s-As) with 8-high. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, as it includes an Ace and a pair of nines. I have no draws and very little showdown value. My plan is to check and likely fold to any significant aggression from Minni.

Flop: 9h 9s As — I have top pair (A♥ J♠) with position and the opponent checked. This is a textbook spot to bet for value and protection. Flash’s calling preflop range will include worse A♦x hands, some broadways, and small pairs — many of which will call a half‑pot. Betting also charges any backdoor draws and prevents giving a free card that could pair the board to give trips or two pair to a caller. I’ll size modest (≈1/2 pot) to get value from worse Aces and medium hands while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. If I get a raise I’ll assume strong or polarized holdings (9x/trips/quads or a big slow‑play) and re‑evaluate (likely exercise caution). Plan: bet 300 to extract now and control future streets.

I have 8-high with no draws on a board of 9h-9s-As. This texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, who likely has many Aces and Nines in her range. With no equity and no potential to improve to a winning hand, there is no reason to call even a half-pot bet. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with Queen-Ten offsuit, which is a very strong hand for this position. My stack is around 144 big blinds, while Minni has over 250 big blinds. I will open with a standard 3x raise to 300 total chips to build the pot and take control of the hand, which has been my consistent sizing for opening raises throughout the tournament.

Heads-up facing a standard open to 300 from the button. I have pocket deuces (2s2h) and stacks are deep (effective ≈ Flash’s 14,175 ≈ 140+ BB), so set-mining is the highest EV line. The pot is 400 and I must call 200, giving me ~25% immediate pot odds — well within reason against a wide opening range. A tiny 3-bet to 500 is too small to fold out hands and too committal with only a small pair; a large 3-bet would risk lots of chips without tangible equity. I’ll call to see a flop; if I hit a set I’ll build the pot, otherwise I’ll cede control / fold to heavy aggression and keep pot-sized decisions manageable.

I called preflop to set-mine and missed — I have pocket 2s on a paired 7-7-3 board (no set). Opponent checked to me, which can be weakness but also could be a slow-played strong hand (77/33/overpairs). I’m out of position and stacks are deep, and I only have 2 outs to make a set (~8–9% to hit by river), so committing chips here without improvement is low EV. Betting would rarely fold out the hands that beat me and would bloated the pot vs hands I can’t beat. I’ll check to control the pot, see how Flash reacts on the turn, call small bets to realize equity, fold to large pressure, and if I hit a deuce I’ll extract value.

On this paired board of 7s-7c-3c, I have two overcards (Queen and Ten) and the pre-flop initiative. Minni has checked, which is expected. A continuation bet here is a standard play to represent a pair or a strong hand, as it is difficult for the Big Blind to have connected with this board unless they have a 7, which is statistically unlikely. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to take the pot down now or deny equity to random high-card hands that Minni might be holding.

I called preflop to set-mine and missed the flop — I have an underpair (22) to a paired 7-7-3 board and I’m out of position. Flash’s continuation bet is small (~300 into a 900 pot, roughly 1/3 pot), which is consistent with a wide c-bet range. Raising without a set would be overly committal and bluff-heavy; folding now throws away the chance to realize equity vs many overcards and bluffs. I’ll take the low-variance line: call 300 to see a turn, keep the pot controllable, fold to heavy turn aggression, call smaller turn bets to realize equity, and extract value if I hit my deuce.

Check. I have an underpair (22) on a paired 7-7-3-4 board and I’m out of position. Flash’s small flop raise is consistent with a wide range, but I have very little improvement equity (only a deuce helps). With deep stacks and a 1,200 pot, I want to keep the pot controllable, realize equity, and avoid bloating the pot with a bluff. Checking preserves the option to call a reasonable river bet or fold to heavy aggression and lets me extract value if I hit a set on the river.

I have Queen high on a paired board of 7-7-3-4. After my flop continuation bet was called, Minni has checked the turn. While I could continue to represent a strong hand, Minni has shown a tendency to be sticky with calls in previous hands. Checking back here allows me to control the pot size, realize my equity, and potentially win at showdown if the river goes check-check or I improve to a pair. It also avoids a potential check-raise on a board where it is difficult to have a very strong range.

Board: 7s 7c 3c 4h 3s — a two-pair board with both sevens and threes on it. I called to set-mine preflop and never improved: pocket 2s are now just a pair of twos and lose to any 7 or 3 (and to any hand that connected better). Flash’s line (preflop open, small flop bet I called, checks turn and river) can be either missed overcards or a slow-trapped 7/3/full house, so I have very limited fold equity and virtually no value hands that will call a river bet. With deep stacks a bluff here is high-risk (can be raised or called by many better hands) and committing chips with an underpair is poor EV. I’ll take the low-variance, high-information line: check to showdown and avoid bloating the pot; if Flash bets I’ll fold to meaningful river aggression.

On this double-paired board (7s over 3s), I have Queen-high. After betting the flop and checking the turn, checking back the river is the most prudent play. My Queen-high has significant showdown value in a heads-up match, especially after the turn went check-check. Minni's range likely consists of missed draws or Ace-high hands; while a bet might occasionally force a fold from a better Ace-high, it is more likely to be called by any pair or better, making a check the best way to realize my equity and reach showdown.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Js7d. In heads-up play the button should open a very wide range — J7o is a playable hand and raising is preferable to simply calling or folding. Raising to the 2bb minimum (200 total) achieves three things: (1) takes the initiative and builds fold equity vs the big blind, (2) keeps the pot small with a marginal hand while preserving deep-stack postflop maneuverability, and (3) balances my range so I’m not always checked/called into tough spots. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing and reads; if he calls I’ll c-bet appropriate flops and use position to pressure. I’ve already posted 50, so raising to 200 means committing 150 more this street.

In a heads-up scenario, I am facing a min-raise from the button while holding 83 suited in the Big Blind. The pot odds are 3:1, requiring approximately 25% equity to make a call profitable. Given the playability of suited connectors/gappers in position or when defending the blind against a wide button opening range, and the low cost to see a flop, calling is a standard defensive play here to avoid being over-exploited by the opponent's frequent opening.

I have a flush draw on a board with trip Aces (paired Aces). Since Minni was the pre-flop raiser, they have a significant range advantage on an Ace-high board. I will check to the aggressor to see how they react. If they bet, I have sufficient equity with my flush draw to call and see a turn card.

I’m the preflop aggressor with a very wide heads-up raising range; Flash’s cold-call from the big blind contains a lot of hands that completely missed this flop. The board Ad 9d Ah is extremely Ace-heavy and my Js7d has no pair and only a weak backdoor diamond — essentially no showdown value. As PFR I should c‑bet frequently to take down pots and represent an Ace, but keep the size modest to control the pot with deep stacks. Betting ~½ pot (200 into 400) applies pressure and folds out many non‑Ax holdings; if Flash raises big I’ll give credit and fold, if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and barrel only on favourable cards (diamond or pairing my J/7) or check back on blanks.

On a board of Ad-9d-Ah, I have an 8-high flush draw. Minni, who was the pre-flop aggressor, has made a half-pot bet of 200. Although the board is paired, which reduces the absolute strength of a flush due to the possibility of a full house, the pot odds offered (3:1) make calling with a flush draw a mathematically sound decision. Raising here would be unwise as Minni is very likely to have an Ace or a 9 in their range, which they would not fold. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see a turn card cheaply.

On the turn, the 4 of diamonds completes my flush (8-high). The board is paired with Aces (Ad and Ah), which means any opponent with an Ace now has trips, and any Ace-9 or Ace-4 combination (though Ace-4 is less likely given the 4 of diamonds is on the board) would have a full house. Additionally, any 99 or 44 would be a full house. While I have a strong hand, the paired board and the low rank of my flush (8-high) suggest some caution is needed. Checking here allows me to see how Minni reacts to the flush-completing card. If Minni has an Ace, she is likely to bet for value, and if she has a higher flush draw that just hit, she will also bet. Checking protects me against being check-raised by a superior hand while keeping the pot manageable if I am already beaten by a boat.

I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop (Ad 9d Ah) which I expected to fold out a lot of non‑Ax hands. Turn is 4d: I have Js7d so I don’t have a pair but I now have a 4‑card diamond (one card to make a flush). Flash checked the turn — many calling hands (overcards, small pairs, single‑diamond floats, 9x) will fold to continued pressure, while the hands that beat me (an Ace or two diamonds = a made flush) are less likely to fold. Betting keeps the initiative and charges draws while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll bet a half‑pot probe (400 into 800) to represent strength (Ax/trips) and to fold out weaker holdings; if I face a large raise I will concede the pot (fold), if I’m called I’ll re‑evaluate the river and only continue with a diamond, a pair, or a clear read.

On the turn, the 4 of diamonds completes my flush, though it is only 8-high. The board is paired with Aces, which means any hand containing an Ace has trip Aces, and hands like A9, A4, 99, or 44 have made full houses. Minni's lead of 400 into 800 (half pot) is a standard value bet. While my hand is strong in a heads-up context, it is vulnerable to higher flushes and all full houses. Raising here would likely only get called by hands that beat me. Calling allows me to realize my equity and see a river card while keeping the pot size controlled in case I am already beat by a boat or a superior diamond. The pot odds (3:1) are more than sufficient to continue with a flush.

The river card is the Nine of Hearts, which pairs the board for a second time (Aces and Nines are both paired). While I have an 8-high flush, this board is now extremely coordinated. Any Ace in Minni's range (which is very possible given her pre-flop raise and aggression on every street) now has a Full House (Aces full of Nines). Furthermore, any Nine in her range has now made a Full House (Nines full of Aces). Even if she does not have a Full House, any diamond higher than an 8 (Td, Jd, Qd, Kd) would beat my flush. Given Minni has led the betting on the flop and turn, I must respect the possibility that she has a dominant hand. I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown, but I must be cautious about calling a large bet.

I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the flop and turn. The river (9h) pairs the board and completes no diamond for me — I missed the flush and didn’t pair my J7. The final board Ad 9d Ah 4d 9h gives full houses to any opponent holding an Ace or a 9, and Flash’s passive call‑call line makes those combos quite plausible. My best hand is just the board’s two pair with a Jack kicker; that kicker only fares well vs hands that missed, but a river bet here risks getting called or check‑raised by hands that now crush me. I planned on the turn to only barrel the river with a diamond, a pair, or a clear read — none of those appeared. Opt to check and take showdown for free rather than inflate the pot when I’m likely behind to A/9 combos.

In heads-up play, the button has a significant advantage and should play a wide range of hands. However, Queen-Deuce offsuit is one of the weaker hands in the range. While a raise is possible to steal the blinds, Minni has been defending and playing aggressively throughout the tournament. With only 18 hands remaining and a deficit of 8,000 chips, I prefer to fold these very weak starting hands and look for better opportunities to take a stand or value bet when my range is stronger.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 4♦5♣. In heads-up play the button should open a very wide range — 4‑5 offsuit is playable because of position and postflop playability. Stacks are deep (~241bb vs 157bb), so I want to seize the initiative without bloating the pot. I’ll raise to the minimum open of 200 (2×BB): it applies pressure to the big blind, builds a pot when I hit paired/connected flops, and preserves flexibility postflop. Plan: continuation bet frequently on dry, A/K/Q high flops; slow-play / check and pot-control or fold to heavy aggression on highly coordinated boards. If Flash 3‑bets small I can call and play postflop for equity; fold to a large 3‑bet/shove. Overall raising to 200 maximizes my fold equity and positional advantage while keeping risk controlled.

I am in the Big Blind with Ks 7c. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button. I have to call 100 more to see a flop. King-high with a decent kicker in a heads-up situation is well ahead of a wide button opening range. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the strength of my hand relative to a typical opening range, a call is mandatory here. I am out of position, but I have a playable hand and sufficient stack depth.

On a board of Ah-Kc-6h, I have second pair (Kings). This board hits the pre-flop raiser's range very hard, as Minni likely has many Aces and broadway cards in her button-opening range. I will check to the aggressor to see how she proceeds. My hand is too strong to fold to a single bet but not strong enough to lead out into an Ace-high board.

I opened a very wide button range preflop with 4♦5♣, and this flop (Ah 6h Kc) is exactly the type of A/K/Q-high board I planned to continuation‑bet. Flash checked, so as the preflop aggressor I should seize the initiative and try to fold out missed holdings and weak Kx/broadways. I’ll make a standard ~1/2‑pot c‑bet (200 chips) — large enough to credibly represent an Ace and pick off many non‑made hands, but not so large that I overcommit with almost no equity. Plan if action continues: fold to a strong check‑raise or big turn shove (my hand has negligible equity), and if called I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check‑control and fold to significant pressure unless I improve).

I have second pair (Kings) on a board that contains an Ace. In a heads-up situation, second pair is quite strong, although the Ace on the board is definitely in Minni's range as the pre-flop aggressor. However, I am getting 4:1 pot odds to call her 200 bet into a 600 pot. Folding a King here would be too tight given the price and the nature of heads-up play. I will call to see how the turn develops and potentially reach a showdown.

On the turn, the board is Ah-6h-Kc-5s. I have second pair (Kings) with a 7 kicker. This is a classic marginal made hand in heads-up play. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor here doesn't accomplish much, as better hands like Aces will call or raise, and I'd prefer to keep her bluffs in. By checking, I allow Minni to continue her aggression with a wide range of hands or check behind with hands that have some showdown value but are weaker than an Ace. I will check and evaluate based on her action and bet sizing.

I opened a very wide button range and c‑bet the A‑high flop; the turn 5s pairs my 5 and converts a missed-bluff into a modest value hand. This is a weak second/bottom pair on a very wet/high board (Ah Kc 6h 5s) — I’m still crushed by any Ax or Kx and vulnerable to sets. With heads‑up ranges wide, though, Flash will still have many worse hands that called the flop (small pocket pairs, Qx/Jx/T9, some Kx) that will call a small turn bet. I want to extract thin value without bloating the pot or inviting big action from stronger hands, so I’ll make a small value bet (~1/3 pot). Size = 300 chips: big enough to get called by worse hands and fold pure air, but small enough to fold to a check‑raise or shove. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll concede (fold) — if he just calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously.

I have second pair (Kings) on an Ace-high board. In heads-up play, second pair is a strong hand, but it is vulnerable to any Ace in Minni's range. Minni has bet on both the flop and the turn, but her turn bet of 300 into a pot of 800 is relatively small, giving me excellent pot odds of nearly 4:1. Given the strength of my hand relative to a wide heads-up button range and the favorable price, I cannot fold. Raising would likely only be called by better hands (Aces), so calling is the most profitable play to keep her range wide and reach a showdown.

On the river, the board is Ah-6h-Kc-5s-Qh. I have second pair (Kings) with a 7 kicker. This hand has significant showdown value in a heads-up game, but it is not strong enough to bet for value, as I am unlikely to be called by many worse hands on this board. The river also completed a potential heart flush and made any Queen a pair (though my Kings still beat a single pair of Queens). By checking, I can reach a showdown against her weaker hands or potentially call a bet if I suspect a bluff, depending on the size. Betting would likely only result in being called by an Ace, two pair, or a flush.

I only have bottom pair (5s) after turning my 5. The river Qh is a dangerous card — it brings another overcard and a third heart, opening up flushes and giving many hands (Ax, Kx, Qx, 6x, sets/full houses) that already called my flop and turn a clear beat on showdown. Flash has passively called my small flop and turn bets, which narrows his range toward hands that dominate my pair. Betting here for thin value is unprofitable (few worse hands will call; better hands will call or raise) and I have virtually no credible bluffs or heart blockers to represent the nut. I’ll check to avoid bloating the pot and see a showdown; if Flash bets a meaningful amount I can fold rather than commit with such a weak holding.

Holding an Ace heads-up from the button (Small Blind) is a strong starting hand. I will raise to 300 to take control of the betting lead and build the pot, as Ace-high hands have significant equity in heads-up play.

Heads-up in the big blind with 9h10h (suited connectors) facing a button open to 300. Flash on the button will open very wide heads-up, so 9h10h has decent equity and excellent playability postflop (nut-flush and straight possibilities). Pot is 400 and I only need to put in 200 more to call (calling makes the pot 600) — ~3:1 immediate pot odds and deep stacks for implied odds. I’m out of position postflop, so 3-betting is a viable line to seize initiative, but it bloats the pot OOP with a hand that plays well multiway postflop. I prefer to call here to realize equity, control pot size, and evaluate the flop: continue aggressively on favorable flops (heart/connected/pair) and fold or proceed cautiously to strong action on dry boards. If Flash 3-bets large preflop I’ll fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing and reads. Calling keeps maximum EV with manageable variance.

Heads-up out of position on 5d 9d 3c with 9h10h — I have top pair with a decent kicker. Flash opened wide from the button, so his range has many worse pairs, floats and diamond draws. I want to extract value from worse hands and deny equity to diamond draws, so I’ll make a firm value/protection c-bet. A ~2/3 pot c-bet (400 into the 600 pot) charges draws and gets value from pockets/single-pair hands while leaving room to fold or call a large raise and reevaluate on the turn.

I have Ace-high with a backdoor straight draw and a backdoor flush draw on a 5-9-3 board. Minni's lead of 400 into a 600 pot (a donk bet) often represents a marginal hand, a draw, or a probe to see where my pre-flop raising range stands. Folding here would be too tight given the strength of an Ace heads-up and my position. Raising would bloat the pot with a hand that currently has only showdown value or thin equity. Calling is the best option to see a turn, maintain pot control, and evaluate Minni's aggression on later streets.

I turned trips (9♥10♥ on 5♦ 9♦ 3♣ 9♣). Villain (Flash) opened wide pre and called my sizable flop action, so his continuing range is a lot of one-pair hands, weak/medium pocket pairs and floats — many of which will happily call a turn bet. With the nuts-like hand on the turn I want to extract value while denying a free river card that could change the showdown dynamics (the lone remaining 9 or any unlikely runner-runner). A firm value bet of ~2/3–3/4 pot will get called by worse pairs, weaker 9x combos with inferior kickers and any stubborn pockets, while still leaving room to fold/reevaluate if he check-raises big. I size to 1000 into the 1400 pot to maximize thin value and put pressure on pure floats/bluffs.

On a board of 5-9-3-9, I am holding Ace-high. Minni has taken a very aggressive line by donking (leading) into the pre-flop raiser on the flop and then firing a significant second barrel on the turn after the board paired the nine. While Ace-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, Minni's sizing (1000 into 1400) and consistency across two streets suggest she likely has a made hand, such as a nine, a pair of fives, or a pocket pair. Even if she is semi-bluffing with a diamond draw, I am in a difficult spot to call down with only Ace-high and no significant draw of my own. Preserving my stack for better spots in these final hands of the tournament is the more prudent strategy.

Heads-up on the button with pocket deuces (2♦2♠). Stacks are very deep (~241 BB) and the cost to see the flop is only 50, but in heads-up I prefer to open-raise to take the initiative, isolate the BB and build value (and fold equity) when I hit a set. A 3x sizing is a solid, standard heads-up open: large enough to discourage limp and define ranges, while leaving plenty of room postflop with deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold), but versus a call I’ll play the flop in position. Raise to 300 total.

I am currently in the Big Blind with 5d 6s, which is a weak starting hand. Minni has been playing aggressively from the Button throughout the match. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds to call, playing a weak, non-connected, offsuit hand out of position against an aggressive opponent often leads to difficult post-flop situations where I'll be forced to fold to continuation bets. With only 15 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to protect my stack and look for better opportunities to close the gap on Minni's lead rather than bleeding chips on marginal hands.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with King-Eight of clubs, which is a strong starting hand. A standard opening raise of 3x the big blind (300 total) is appropriate here to exert pressure and take control of the hand, which has been my consistent strategy with playable hands.

Facing a button open to 300 with 8♦6♠ in the big blind. Heads-up this is a defendable, marginal hand — it has decent equity vs a wide opening range and reasonable postflop playability. I’m getting good pot odds to call (200 more into a ~400 pot) and stacks are deep so implied odds exist. I won’t 3‑bet OOP with 8‑6o (poor hand to build a big pot out of position); better to call, see a flop, and play straightforwardly: continue on favorable flops (pair, two‑pair/straights/draws), otherwise pot‑control or fold to heavy aggression. Call 200 more (total committed 300).

Pot is 600, I hold 8♦6♠ on 3♣ 9♥ 9♣ — no pair, no meaningful draw. Preflop I defended as planned but this flop is unfavorable (paired board strongly favors the raiser’s range and gives me almost no equity). Heads‑up Flash C‑bets very frequently, so my best line is pot control: check and concede the pot to aggression rather than bloating the pot out of position with a weak bluff. If Flash checks back I get a free turn to reassess; if he bets I’ll fold to a standard c‑bet (only consider calling an extremely tiny probe). Preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

On a board of 3c 9h 9c, I have a flush draw with Kc 8c along with overcards. Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. A standard continuation bet of 300 (half pot) is effective here to capitalize on the folded equity while building the pot for my significant draw equity. If I am called, I still have many ways to improve on the turn.

I defended preflop with 8♦6♠ as a marginal heads‑up call, but this flop is very unfavorable. On 3♣9♥9♣ I have no pair and no meaningful backdoors; the preflop raiser’s range (any 9, 3, overcards, broadway pairs/one‑pair hands) is heavily favoured and he c‑bets frequently. Calling 300 into a 900 pot requires ~25% equity; my actual equity here is well below that and a call just leaves me out of position facing turn aggression and awkward folds. Raising as a bluff is thin with no equity or backdoors. Preserve the stack and fold this marginal spot.

Heads-up on the button with 9♣5♥ (SB). In two-handed play this is a limp-or-raise spot where raising is preferred: we have position postflop and can open a very wide range to apply pressure. The pot is 150 and it's 50 to call — a min-raise to 200 (total committed) grabs the initiative, creates fold equity, and lets us play the bigger portion of the range in position. With deep effective stacks we can realize equity or c-bet favorable flops; if Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this weak offsuit hand, but vs a call we can continue aggressively. Action: open-raise to the min (200) to maximize pressure and exploit heads-up dynamics.

In heads-up play, 4d 7d is a reasonable hand to defend the Big Blind with, especially when facing a minimum raise. The pot odds are 3:1, making a call profitable in the long run given the suited connectivity potential of the hand. I will call to see the flop.

I have completely missed this flop with 7-high and no significant draws. The board contains both an Ace and a Queen, which hits a pre-flop raiser's range very hard. As the first to act on this street, I will check and see how Minni responds, likely folding to any bet.

Flop As Qc 5s — I hold 9c5h, only a pair of fives. Heads‑up I opened preflop and have position, Flash checked which often shows weakness or pot control. This is not a hand I want to slowplay: betting gives me fold equity against overcards and unpaired hands (Kx, Jx, random floats) and lets me define my opponent’s range. I’ll fire a standard ~50% pot c‑bet (200) — large enough to take the pot down or get value from worse hands while small enough to fold to a sizable check‑raise. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he check‑raises big I’ll relinquish the pot with this vulnerable pair.
