I have 7-high on an Ace-Queen-Five rainbow board. This flop hits Minni's pre-flop raising range extremely well, as it contains both high cards. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Minni's continuation bet of 200 into a 400 pot is standard, but since I have absolutely no equity and very little room to bluff on this texture, I must fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding pocket Jacks (Js Jd) on the button. This is a premium hand in heads-up play. My goal is to build a pot for value while also providing some protection against potential overcards on the flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 has been consistent with my previous play and is the correct strategic move here.

Facing a standard 3bb open to 300 from Flash (SB). I’m in the big blind with T5o — not a strong hand, but heads-up the opener’s range is wide and I’m getting good price. Pot is 400 and the call is 200 (calling 200 to win a 600 pot => ~3:1, so I need ~25% equity). With both stacks very deep, I prefer to see a flop and use postflop play rather than 3-bet bluff out of position with a weak holding. I will defend and evaluate on the flop — continue with any pair, strong draw, or favorable texture; fold to heavy aggression without improvement. Action: call the 200 (total committed = 300).

Board is 8♦ 6♦ 6♥ and I hold T♠5♥ — I completely missed (no pair, no diamond). I’m out of position vs the preflop raiser, and this paired, two-diamond board hits many parts of his opening range (6x, 8x, diamonds), so my fold equity OOP is limited. With deep stacks I don’t want to build a pot OOP with pure air; better to control the pot and re-evaluate. Plan: check and fold to large/committed bets, consider floating only to small bets with a clear turn-bluff plan if a safe (non-diamond, non-pairing) card comes or if I pick up a pair. If villain checks back I keep the pot small and look for better spots later.

I have an overpair to the board (Jacks) on a paired board texture (8-6-6). Minni checked to me as the pre-flop aggressor. This is a clear value betting situation. I want to get paid by 8x hands, smaller pocket pairs, and various draws (diamond flushes or straight draws like 97 or 75). I also need to protect my hand against overcards like Queens, Kings, and Aces that could fall on the turn. I will bet 350 into the 600 pot.

I checked the flop OOP with T♠5♥ and the board is 8♦ 6♦ 6♥. Flash (the SB/raiser) has bet 350 into a 600-pot (now 950) — I must call 350 to continue, which requires ~27% equity. I have no pair and no diamond draw (only three tens left as outs). My chance to hit a ten by the river is roughly 12.5%, and even if I pair a ten I’ll often be second-best against hands that connect with this texture (6x, 8x, diamond draws, overpairs). I have essentially no fold equity OOP and don’t want to bloat a deep-stack pot with pure air. Given the poor equity vs a reasonable c-bet range and the unfavorable board texture, folding is the +EV play — preserve my stack and wait for a better spot in position.

Heads-up on the button (also the small blind) with J♥2♥. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but in a heads‑up match I should open a wide range from the button to apply pressure and take advantage of position postflop. J2s is a playable heads‑up hand (suit + blockers to hearts) so I’ll open‑raise to a standard 3x BB sizing to 300 total. This sizing gives good fold equity against the BB, looks like a normal opening range, and keeps the initiative. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet without a stronger hand, but if called use position to c‑bet dry flops, realize equity on flush/straight turns, and control the pot on coordinated boards.

I have been dealt pocket Kings, which is an exceptionally strong hand in heads-up play. Minni raised from the button to 300. Since I am out of position for the remainder of the hand, I want to build the pot immediately and maximize value. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard size that allows me to either take the pot down now or set up a favorable stack-to-pot ratio for post-flop play. If Minni continues to 4-bet, I am more than happy to commit my entire stack.

I opened to 300 from the button with J♥2♥ and planned to fold to a large 3‑bet. Flash 3‑bet to 1,000 which is a sizeable reraise and signifies a fairly value‑heavy (and some polarized) range. Calling 700 into a 1,300 pot means I need ≈35% equity to break even; J2♥ versus a typical 3‑bet range has substantially less equity and is often dominated. 4‑betting as a bluff isn’t attractive (weak blockers, deep stacks, and little fold equity against a competent opponent). I’ll fold and preserve my stack for better, more profitable spots.

I have King-Queen suited on the button in a heads-up match. This is a premium hand and should always be played aggressively. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take advantage of my positional advantage throughout the hand.

Heads-up vs the button — Flash opened to 300 (a standard 3x). I’m on the big blind with 9♥T♣, deep stacks (≈235 BB). This is a playable hand heads-up: decent connectivity and reasonable equity versus a wide opening range. Calling costs me 200 more to reach a total commitment of 300, giving roughly ~33% pot odds — 9T has sufficient equity vs a wide button raising range. I’d rather keep the pot manageable and see a flop out of position than iso-3bet OOP with an offsuit, marginal hand. I’ll call and evaluate on the flop; a small flat also preserves my ability to realize equity and apply postflop aggression when appropriate.

Flop is 2c-4d-3h — a very dry, low board that misses most of the button’s opening range. I hold T9o with no pair and only a virtually nonexistent backdoor straight (would need both a 5 and a 6). Out of position and deep-stacked, I don’t want to inflate the pot with such a marginal hand or lead on a board that favors the opener’s c-bet frequency. Best line is to check and control the pot: I can call small-to-medium continuation bets to realize equity and fold to large, polarized pressure. If Flash checks back I’ll take a free turn card and plan a delayed bluff or small stab depending on the turn; if he barrels small I’ll often call; if he jams or overbets massively I’ll fold. Action: check to keep options and avoid getting outplayed OOP.

On a low, connected board of 2-4-3, I have two overcards and a backdoor heart flush draw with Kh Qh. Although this board texture can favor the Big Blind's calling range, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should maintain initiative with a continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half pot) is large enough to fold out completely air hands while keeping the pot under control if Minni has a piece of the board or a straight draw.

Heads-up against the button on a very dry 2c-4d-3h flop — Flash opened wide preflop and is c-betting a small 300. I have T9o with no pair or real draw but deep stacks, so I want to keep pots manageable and not fold away equity vs a wide c-betting range. Calling 300 into the ~900 pot gives roughly 25% break-even equity, and keeping his bluffs in is profitable; raising OOP with no pair and no fold equity is too risky. Plan: call, see the turn and re-evaluate — fold to a large polarized overbet, call small barrels, and start applying pressure or value bet if I improve (pair or a clean runner-runner).

Check. I have T9o on 2c-4d-3h-2s with no pair and no real draw. Flash opened wide and c-bet the flop; when he checks the paired turn it often indicates he’s checking a range containing air, medium cards, and some slowplayed pairs — but it also removes a lot of fold equity for a bluff. OOP and deep-stacked, my priority is pot control: I don’t want to inflate a marginal holding into a big pot or get check-raised off. I’ll check to see the river and then decide — call small river bets for pot odds/frequency, fold to large overbets, or seize a cheap bluff opportunity if the river is favourable.

On the turn, the board pairs with a deuce (2c-4d-3h-2s). Minni has checked again after calling my flop continuation bet. With King-high, I have some showdown value against her draws (like 56 or 5x) but I'm likely behind any pair or an Ace. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, see a free river card, and potentially realize my equity if I hit a King or Queen. There is little value in betting here as it is unlikely to fold out better hands like a small pair.

I missed — T♣9♥ has no pair or meaningful showdown value on 2c-4d-3h-2s-7c. Flash opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop then checked the turn, which often indicates a wide, medium-strength or bluffing range that checked down. Checking behind surrenders the pot to any river aggression and gives up a free chance to fold out many missed hands (A/K/x, small unpaired hands) that will check back the turn but fold to credible river pressure. I want a size that credibly represents a made hand (2s/3s/4s trips/full house or a turned pair) and generates fold equity, but isn’t too large relative to deep stacks. A ~2/3-pot river stab (800 into 1200) should fold many of Flash’s missed overcards and marginals; if he raises I’ll concede the bluff (fold) because I have no showdown value. Betting 800 is the best exploitative line here.

On a board of 2c-4d-3h-2s-7c, I am holding Kh-Qh for King-high. The action went: I raised pre-flop, c-bet the flop, and checked the turn after Minni called my flop bet. On the river, the 7c doesn't change the board much, but Minni leads for 800 into a 1200 pot (a 66% pot bet). My King-high is only beating pure bluffs like 65 or 86. Any pocket pair, any piece of the board (4, 3, 7, or 2), and any Ace-high hand (which is a significant part of the Big Blind's calling range) beats me. Since I don't even have Ace-high to catch potential bluffs, calling here would be a pure hero call. Given the tournament stage and the pot odds, I don't believe my King-high is good 28% of the time against her leading range.

Heads‑up on the button with positional advantage postflop. 5♣2♦ is a “trash” hand and has very little showdown value, but in a two‑player game the correct overall strategy is to be aggressive and open a very wide range to seize the initiative and collect the blind. Calling 50 to just see a flop is cheap but gives Flash free odds to play position; a raise buys fold equity and keeps me in control. I’ll open‑raise to 300 total (3×BB) — small enough to keep the pot manageable with these deep stacks but large enough to force folds from very weak big‑blind hands. If Flash 3‑bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll play the flop cautiously, c‑bet selectively on favorable textures and fold to strong resistance.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-King suited, a premium powerhouse hand in heads-up play. Minni raised to 300 from the small blind/button. Given the strength of my hand and the need to build the pot while out of position, I will 3-bet to 1000 to extract value and take control of the hand.

I opened wide to 300 to seize the initiative heads‑up, but facing Flash’s 3‑bet to 1,000 this hand is not worth continuing. 5♥2♣ has almost no showdown equity vs a typical 3‑bet range and is frequently dominated. Calling 700 into the ~1,300 pot requires roughly mid‑20s% equity to be +EV; 5‑2o is well below that against a reasonable 3‑bet range. A 4‑bet bluff would be committing too much with almost no fold equity and is out of line here. I’ll fold, preserve my deep stack and wait for a much better spot to apply pressure.

In heads-up play, the Button (Small Blind) should open a wide range of hands to capitalize on position post-flop. T6o is a marginal hand but generally falls within the top 60-70% of hands, making it a standard opening candidate. I have been raising to 300 consistently with non-premium hands and will maintain that sizing to attempt to take the blinds or play a pot in position. If Minni 3-bets, I will likely fold given the weakness of my hand.

Facing a standard heads-up open to 300 from the button while I’m in the big blind with Jh4s. The hand is unsuited, poorly connected and frequently dominated by a button’s opening range; I’d need roughly 33% equity to justify calling (200 into a 400 pot gives 200/(400+200)=33.3%). I’d also be out of position postflop against an opponent who raises and c-bets a lot, so implied odds and playability are poor. Raising as a bluff is possible but risky with deep stacks and little fold equity from this holding. With a large stack and plenty of hands left, the disciplined play is to fold and wait for a much better spot.

I’m on the button (also the small blind) with 8♠2♣ — a very weak hand, but this is heads-up and I have a big positional advantage. The pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete; standard heads-up strategy is to open-raise a very wide range to seize initiative and deny the BB good odds. I’ll raise to 300 total (a standard open-size at this table) to apply pressure. If Flash folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position and c-bet selectively; if he 3-bets I’ll fold this junk hand. Raising here maximizes long-term EV by exploiting heads-up dynamics while preserving a clear fold-to-3bet plan.

I am holding Ace-Jack offsuit in the Big Blind, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has been opening frequently from the button. Given my hand strength and the tournament stage, I want to put pressure on Minni and build the pot. A 3-bet here is the standard aggressive play to either take the pot down pre-flop or gain initiative for post-flop play.

I opened to 300 on the button with 8♠2♣ and Flash responded with a clean 3‑bet to 1,000 (pot ~1,300). 8‑2 offsuit has almost no equity and plays terribly postflop vs a 3‑bet range — it’s easily dominated and offers very poor turn/river playability. Calling 700 to see a flop in position is a low‑EV call here, and a tiny 4‑bet bluff to 1,700 is unnecessary risk against a deep, likely calling/4‑betting opponent. Stick to the preflop plan: fold and preserve the stack for better spots in position.

I am holding Ace-King offsuit, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. Being on the button, I want to seize the initiative and build a pot. A standard 3x big blind raise to 300 is effective here to either take down the blinds or set up a post-flop situation where I have both card strength and positional advantage. My recent aggressive 3-bets have caused Minni to fold, so maintaining this pressure is consistent with my current strategy.

I’m in the big blind with 8♥4♥ facing a raise to 300 from the button. Pot is 400 and I must call 200, so my break-even equity is 200/(400+200)=33.3%. 8♠4♠ suited is a weak, low, unconnected hand that will be frequently dominated by the button’s opening range and I’ll be out of position postflop. 3-betting as a light bluff here is too thin versus an aggressive opener and calling OOP with this holding gives poor implied odds. Preserve stack and fold.

Heads-up on the button with A♦K♣ — a premium hand. Calling 50 is too passive: I want to seize the initiative, build a pot with position and deny the BB cheap equity. A standard 3xBB open to 300 is size-consistent with my earlier opens, extracts value from worse A/K/x and medium pairs, and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. If Flash 3-bets I will re-evaluate (call or 4-bet depending on sizing), but preflop the correct play is an aggressive open.

I am in the Big Blind holding 10-2 suited. Minni, who has been quite aggressive with button raises, has raised to 300. While I am getting 3:1 odds to call, this hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. With only six hands remaining in the tournament and being currently out-chipped, I want to preserve my stack for a more advantageous spot rather than potentially bleeding chips with a marginal hand on a difficult board.

Holding K5o on the button in a heads-up scenario, I have a standard opening hand. Minni has been folding frequently to my 3x pre-flop raises in the last few hands (Hands 139, 141, 143, 144). I will continue this aggressive strategy by raising to 300 to try and win the pot immediately or play post-flop in position.

Heads-up in the big blind against a 300 open from the button. Pot is 400 and it costs me 200 more to call (total 300). Q♣2♠ is a weak hand but has some showdown value and blocks some Qx combos; against a wide button opening range I want to defend rather than give up a big portion of my stack. A min-raise to 500 would be a light 3-bet with a very poor kicker and would commit more chips out of position with little fold equity. Calling keeps the pot small, lets me realize equity cheaply, and lets me proceed only when I hit a pair or a meaningful draw — otherwise I can fold to heavy action on the flop. Therefore I will call the 200 (total 300).

I flopped a small pair (2♠) with a Q kicker on a dry 6♠-7♦-2♣ board. Heads-up against a wide button opening range this is a hand with reasonable showdown value but it's vulnerable to 6x/7x and bigger pairs — and I'm out of position. I want pot control and to let the button c-bet so I can call bluffs and fold to clear strength rather than build the pot myself with a thin value hand. Checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me evaluate turn action (call small/medium bets, fold to large pressure unless improved). Therefore I will check.

I raised pre-flop and the flop 6-7-2 is very dry. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a standard continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to capitalize on my range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor and attempt to win the pot immediately.

Call 300. Heads-up on a very dry 6♠–7♦–2♣ flop I have a small pair (2♠) with a Q♣ kicker. Flash’s 300 into the current pot gives me roughly 25% pot odds (call 300 to win ~1200), and versus a wide button c-betting range my hand has reasonable showdown value and enough equity to continue. Raising would bloat the pot out of position with a vulnerable hand and little fold equity; folding surrenders too much equity for the price. I’ll call to keep the pot controlled, realize equity, and reassess on the turn — folding to very large pressure unless I improve to two pair/trips or top pair with a Q, and calling small/medium turn bets to reach showdown.

I have 2♠ Q♣ on 6♠ 7♦ 2♣ 6♣ — that’s two pair (sixes and twos) but the hand is very vulnerable: any 6 (trips), any 7 (better two pair) or a 2 with a bigger kicker can beat me. Flash checked to me on the turn after c-betting the flop, which is a mixed signal (could be weakness, pot-control with a medium pair, or a trap with a monster). There are no meaningful draws to charge, and betting here would bloat the pot out of position and risk a check-raise that I don’t want to face with a second-pair two pair. Pot-control and showdown value are my priorities: I’ll check and see what Flash does on the river, prepared to call small/medium bets but fold to clear large aggression.

On a paired board of 6s-7d-2c-6c, I am holding King-high. I was the pre-flop aggressor and fired a continuation bet on the flop, which Minni called. Her call on the flop suggests she has a piece of the board, likely a 7, a 2, or a mid-pocket pair. Now that the turn has paired the 6, and she has checked again, I have a choice between betting again to push her off a marginal hand or checking behind to see a free river. Since a second barrel is unlikely to fold out a 7 and I still have some showdown value with King-high against her draws (like 89 or 54), I will check behind to control the pot size and potentially improve on the river or reach a cheap showdown.

I hold 2♠ Q♣ on 6♠ 7♦ 2♣ 6♣ 6♦ — that’s a full house (sixes full of twos). It’s a very strong but vulnerable hand: any 6 in Flash’s hand is now quads and any 7 gives a better full house. Flash’s line (button open, c-bet flop, check-turn) is wide and includes many hands I beat (Kx/Ax/Qx) but also the critical combos that beat me (6x or 7x) and the tying combos (2x). Betting here would bloat the pot and mainly get called by the small subset of hands that beat me or fold out weaker hands I want value from. Pot control and locking up showdown value is preferable — I’ll check and go to showdown (and call a normal river bet; only fold to an extremely polarized large overbet that very strongly represents quads).

The board has run out 6s-7d-2c-6c-6d, putting three of a kind (sixes) on the table. My best five-card hand is now Three of a Kind, Sixes, with a King and a Seven as my kickers (6-6-6-K-7). In this situation, the winner is determined by who holds the highest kicker in their hole cards (unless someone has a pocket pair that makes a full house). My King-high is the second-best possible kicker after an Ace. Minni has checked to me on the river. Since I have significant showdown value with the King kicker and it's unlikely she will call a bet with a worse hand or fold a better one (like an Ace), checking behind is the most sensible play to realize my equity and win the pot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Q♦4♥. In heads-up play position is huge and even marginal hands have value for opening. I’ll take the initiative and raise to a standard 3xBB (300) to get fold equity and charge the big blind’s wide defending range while keeping the pot size reasonable. If Flash 3-bets heavily I’ll fold Q4o; if he calls I’ll use position to continue aggressively on favorable flops or pot-control on bad ones. Preserves my chip lead while applying pressure with a straightforward, low-variance raise size.

With only four hands remaining in the tournament, I am currently behind Minni by approximately 8,000 chips. While the pot odds to call the raise are 2:1, 7-5 offsuit is a very weak hand to play out of position against a chip leader who has shown consistent aggression. At this stage, preserving my stack for a better opportunity to double up or take a significant pot in the final three hands is more strategic than defending a marginal hand that will likely result in a loss on most flops.

I am on the button with 4d 3c, which is a very weak hand. We are late in the tournament (Hand 148 of 150) and Minni has a significant chip lead. While the cost to call the big blind is only 50 chips, this hand performs poorly post-flop and doesn't warrant an investment against a chip leader who has been playing aggressively. Folding is the most prudent way to preserve my stack for the final few hands.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with AhTs is a strong, aggressive spot. I want the initiative and to isolate the big blind — this hand plays well multi-way but best in heads-up pots where I have position postflop. A min-raise to 200 is too weak and gives the BB good odds to call wide; simply calling 50 is passive and sacrifices fold equity. A standard 3x BB sizing (300 total) applies pressure, wins the blinds often, and still keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks. If called I retain position to realize equity and extract value; if Flash folds I pick up the pot uncontested.

With only two hands left in the tournament and holding 5-2 offsuit in the big blind, I have no reason to defend against a standard open from Minni. This hand is significantly below the average range required to call a raise, especially when the goal is to protect my remaining stack and look for a better spot in the final hand. I will fold and conserve my 15,675 chips.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I am currently behind Minni by 8,600 chips. To have any chance of overtaking her, I need to play aggressively and build a significant pot. Queen-Trey suited is a strong enough hand to raise from the Button in a heads-up situation. I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and build the pot early.

Situation: heads-up vs Flash who opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the big blind with JdQs, stack 24,225. Pot is ~400 and I need 200 to call; min-raise is to 500. Analysis: JQo is a strong heads-up hand and fares well against a wide button opening range. Folding is overly tight; calling concedes initiative and leaves me out of position postflop. Action: I want to apply pressure and either take the pot now or play a defined hand postflop, so I’ll 3-bet to the minimum. Plan: raise to 500 (total committed preflop) to get value and fold equity vs a wide range. If Flash 4-bets big I’ll respect that and fold (QJo performs poorly vs a polarized 4-bet range); if he calls I’ll play the flop straightforwardly (c-bet favorable boards, control pot size on scary runouts).

In the final hand of the tournament, I am trailing Minni by a significant margin (8,450 chips). To have any chance of overtaking her or maximizing my final stack, I need to play for a large pot. Minni's 3-bet to 500 is very small, offering me excellent pot odds to call with a suited Queen. I will call the 3-bet to see a flop in position, which allows me to keep her range wide and gives me the opportunity to potentially hit a strong hand or draw that I can then use to commit my entire stack and seek a double-up.

I was the preflop 3-bettor to 500 and Flash just called. Flop 6d-9c-2c is very dry and favorable for a c-bet: few strong hands connect and my QJo has two overcards and decent equity vs his wide calling range. As the aggressor I want to continue applying pressure to fold small pairs and get value from worse broadways/Ax. I’ll make a standard ~60% pot c-bet (600 into a 1000 pot). If he check-raises I’ll give credit and fold (he’ll typically show sets/strong draws); if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously, barreling only on safe cards and pot-controlling vs resistance.

As this is the final hand of the tournament and I am trailing Minni by over 8,000 chips, my priority is to maximize my stack and potentially find a way to double up. My hand, Queen-Trey of diamonds, gives me an overcard and a backdoor diamond flush draw on a board of Six-Nine-Deuce. Minni's bet of 600 into a 1,000 pot provides me with the pot odds to call and see a turn card. Calling allows me to keep my stack relatively healthy while maintaining the potential to hit a pair or a strong draw on the turn, which would be necessary to commit more chips and try to close the gap in the final standings.

I was the preflop 3-bettor and c-bet the dry 6♦-9♣-2♣ flop; Flash called. The turn T♣ brings a third club and materially changes the board — many of Flash’s flop-calling hands (Ax clubs, Kx clubs, suited clubs, some pocket pairs that flat-called) now have a completed flush or significantly improved equity. My J♦Q♠ has two overcards and ~8 theoretical outs to a straight (K’s and 8’s), but I have no club and several of those outs are “dirty” (club outs that could give him the nut-flush). This is not a safe card to barrel on. I’ll exercise pot control and check to avoid getting raised or called by made clubs/strong pairs; if Flash bets a small size (roughly ≤½ pot) I’ll consider calling to realize equity, but against a large turn bet I’ll fold. If he checks back I get a free river to realize equity or take a controlled shot there.

Minni checked the turn after showing aggression on the pre-flop and flop. My hand, Queen-Trey of diamonds, currently has no pair and very little showdown value on a board with three clubs. While I am significantly behind in the tournament standings, the goal is to maximize my stack. Betting here as a bluff is risky because Minni's check could be a trap with a flush or a strong pair, or she might be calling down with an Ace-high. By checking back, I take a free card to the river, hoping to hit a Queen or simply reach showdown in a pot that I am unlikely to win with a bet against a player who has shown consistent strength throughout the hand.

The river Ac completes a very dangerous board: 6♦-9♣-2♣-T♣-A♣. I hold J♦Q♠ with no club and no pair — essentially QJ high. After my preflop 3-bet and flop c-bet, Flash’s flop call includes a lot of single-club combos and medium pairs that now either made a flush on the river (single-club hands) or have at least a pair (A×, T×, 9×, pocket pairs). Betting here would be a pure bluff that’s getting called by a large portion of his range and folded by only the absolute air; EV of a river bluff is very poor. Checking preserves stack, gets to showdown, and avoids losing more to the many hands that improved. I’ll check and go to showdown; if Flash now leads out with a sizable bet I’ll fold given my lack of equity.

This is the final hand of the tournament. The board has four clubs, including the Ace of clubs, making any club a flush. Minni has checked both the turn and the river after showing aggression on the pre-flop and flop. While I have no showdown value with Queen-high, the risk of bluffing into a potential club in Minni's hand outweighs the potential gain, especially since winning this pot wouldn't be enough to overtake her for first place. I will check to see the showdown and conclude the tournament.
